| C5 | Conv (FC) | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ | $5 \times 5 \times 16, K = 120$ | 1 | 0 | $1 \times 1 \times 120$ | **48,120** | $\tanh$ |
| F6 | FC | $120$ | — | — | — | $84$ | **10,164** | $\tanh$ |
| Out | FC | $84$ | — | — | — | $10$ | **850** | Softmax |
| **Total** | | | | | | | **61,750** | |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** What is LeNet-5? State the input size, output size, and the total number of trainable parameters in the original LeNet-5 architecture. *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
LeNet-5 is a pioneering 7-layer Convolutional Neural Network proposed by **Yann LeCun et al. in 1998** for handwritten digit recognition. It accepts a grayscale input image of size **$32 \times 32 \times 1$** and produces a probability distribution over **10 output classes** (digits 0 to 9). The architecture consists of two convolutional layers (C1, C3), two average-pooling layers (S2, S4), a fully connected convolutional layer (C5), and two fully connected layers (F6, Output). The total number of trainable parameters is **$\approx 61{,}750$**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between the pooling operation used in the original LeNet-5 and the pooling operation commonly used in modern CNNs. Why was average pooling originally chosen? *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The original LeNet-5 uses **average pooling** (subsampling) with a $2 \times 2$ kernel and stride 2, where the output of each pooling region is the arithmetic mean of its inputs. In contrast, modern CNNs (e.g., AlexNet, VGG, ResNet) predominantly use **max pooling**, which outputs the maximum value in each region. Average pooling was originally chosen because (i) it provides a smoother, less aggressive downsampling, preserving the average activation intensity — useful for the low-contrast digit images of the 1990s, and (ii) it has a probabilistic interpretation (it acts like a low-pass filter on the feature map). Max pooling, although not used in LeNet, was later found to be more effective at preserving the strongest detected features and became standard in modern architectures.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Draw the complete block architecture of LeNet-5. For each layer, state the input dimension, filter size, output dimension, number of parameters, and activation function used. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** Compute the total number of trainable parameters in LeNet-5. Justify why most of the parameters are concentrated in the later layers despite the earlier layers having larger feature maps. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Architecture — Tabular Description:**

| Layer | Type | Input | Filter | Output | Params | Activation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| C1 | Convolution | $32 \times 32 \times 1$ | $5 \times 5, K = 6$ | $28 \times 28 \times 6$ | 156 | $\tanh$ |
| S2 | Avg-Pool | $28 \times 28 \times 6$ | $2 \times 2, S = 2$ | $14 \times 14 \times 6$ | 12 | $\tanh$ |
| C3 | Convolution | $14 \times 14 \times 6$ | $5 \times 5, K = 16$ | $10 \times 10 \times 16$ | 2,416 | $\tanh$ |
| S4 | Avg-Pool | $10 \times 10 \times 16$ | $2 \times 2, S = 2$ | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ | 32 | $\tanh$ |
| C5 | Conv (FC) | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ | $5 \times 5, K = 120$ | $1 \times 1 \times 120$ | 48,120 | $\tanh$ |
| F6 | FC | 120 | — | 84 | 10,164 | $\tanh$ |
| Out | FC | 84 | — | 10 | 850 | Softmax |

A block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) should accompany the answer.

**(b) Total Parameters and Justification:**

Using $\text{Params} = (F \times F \times D_{\text{in}} + 1) \times K$ for each conv layer and $(N_{\text{in}} + 1) \times N_{\text{out}}$ for FC layers:

$$\text{Total} = 156 + 12 + 2{,}416 + 32 + 48{,}120 + 10{,}164 + 850 = 61{,}750$$

**Justification:** Although the early layers (C1, S2, C3, S4) have larger spatial feature maps, their **channel depth is very small (6, then 16)**, so the filter tensors are small. In contrast, **C5 uses $5 \times 5 \times 16$ filters multiplied by 120 channels**, and **F6 has $120 \times 84$ weights** — these deep, narrow layers produce massive weight matrices. Hence, $\approx 78\%$ of the parameters are concentrated in the last three layers, even though they handle compact representations.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** Explain the convolution and average-pooling operations as used in LeNet-5. For an input of size $32 \times 32 \times 1$ and a $5 \times 5$ filter with stride 1 and no padding, compute the output feature map size. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the role of activation functions in LeNet-5. Why was $\tanh$ originally used, and what modern alternatives are used? Justify with a comparison table. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Convolution and Pooling Operations:**

**Convolution** slides a $5 \times 5$ kernel over the input image, computing element-wise multiplication followed by summation at every position, and adds a bias term. The output is a **feature map** that highlights where the input pattern (defined by the kernel) occurs.

**Average Pooling** divides the feature map into non-overlapping $2 \times 2$ regions and replaces each region with the arithmetic mean of its four values. This **downsamples** the feature map by a factor of 2, reducing computational cost and providing mild translational invariance.

**Output Size Calculation (C1):**

$$H_{\text{out}} = \frac{32 - 5 + 0}{1} + 1 = 28$$

$$W_{\text{out}} = \frac{32 - 5 + 0}{1} + 1 = 28$$

$$\text{Output Feature Map} = 28 \times 28 \quad (\text{per filter})$$

**(b) Activation Functions in LeNet-5:**

| Property | $\tanh$ (Original) | ReLU (Modern) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Formula** | $\tanh(x) = \dfrac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$ | $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ |
| **Range** | $\left[-1, +1\right]$ | $\left[0, +\infty\right)$ |
| **Gradient Behaviour** | Vanishing gradient for large $\vert x \vert$ | Constant gradient = 1 for $x > 0$ |
| **Computational Cost** | Expensive (exponentials) | Cheap (max operation) |
| **Suitability in LeNet** | Original 1998 choice | Used in modern re-implementations |
| **Output Layer** | RBF in 1998, Softmax in modern variants | Softmax remains standard |

**Justification:** In 1998, the $\tanh$ activation was preferred because it is zero-centred, which helps gradient-based optimisation converge faster than sigmoid. However, $\tanh$ still suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** for large $\vert x \vert$. Modern re-implementations of LeNet use **ReLU**, which mitigates vanishing gradients and is computationally cheaper, leading to faster training convergence.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in the KTU Valuation Hall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term:** When computing parameters for a conv layer, students often write $(F \times F \times D_{\text{in}}) \times K$ instead of $(F \times F \times D_{\text{in}} + \mathbf{1}) \times K$. The "+1" accounts for the bias per filter. **This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if omitted.**
> 2. **Confusing padding and stride in the output formula:** The correct formula is $H_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{in}} - F + 2P}{S} + 1$, NOT $\dfrac{H_{\text{in}} - F}{S} + 1$ unless $P = 0$.
> 3. **Mixing up parameter count with connection count:** The original LeNet paper reports both. **Parameters = weights + biases (what is stored)**; **Connections = parameters × spatial output size (number of multiply-accumulate operations in a forward pass).**
> 4. **Forgetting the floor function:** If $(H_{\text{in}} - F + 2P)$ is not divisible by $S$, the output is $\left\lfloor \cdot \right\rfloor + 1$, not a decimal. Always apply the floor.
> 5. **Saying "LeNet has 5 layers" (which is technically true for the 5 learnable stages) but "LeNet-5 has 7 layers" (counting each conv and pool separately).** Read the question carefully!

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before the exam):**
>
> - **Year:** LeNet-5 was proposed in **1998** by **Yann LeCun** and collaborators.
> - **Task:** Handwritten digit recognition (10 classes).
> - **Input:** Grayscale image of size **$32 \times 32 \times 1$**.
> - **Architecture Pattern:** Two blocks of **(Conv → Pool)** followed by **three Fully Connected layers**.
> - **Layer-wise Output Dimensions:** $32 \to 28 \to 14 \to 10 \to 5 \to 1 \to 84 \to 10$.
> - **Filter Sizes:** Always **$5 \times 5$** for convolutions; **$2 \times 2$** for pooling.
> - **Strides:** Conv = **1**, Pool = **2**.
> - **Padding:** Always **0 (valid convolution)** in the original LeNet-5.
> - **Pooling Type:** **Average Pooling** (not max pooling).
> - **Activation:** **$\tanh$** for hidden layers; **Softmax** (or RBF originally) for output.
> - **Total Parameters:** **61,750** (memorise this number — it is a frequent 2-mark direct question).
> - **Parameter Concentration:** **C5 layer alone holds $\approx 78\%$ of the parameters**.
> - **Historical Achievement:** Deployed in **banking cheque-reading systems** in the late 1990s; one of the first successful commercial CNN applications.
> - **Significance in Syllabus:** LeNet is the **canonical example of an end-to-end trainable CNN** and the direct ancestor of AlexNet, VGG, and ResNet.
> - **Forward Pass Master Formula (memorise):** $H_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{H_{\text{in}} - F + 2P}{S} + 1$.
> - **Parameter Master Formula (memorise):** $\text{Params} = (F \times F \times D_{\text{in}} + 1) \times K$.
> - **Receptive Field Awareness:** The deeper the layer, the larger the receptive field — that is, deeper neurons "see" more of the original image.
> - **Modernisation Trend:** Re-implementations of LeNet use **ReLU** instead of $\tanh$ and **max pooling** instead of average pooling, achieving faster convergence and higher test accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AlexNet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. AlexNet — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AlexNet** is a pioneering deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture introduced by **Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey E. Hinton** in their landmark **2012 NIPS paper** titled *"ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks."* It won the **ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC-2012)** by achieving a **top-5 error rate of 15.3%**, compared to **26.2%** by the next best competitor (a non-deep-learning entry). It is the architecture that effectively launched the modern deep-learning revolution in computer vision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> AlexNet is an 8-layered CNN consisting of **5 Convolutional Layers** and **3 Fully Connected (FC) Layers**. It introduced several architectural innovations — **ReLU activation, Dropout regularization, overlapping max-pooling, Local Response Normalization (LRN), and GPU-based parallel training** — that together made deep CNNs practical and dominant for large-scale image classification.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Visual Brain in 8 Layers"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: Reading a Street Sign from Far Away**
> 
> Imagine you are trying to recognize a faraway street sign. Your eyes first catch **broad shapes and colors** (low-level features: edges, gradients). As your gaze moves deeper, your brain starts identifying **patterns** (mid-level: corners, textures, strokes). Finally, the highest brain region combines these into **the full concept** ("Stop Sign").
> 
> AlexNet works the same way:
> - **Layers 1–2** = "Edge and color detectors" (eyes / retina).
> - **Layers 3–4** = "Texture and part detectors" (visual cortex V1/V2).
> - **Layer 5** = "Object-part detectors" (higher visual cortex).
> - **FC Layers 6–8** = "Decision-making" (the brain's interpretation center).

## 1.3 Architectural Overview at a Glance

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Total trainable parameters | **≈ 60 million** |
| Convolutional layers | **5** |
| Fully Connected layers | **3** |
| Input image size | **227 × 227 × 3** (RGB) |
| Output classes | **1000** (ImageNet classes) |
| Activation function | **ReLU** (after every conv & FC layer) |
| Regularization | **Dropout (p = 0.5)** in FC layers |
| Training hardware | **2 × NVIDIA GTX 580 GPUs** (≈ 1.5 GB VRAM each) |
| Final classifier | **Softmax (1000-way)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Architecture Breakdown

AlexNet is **split across 2 GPUs** because a single GTX 580 could not hold the entire network. The "GPU 1 / GPU 2" split is therefore an *engineering reality*, not a conceptual design choice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick (exam-ready):** 
> Conv layers see *where* something is. FC layers see *what* it is. 
> "Where shrinks, What thinks."

### Layer-wise Specification

| # | Layer | Filter / Kernel | Stride | Output Size (per GPU) | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **C1** | Conv-1 + ReLU + LRN + MaxPool | **11 × 11 × 3**, stride 4 | 4 | 55 × 55 × 48 | 11·11·3·48 = **17,424** |
| **C2** | Conv-2 + ReLU + LRN + MaxPool | **5 × 5 × 48**, pad 2 | 1 | 27 × 27 × 128 | 5·5·48·128 = **153,600** |
| **C3** | Conv-3 + ReLU | **3 × 3 × 256**, pad 1 | 1 | 13 × 13 × 192 | 3·3·256·192 = **442,368** |
| **C4** | Conv-4 + ReLU | **3 × 3 × 192**, pad 1 | 1 | 13 × 13 × 192 | 3·3·192·192 = **331,776** |
| **C5** | Conv-5 + ReLU + MaxPool | **3 × 3 × 192**, pad 1 | 1 | 13 × 13 × 128 | 3·3·192·128 = **221,184** |
| **F6** | FC-1 + ReLU + Dropout | — | — | 4096 | 6·6·256·4096 = **37,748,736** |
| **F7** | FC-2 + ReLU + Dropout | — | — | 4096 | 4096·4096 = **16,777,216** |
| **F8** | FC-3 + Softmax | — | — | 1000 | 4096·1000 = **4,096,000** |

## 2.2 The Six Key Innovations of AlexNet

1. **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit):** Solves the vanishing-gradient problem of sigmoid/tanh, enabling training of deep networks ~6× faster.
2. **Dropout (p = 0.5):** Randomly deactivates 50% of neurons in FC layers to prevent overfitting.
3. **Local Response Normalization (LRN):** A biologically inspired lateral inhibition that normalizes activations across neighboring feature maps.
4. **Overlapping Max Pooling:** Pooling with stride 2 over 3×3 windows (instead of 2×2 non-overlapping), reducing top-1 and top-5 error by ~0.4% and ~0.3% respectively.
5. **Data Augmentation:** Image translations, horizontal reflections, and **PCA-based color jittering** to artificially inflate the training set.
6. **GPU Parallelization:** Splitting the network across two GPUs using a clever cross-GPU connection pattern in C3, C4, C5.

## 2.3 Core Formulas — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE. Memorize them.**

### (a) ReLU Activation
$$
f(x) = \max(0, x)
$$

Derivative:
$$
f'(x) = 
\begin{cases} 
1, & x > 0 \\ 
0, & x \le 0 
\end{cases}
$$

### (b) Softmax (Output Layer for 1000 Classes)
$$
P(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{1000} e^{z_j}}
$$

### (c) Local Response Normalization (LRN)
$$
b_{x,y}^{i} = \frac{a_{x,y}^{i}}{\left( k + \alpha \sum_{j=\max(0,\, i-n/2)}^{\min(N-1,\, i+n/2)} (a_{x,y}^{j})^{2} \right)^{\beta}}
$$

**Paper hyperparameters:** $k = 2$, $\alpha = 10^{-4}$, $\beta = 0.75$, $n = 5$.

### (d) Cross-Entropy Loss (used in AlexNet)
$$
L = -\sum_{i=1}^{1000} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)
$$

### (e) Dropout (Inference vs. Training)
$$
\hat{y}_{\text{train}} = \frac{1}{p}\, y \cdot \text{Mask}, \quad \hat{y}_{\text{test}} = y
$$

### (f) Output Size of a Convolution Layer
$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

where $W$ = input size, $K$ = kernel size, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride.

### (g) Output Size of a Pooling Layer
$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{W - K_{p}}{S_{p}} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

## 2.4 Parameter Count Formula (General Conv Layer)
$$
\text{Params}_{\text{conv}} = (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}}
$$

The "+1" accounts for the bias term per output filter.

## 2.5 Why AlexNet Mattered (Engineering Utility)

| Domain | Impact |
|---|---|
| Image classification | **State-of-the-art on ImageNet 2012** |
| Transfer learning | Pre-trained AlexNet became the **default backbone** for detection/segmentation tasks |
| GPU computing in DL | Proved that **consumer GPUs could train CNNs** — birth of GPU-DL ecosystem |
| Activation functions | ReLU replaced sigmoid/tanh as the **de-facto standard** |
| Regularization | Made **Dropout mainstream** in deep networks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Conv1 Output Spatial Dimensions

We compute the output of **Conv-1** explicitly using the formula derived in Section 2.

### Given
- Input image: $W = 227$
- Kernel size: $K = 11$
- Padding: $P = 0$ (no padding for Conv-1)
- Stride: $S = 4$

### Step-by-step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{1} &= \left\lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
      &= \left\lfloor \frac{227 - 11 + 2(0)}{4} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
      &= \left\lfloor \frac{216}{4} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
      &= \lfloor 54 \rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
      &= 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The output feature map of Conv-1 has spatial size **55 × 55** (per GPU: 55 × 55 × 48). ✔ Matches the original paper.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Parameter Count for Conv-2 (per GPU)

### Given
- Input feature maps (per GPU): **27 × 27 × 48**
- Kernel size: **5 × 5**
- Output filters (per GPU): **128**
- Bias included.

### Step-by-step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Params}_{\text{conv2}} &= (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}} \\[6pt]
                            &= (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 48 + 1) \cdot 128 \\[6pt]
                            &= (1200 + 1) \cdot 128 \\[6pt]
                            &= 1201 \cdot 128 \\[6pt]
                            &= 153{,}728
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note (without bias):** $5 \cdot 5 \cdot 48 \cdot 128 = 153{,}600$. The paper convention **excludes** bias from the count to give **153,600**, the rounded number cited.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Output Dimension After Conv-2 → MaxPool

### Conv-2 Output (before pooling)
Input to Conv-2 = 27 × 27 × 48 (after LRN + MaxPool from previous stage, per GPU).
With padding $P = 2$ (same padding), stride $S = 1$, kernel $K = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{2}^{\text{conv}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{27 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
                    &= \left\lfloor \frac{26}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
                    &= 27
\end{aligned}
$$

### MaxPool of Conv-2 Output
Pooling kernel $K_p = 3$, stride $S_p = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{2}^{\text{pool}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{27 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
                    &= \left\lfloor \frac{24}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \\[6pt]
                    &= 12 + 1 \\[6pt]
                    &= 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The post-MaxPool output of Conv-2 is **13 × 13 × 128** (per GPU). ✔ Matches paper.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — FC-1 Input Flatten Dimension

### Pre-Flatten Feature Map
Before FC-1, the feature map size is **6 × 6 × 256** (combined from both GPUs after the final MaxPool of Conv-5).

### Flattening Operation
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{FC1}} &= 6 \times 6 \times 256 \\[6pt]
               &= 36 \times 256 \\[6pt]
               &= 9{,}216
\end{aligned}
$$

### Parameter Count of FC-1 (with bias)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Params}_{\text{FC1}} &= (D_{\text{FC1}} + 1) \cdot 4096 \\[6pt]
                           &= (9{,}216 + 1) \cdot 4096 \\[6pt]
                           &= 9{,}217 \cdot 4{,}096 \\[6pt]
                           &= 37{,}752{,}832
\end{aligned}
$$

The paper cites **37,748,736** parameters (without bias):
$$
9{,}216 \times 4{,}096 = 37{,}748{,}736
$$

**Key Insight:** FC-1 alone has **~63% of all parameters** in AlexNet. This is why dropout is applied *only* here.

---

## 3.5 Worked Derivation 5 — Total Parameter Count

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 17{,}424 + 153{,}600 + 442{,}368 + 331{,}776 + 221{,}184 \\
             &\quad + 37{,}748{,}736 + 16{,}777{,}216 + 4{,}096{,}000 \\[6pt]
             &\approx 59{,}788{,}304
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Parameters} \approx 60 \text{ million}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Derivation 6 — LRN Numerical Example

Suppose 3 consecutive channel activations at one pixel are: $a^{1} = 1.0$, $a^{2} = 2.0$, $a^{3} = 3.0$.
Use paper values $k=2$, $\alpha = 10^{-4}$, $\beta = 0.75$, $n = 5$.

For channel $i = 2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{denom} &= \left( k + \alpha \sum_{j=1}^{3} (a^{j})^{2} \right)^{\beta} \\[6pt]
             &= \left( 2 + 10^{-4}(1 + 4 + 9) \right)^{0.75} \\[6pt]
             &= \left( 2 + 10^{-4}(14) \right)^{0.75} \\[6pt]
             &= (2.0014)^{0.75} \\[6pt]
             &\approx 1.5011
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b^{2} &= \frac{a^{2}}{\text{denom}} = \frac{2.0}{1.5011} \approx 1.3323
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice $b^{2}$ is *slightly reduced* relative to $a^{2}$. This is **lateral inhibition**: a strongly activated neuron *suppresses* its neighbors, encouraging sparse, competitive activations.

---

## 3.7 KTU Analytical Matrix — Quick-Recall

| Question Asked | Formula to Apply |
|---|---|
| Output spatial size? | $O = \lfloor(W - K + 2P)/S\rfloor + 1$ |
| Parameters in a conv layer? | $(K \cdot K \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}}$ |
| Dropout output (train)? | Scale by $1/p$ |
| Softmax probability? | $e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ |
| Why ReLU over sigmoid? | No vanishing gradient for $x > 0$ |
| Total AlexNet params? | $\approx 60$ million |
| Largest layer by params? | FC-1 (~37.7M) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level AlexNet Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT IMAGE 227x227x3"] --> B["CONV1 LAYER\n11x11 STRIDE 4\nReLU + LRN + MAXPOOL\nOUTPUT 55x55x96"]
    B --> C["CONV2 LAYER\n5x5 PAD 2\nReLU + LRN + MAXPOOL\nOUTPUT 27x27x256"]
    C --> D["CONV3 LAYER\n3x3 PAD 1\nReLU\nOUTPUT 13x13x384"]
    D --> E["CONV4 LAYER\n3x3 PAD 1\nReLU\nOUTPUT 13x13x384"]
    E --> F["CONV5 LAYER\n3x3 PAD 1\nReLU + MAXPOOL\nOUTPUT 6x6x256"]
    F --> G["FLATTEN LAYER\n9216 NEURONS"]
    G --> H["FC6 LAYER\n4096 NEURONS\nReLU + DROPOUT 0.5"]
    H --> I["FC7 LAYER\n4096 NEURONS\nReLU + DROPOUT 0.5"]
    I --> J["FC8 LAYER\n1000 NEURONS\nSOFTMAX"]
    J --> K["OUTPUT PROBABILITIES\n1000 CLASSES"]
```

## 4.2 Dual-GPU Training Split (Mermaid Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GPU1["GPU 1 PATH"]
        G1A["CONV1 55x55x48"] --> G1B["CONV2 27x27x128"]
        G1B --> G1C["CONV3 part 13x13x192"]
    end
    subgraph GPU2["GPU 2 PATH"]
        G2A["CONV1 55x55x48"] --> G2B["CONV2 27x27x128"]
        G2B --> G2C["CONV3 part 13x13x192"]
    end
    G1C --> MERGE["CROSS GPU MERGE\nCONV4 takes input from BOTH GPUs"]
    G2C --> MERGE
    MERGE --> G2D["CONV5 6x6x256"]
    G2D --> FC["FC LAYERS\n4096 4096 1000"]
    FC --> OUT["SOFTMAX OUTPUT"]
```

## 4.3 ReLU vs Sigmoid (Conceptual Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SIGMOID f x = 1 / 1+e^-x\nVANISHING GRADIENT FOR LARGE x"] -.->|SLOW TRAINING| R["RESULT"]
    R["RELU f x = max 0 x\nGRADIENT IS 1 FOR x GREATER THAN 0"] -->|FAST TRAINING| RESULT["6X FASTER CONVERGENCE"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block-Level Architecture (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEATURE_EXTRACTION["FEATURE EXTRACTION BLOCK"]
        F1["LOW LEVEL FEATURES\nEDGES CORNERS GRADIENTS\nLAYERS 1 AND 2"]
        F2["MID LEVEL FEATURES\nTEXTURES PARTS\nLAYERS 3 AND 4"]
        F3["HIGH LEVEL FEATURES\nOBJECT PARTS\nLAYER 5"]
    end
    subgraph CLASSIFICATION["CLASSIFICATION BLOCK"]
        C1["FULLY CONNECTED\nREASONING LAYERS\nFC6 FC7 WITH DROPOUT"]
        C2["DECISION LAYER\nFC8 SOFTMAX\n1000 CLASS PROBABILITIES"]
    end
    INPUT2["INPUT 227x227x3"] --> F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> C1 --> C2 --> OUTPUT2["PREDICTED CLASS"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Explain any three architectural innovations introduced in AlexNet that were not present in earlier CNNs such as LeNet-5.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **ReLU Activation (1 mark):** AlexNet used ReLU $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ instead of sigmoid/tanh, which eliminated the vanishing gradient problem and produced ~6× faster training.

2. **Dropout Regularization (1 mark):** Dropout with probability 0.5 was applied to the fully-connected layers (FC6, FC7) to prevent co-adaptation of neurons and reduce overfitting on the 1.2-million-image training set.

3. **GPU Parallelization (1 mark):** AlexNet was the first large-scale CNN to be split across **two GTX 580 GPUs** using cross-GPU connections in the middle layers, enabling training of networks far deeper than previously feasible.

*(Other valid answers: LRN, overlapping max pooling, data augmentation with PCA-color jittering.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is ReLU preferred over sigmoid activation in deep networks like AlexNet?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Vanishing gradient problem (1 mark):** Sigmoid's derivative $\sigma'(x) \le 0.25$ and saturates for large $|x|$, causing gradients to vanish in deep networks. ReLU's derivative is exactly **1 for $x>0$**, allowing gradients to flow without attenuation.

2. **Computational efficiency (1 mark):** ReLU involves only a max operation, whereas sigmoid requires expensive exponentials. This gives a major speed advantage.

3. **Empirical convergence (1 mark):** Krizhevsky et al. reported that a 4-layer CNN with ReLU reached **25% training error** in **6 epochs**, while an equivalent sigmoid network failed to reach this error even after **25× more epochs**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Draw the architecture of AlexNet and explain the role of each layer type in feature extraction and classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Local Response Normalization (LRN)**. State its formula and explain how it improves generalization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) AlexNet Architecture — 7 marks**

AlexNet has **8 trainable layers**: 5 convolutional + 3 fully connected.

| Stage | Layer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conv1 (11×11, stride 4)** | Detects low-level features: edges, color blobs. Output: 55×55×96. |
| 2 | **Conv2 (5×5, pad 2)** | Detects mid-level textures and corners. Output: 27×27×256. |
| 3 | **Conv3 (3×3, pad 1)** | Captures object parts; receives input from **both GPUs** (cross-GPU connection). |
| 4 | **Conv4 (3×3, pad 1)** | Refines object-part detectors. |
| 5 | **Conv5 (3×3, pad 1)** + MaxPool | Final feature map: 6×6×256. |
| 6 | **FC6 (4096)** + ReLU + Dropout(0.5) | Combines spatial features into high-level reasoning. |
| 7 | **FC7 (4096)** + ReLU + Dropout(0.5) | Further reasoning layer. |
| 8 | **FC8 (1000)** + Softmax | Outputs 1000 class probabilities. |

**Role distinction (1 mark):** Conv layers act as **automatic feature extractors**, while FC layers act as a **trainable classifier** similar to an MLP.

---

**(b) Local Response Normalization — 7 marks**

**Diagram (3 marks):**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEATURE MAP i-1"] --> N["LRN NORMALIZATION"]
    B["FEATURE MAP i"] --> N
    C["FEATURE MAP i+1"] --> N
    N --> OUT["NORMALIZED ACTIVATION b"]
```

**Formula (2 marks):**
$$
b_{x,y}^{i} = \frac{a_{x,y}^{i}}{\left( k + \alpha \sum_{j=i-n/2}^{i+n/2} (a_{x,y}^{j})^{2} \right)^{\beta}}
$$

with $k=2$, $\alpha = 10^{-4}$, $\beta = 0.75$, $n = 5$.

**Explanation (2 marks):** LRN implements **lateral inhibition**, a biological concept where a strongly firing neuron suppresses its neighbors. This creates **competition** among adjacent feature maps, encouraging **sparse activations**, improving generalization and reducing top-1 error by ~1.4%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain **Dropout regularization** as used in AlexNet. Derive how it modifies the forward pass during training and inference. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **total number of trainable parameters** in AlexNet. Identify the layer containing the maximum parameters and explain why this creates a need for dropout. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Dropout — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Dropout is a regularization technique in which, during each training iteration, each neuron in selected layers is **temporarily deactivated** (output forced to 0) with probability $p$. In AlexNet, $p = 0.5$ in FC6 and FC7.

**Training forward pass (3 marks):**
$$
\hat{y} = \frac{1}{p}\,(W \cdot (r \ast x) + b), \quad r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)
$$

**Inference forward pass (2 marks):**
$$
\hat{y} = W \cdot x + b
$$

At test time, **all neurons are used** and no scaling is applied (since weights are already scaled during training).

**Why it helps:** Prevents co-adaptation; effectively trains an ensemble of $2^{N}$ sub-networks.

---

**(b) Parameter Count — 7 marks**

| Layer | Calculation | Params |
|---|---|---|
| Conv1 | $11 \cdot 11 \cdot 3 \cdot 96$ | 34,944 |
| Conv2 | $5 \cdot 5 \cdot 96 \cdot 256$ | 614,400 |
| Conv3 | $3 \cdot 3 \cdot 256 \cdot 384$ | 884,736 |
| Conv4 | $3 \cdot 3 \cdot 192 \cdot 384$ | 663,936 |
| Conv5 | $3 \cdot 3 \cdot 192 \cdot 256$ | 442,368 |
| **FC6** | $9216 \cdot 4096$ | **37,748,736** |
| FC7 | $4096 \cdot 4096$ | 16,777,216 |
| FC8 | $4096 \cdot 1000$ | 4,096,000 |

$$
\text{Total} \approx 62{,}362{,}336 \;\;(\text{without biases})
$$

The paper rounds this to **~60 million**.

**Layer with maximum parameters (1 mark):** **FC6** with **~37.7 million** parameters — over **60%** of the entire network. This creates enormous capacity for memorization, making overfitting almost inevitable without **dropout (p = 0.5)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Image size confusion:** Many students write **224 × 224** instead of **227 × 227**. The paper used **227** (rounded due to odd-numbered filters); 224 is the VGG convention. *(−1 mark)*
> 
> 2. **Parameter formula errors:** Forgetting the bias term $(+1)$ or miscounting input channels in split-GPU layers. *(−2 marks)*
> 
> 3. **Confusing LRN with Batch Normalization:** BatchNorm was introduced in 2015 by Ioffe & Szegedy. **AlexNet (2012) used LRN, NOT BatchNorm.** *(−2 marks)*
> 
> 4. **Writing "Dropout prevents overfitting" without explanation:** Always state the *mechanism*: neuron deactivation probability + ensemble averaging. *(−1 mark)*
> 
> 5. **Forgetting ReLU in conv layers:** Every conv layer in AlexNet is followed by ReLU. *(−1 mark)*
> 
> 6. **Sigmoid derivative mistake:** Writing $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ is fine, but many forget to note that $\sigma'(x) \le 0.25$ → vanishing gradient. *(−1 mark)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for AlexNet (KTU Module 2)**
> 
> - **Authors:** Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton (2012 NIPS paper).
> - **Achievement:** Won **ILSVRC-2012** with **15.3% top-5 error** vs **26.2%** runner-up.
> - **Architecture:** **8 layers** (5 conv + 3 FC), **~60 million parameters**.
> - **Input:** **227 × 227 × 3** RGB image.
> - **Output:** **1000 classes** via Softmax.
> - **Activation:** **ReLU** after every conv & FC layer (no sigmoid anywhere).
> - **Regularization:** **Dropout (p = 0.5)** in FC6 and FC7 only.
> - **Normalization:** **Local Response Normalization (LRN)** with $k=2, \alpha=10^{-4}, \beta=0.75, n=5$.
> - **Pooling:** **Overlapping max-pool** (3×3, stride 2).
> - **Augmentation:** Image translations, horizontal flips, **PCA color jittering**.
> - **Hardware:** **Two GTX 580 GPUs**, ~1.5 GB VRAM each.
> - **Key formulas to memorize:**
>   - ReLU: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$
>   - Softmax: $e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$
>   - Conv output: $O = \lfloor(W - K + 2P)/S\rfloor + 1$
>   - Conv params: $(K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}}$
> - **Largest layer:** **FC6** with **~37.7M parameters** (~63% of total).
> - **Did NOT use:** Batch Normalization (came later), Residual connections (came with ResNet in 2015).
> - **Impact:** Proved GPUs can train deep CNNs; launched modern deep-learning era; ReLU and Dropout became industry standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ResNet

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ResNet (Residual Network)** is a class of deep convolutional neural network architecture introduced by **Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, and Jian Sun** of Microsoft Research in their landmark 2015 paper *"Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition"*. The defining innovation of ResNet is the introduction of **identity shortcut (skip) connections** that bypass one or more layers, enabling the training of networks with **extraordinary depth** — up to **152 layers** (and even over 1000 layers in later variants) — without suffering from the **degradation problem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *ResNet is a deep CNN architecture that reformulates the layer learning objective from direct mapping H(x) to residual mapping F(x) = H(x) − x, using identity skip connections to enable optimization of very deep networks.*

The mathematical backbone of ResNet is the **residual learning formulation**:

$$H(x) = F(x) + x$$

where:
- $x$ is the input to a residual block
- $F(x)$ is the residual function learned by stacked convolutional layers
- $H(x)$ is the desired underlying mapping

## 1.2 The Problem ResNet Solves: Degradation vs. Vanishing Gradient

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** ResNet primarily solves the **Degradation Problem**, NOT the vanishing gradient problem. The degradation problem refers to the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding more layers to a *suitably deep* model leads to **higher training error**, even when the optimization is feasible. This is distinct from the vanishing gradient issue (which is also partially mitigated by the skip connections).

| Problem | Cause | Effect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Backpropagated gradients shrink exponentially through chain rule | Early layers learn negligibly; training stalls |
| **Degradation Problem** | Optimizer cannot easily learn identity mappings in deep stacks | Training accuracy saturates and then degrades |
| **Exploding Gradient** | Gradients grow exponentially | Training diverges or becomes numerically unstable |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Study Cram Sessions"

> [!TIP]
> **The Cramming Student Analogy:** Imagine a student preparing for an exam by adding **more and more hours of study** every day. After 4 hours/day, scores improve. After 8 hours, scores plateau. After 14 hours, scores actually **drop** because the student becomes exhausted, forgets earlier material, and the learning process becomes counterproductive. The intuitive solution is **not to study harder, but to make each study session more efficient** by allowing the brain to **shortcut** to the previously learned material rather than re-deriving everything from scratch.

This is exactly what ResNet does. Instead of forcing each stack of layers to learn an entirely new transformation $H(x)$, ResNet lets the layers learn only the **residual (difference)** $F(x) = H(x) - x$ — and the **identity shortcut** preserves the original input $x$. The network can thus easily fall back to identity mapping (just by driving $F(x) \to 0$), which is the simplest possible transformation, thereby **preventing degradation**.

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy: The Highway Bypass

> [!TIP]
> **The Highway Analogy:** Consider a city with congested narrow roads (the original deep network). As traffic increases (deeper networks), the roads become more and more jammed (degradation). The solution is to build **highway bypasses** (skip connections) that allow vehicles (data/gradients) to skip past the congested segments. Vehicles can still use the local roads (convolutional layers) to learn complex local features, but the bypass ensures that information flows efficiently from input to output without loss.

## 1.5 Why ResNet Was a Watershed Moment

Before ResNet (2015), state-of-the-art CNNs had reached depth of around **16-22 layers** (VGG, GoogLeNet). ResNet achieved a top-5 error of **3.57%** on the ImageNet dataset, winning the **ILSVRC 2015 classification task**, and was a foundational architecture for nearly all subsequent deep learning breakthroughs including:
- Object detection (Faster R-CNN, YOLO backbones)
- Semantic segmentation (DeepLab)
- Medical imaging (tumor detection, COVID-19 diagnosis)
- Generative models (ResNet generators in GANs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Benchmark:** ResNet-152 achieved **3.57% top-5 error** on ImageNet — *lower than the human benchmark of ~5%* — with a model depth **8× deeper than VGG-19** while having **lower computational complexity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Mathematical Formulation

In a traditional deep network, each stacked layer is expected to learn a direct mapping $H(x)$. ResNet reformulates this expectation. Instead of approximating $H(x)$ directly, the layers approximate the **residual function**:

$$F(x) := H(x) - x$$

The original mapping is then recast as:

$$H(x) = F(x) + x$$

The network is thus forced to learn the **deviation from identity**, not the entire transformation from scratch.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Residual Building Block

A **residual block** consists of two parallel paths:

### Path 1: The Residual Mapping F(x)
This is the path through 2 or 3 convolutional layers with batch normalization and ReLU activations. For a "basic" block:

$$F(x) = W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 \cdot x + b_1) + b_2$$

For a "bottleneck" block (used in deeper networks):

$$F(x) = W_3 \cdot \sigma\bigl(W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 \cdot x + b_1) + b_2\bigr) + b_3$$

### Path 2: The Identity Shortcut
The input $x$ is forwarded directly to the output via a skip connection that performs **element-wise addition** with $F(x)$.

### Final Output
$$y = \sigma\bigl(F(x, \{W_i\}) + x\bigr)$$

where $\sigma$ denotes ReLU and $\{W_i\}$ are the learnable weights of the residual layers.

## 2.3 The "+1" in the Gradient: Why Skip Connections Save the Day

During backpropagation, applying the chain rule to the residual block:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **+1** term in the gradient is the *single most important contribution* of skip connections. Even in the extreme case where $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} \to 0$ (e.g., the residual layers collapse or saturate), the gradient **never vanishes** because it always has a direct path of magnitude 1 back to the input. This is the mathematical explanation of why ResNet effectively eliminates the vanishing gradient problem and enables training of ultra-deep networks.

## 2.4 Types of Shortcut Connections

| Shortcut Type | Formula | Use Case |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Identity Shortcut** | $y = F(x) + x$ | When input and output dimensions match |
| **Projection Shortcut** | $y = F(x) + W_s \cdot x$ | When dimensions need to match (downsampling) |
| **Zero Padding Shortcut** | $y = F(x) + \text{pad}(x)$ | Cheap dimension matching (used in original ResNet) |

## 2.5 ResNet Family: Architectural Comparison

| Architecture | Layers | Basic/Bottleneck Blocks | Parameters (M) | FLOPs (G) | Top-5 Error (ImageNet) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **ResNet-18** | 18 | Basic (2-layer) | 11.7 | 1.8 | 10.76% |
| **ResNet-34** | 34 | Basic (2-layer) | 21.8 | 3.6 | 8.70% |
| **ResNet-50** | 50 | Bottleneck (3-layer) | 25.6 | 4.1 | 7.02% |
| **ResNet-101** | 101 | Bottleneck (3-layer) | 44.5 | 7.8 | 6.43% |
| **ResNet-152** | 152 | Bottleneck (3-layer) | 60.2 | 11.5 | 6.16% |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula/Expression | Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Residual Mapping | $F(x) = H(x) - x$ | The deviation from identity |
| Forward Pass | $y = \sigma(F(x) + x)$ | Output after residual addition + activation |
| Basic Block | $F = W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2$ | Two-conv residual unit |
| Bottleneck Block | $F = W_3 \cdot \text{ReLU}(W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2) + b_3$ | Three-conv residual unit with 1x1 reductions |
| Gradient Flow | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot (\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1)$ | The "+1" ensures non-vanishing gradients |
| Projection Shortcut | $y = F(x) + W_s x$ | When dimensions differ |
| Output Dimension (Conv) | $O = \lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \rfloor + 1$ | For a single conv layer, where $I$=input, $K$=kernel, $P$=padding, $S$=stride |
| Bottleneck Compression Ratio | $r = \frac{C_{\text{in}}}{C_{\text{mid}}}$ | Typically $r = 4$ in ResNet |
| Network Depth Formula | $D = 2n_1 + 3n_2 + 3n_3 + 3n_4 + 1$ | Total layers: 1 conv + 4 stages |
| Receptive Field | $RF_{l} = RF_{l-1} + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i$ | Cumulative receptive field |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

ResNet architectures are foundational in:
1. **Medical Imaging** — Tumor segmentation in CT/MRI scans (e.g., U-Net with ResNet encoders)
2. **Autonomous Vehicles** — Object detection backbones in Tesla, Waymo perception stacks
3. **Face Recognition** — ArcFace, FaceNet use ResNet backbones for embeddings
4. **Transfer Learning** — Pre-trained ResNets on ImageNet are the de-facto feature extractors for downstream tasks
5. **Generative AI** — StyleGAN, BigGAN, and Diffusion models use ResNet blocks as fundamental building blocks
6. **Remote Sensing** — Satellite image classification, land-use segmentation

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forward Pass Through a Basic Residual Block

**Given:** A residual block with input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ and two convolutional layers with weights $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C \times 3 \times 3}$ and $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C \times 3 \times 3}$, biases $b_1, b_2$, ReLU activation $\sigma(\cdot)$, and Batch Normalization $\text{BN}(\cdot)$.

**Step 1 — Apply first convolution, BN, and ReLU to input $x$:**

$$z_1 = W_1 * x + b_1$$

This is the convolution operation where $*$ denotes the discrete convolution. For a single position $(i,j,c)$:

$$z_1(i,j,c) = \sum_{c'=1}^{C} \sum_{p=-1}^{1} \sum_{q=-1}^{1} W_1(c, c', p, q) \cdot x(i+p, j+q, c') + b_1(c)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Batch Normalization** to normalize activations:

$$\hat{z}_1 = \text{BN}(z_1) = \gamma \cdot \frac{z_1 - \mu_{\text{batch}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\text{batch}}^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$$

where $\mu_{\text{batch}}$ and $\sigma_{\text{batch}}^2$ are the per-channel batch mean and variance, and $\gamma, \beta$ are learnable scale and shift parameters.

**Step 3 — Apply ReLU non-linearity:**

$$a_1 = \sigma(\hat{z}_1) = \max(0, \hat{z}_1)$$

**Step 4 — Apply second convolution and BN to $a_1$:**

$$z_2 = W_2 * a_1 + b_2$$

$$\hat{z}_2 = \text{BN}(z_2)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the residual function $F(x)$:**

$$F(x) = \hat{z}_2 = \text{BN}(W_2 * \text{ReLU}(\text{BN}(W_1 * x + b_1)) + b_2)$$

**Step 6 — Identity shortcut addition:**

The shortcut carries the original input $x$ unchanged. For dimension matching, either:
- Use $x$ directly if dimensions match (true identity)
- Use $W_s \cdot x$ if a projection is needed (typically $1 \times 1$ conv)

**Step 7 — Final ReLU activation:**

$$y = \sigma(F(x) + x) = \text{ReLU}(F(x) + x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The original ResNet paper applies ReLU **after** the addition, but later variants (Pre-activation ResNet, ResNet-v2) apply BN and ReLU **before** the convolutions for better gradient flow.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Backward Pass — The "Magic +1" of Skip Connections

**Given:** The output $y = \sigma(F(x) + x)$ propagates to a loss $\mathcal{L}$. We want to compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the chain rule to $y$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $y = \sigma(F(x) + x)$ with respect to its argument $u = F(x) + x$:**

Let $u = F(x) + x$. Then $y = \sigma(u)$. Since $\sigma$ is ReLU, its derivative is:

$$\sigma'(u) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } u > 0 \\ 0, & \text{if } u \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

For the case $u > 0$ (active ReLU):

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial u} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $u = F(x) + x$ with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial x}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1$$

**Step 4 — Combine steps 1, 2, and 3:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot 1 \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right) = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right)$$

**Step 5 — Analysis of the critical "+1" term:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}}_{\text{Residual path contribution}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}}_{\text{Direct skip path contribution}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even if the residual path gradient $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}$ becomes negligibly small (vanishes), the **direct skip path contribution $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}$** remains intact, ensuring the gradient is never zero. This is the fundamental reason skip connections eliminate the vanishing gradient problem.

**Step 6 — Stacking $L$ residual blocks in sequence:**

For a chain of $L$ blocks with outputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_L$ where $x_{l+1} = F_l(x_l) + x_l$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \prod_{i=l}^{L-1} \left(\frac{\partial F_{i+1}}{\partial x_{i+1}} + 1\right)$$

**Step 7 — Take the absolute value to analyze gradient magnitude:**

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \right\vert \cdot \prod_{i=l}^{L-1} \left\vert \frac{\partial F_{i+1}}{\partial x_{i+1}} + 1 \right\vert$$

Since each term $\left\vert \frac{\partial F_{i+1}}{\partial x_{i+1}} + 1 \right\vert \geq 1$ (because of the +1), the product is **always greater than or equal to 1**, guaranteeing that the gradient magnitude **does not decay** as it propagates backward.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pre-Activation vs. Post-Activation ResNet

In the **original ResNet (post-activation)**, the order is: Conv → BN → ReLU → Conv → BN → Add → ReLU.

In **Pre-activation ResNet (ResNet-v2)**, the order is: BN → ReLU → Conv → BN → ReLU → Conv → Add.

**Forward pass for pre-activation:**

$$x_{l+1} = x_l + F_l(\text{ReLU}(\text{BN}(x_l)))$$

**Gradient flow in pre-activation:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_{l+1}} \cdot \left(I + \frac{\partial F_l}{\partial x_l}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Pre-activation is mathematically cleaner because the gradient through the identity path is exactly $I$ (the identity matrix), with no interference from ReLU. This leads to cleaner optimization and empirically better performance on deeper networks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Why Deeper ResNets Generalize Better

| Network Depth | Plain Net (Top-5 Err) | ResNet (Top-5 Err) | Gap |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 18 layers | 27.94% | 10.76% | 17.18% |
| 34 layers | 28.54% | 8.70% | 19.84% |
| 50 layers | N/A (vanishing) | 7.02% | N/A |
| 101 layers | N/A | 6.43% | N/A |
| 152 layers | N/A | 6.16% | N/A |

The plain (non-residual) network's error **increases** from 18 to 34 layers (degradation), while ResNet's error **monotonically decreases** with depth.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Gradient Magnitude in a 10-Layer Network

Suppose each layer's weight gradient is $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} = 0.1$ on average. For a plain network:

$$g_{\text{plain}} = 0.1^{10} = 10^{-10} \quad \text{(vanishes!)}$$

For a ResNet with skip connections:

$$g_{\text{resnet}} = (0.1 + 1)^{10} = 1.1^{10} \approx 2.59 \quad \text{(preserved!)}$$

This numerical comparison (without using the | symbol) vividly demonstrates why ResNets can be trained to extreme depths.

## 3.6 Analytical Derivation: Output Dimension Through ResNet Stages

For an input image of size $224 \times 224 \times 3$:

$$H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{\text{in}} - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

| Stage | Layer | Output Size (HxWxC) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Input | — | 224 x 224 x 3 |
| Conv1 | 7x7, stride 2 | 112 x 112 x 64 |
| MaxPool | 3x3, stride 2 | 56 x 56 x 64 |
| Stage 1 | 3 bottleneck blocks | 56 x 56 x 256 |
| Stage 2 | 4 bottleneck blocks (downsample) | 28 x 28 x 512 |
| Stage 3 | 6 bottleneck blocks (downsample) | 14 x 14 x 1024 |
| Stage 4 | 3 bottleneck blocks (downsample) | 7 x 7 x 2048 |
| AvgPool | 7x7, stride 1 | 1 x 1 x 2048 |
| FC | 1000 classes | 1 x 1 x 1000 |
