# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the degradation problem in deep neural networks. How does ResNet address it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **degradation problem** refers to the phenomenon where, as the depth of a neural network increases beyond a certain threshold, the **training accuracy saturates and then degrades** even when batch normalization and other regularization techniques are used. Importantly, this is **not** caused by overfitting (test error also degrades), but by the optimizer's inability to find good solutions for the deep network.

**How ResNet addresses it:** ResNet reformulates the layer's learning objective from directly approximating the desired mapping $H(x)$ to approximating a **residual mapping** $F(x) = H(x) - x$. The original mapping is reconstructed as $H(x) = F(x) + x$ via an **identity shortcut connection**. This makes it easy for the optimizer to fall back to the identity mapping (by driving $F(x) \to 0$) when additional layers are unnecessary, effectively preventing degradation.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + ResNet solution (1.5)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Write the mathematical formulation of a residual block and explain the role of the +1 term in gradient backpropagation.**

**Model Answer:**

The forward pass of a residual block is:

$$y = \sigma\bigl(F(x, \{W_i\}) + x\bigr)$$

The gradient flowing back to the input $x$ during backpropagation is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right)$$

**Role of the +1 term:** The +1 is the gradient of the identity shortcut path $\frac{\partial x}{\partial x} = 1$. Even if the residual gradient $\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}$ vanishes (e.g., weights saturate), the +1 ensures that the total gradient **never becomes zero**, thus eliminating the vanishing gradient problem. This guarantees that every layer in an arbitrarily deep ResNet receives a non-zero gradient signal, enabling successful training.

**Mark Distribution:** Formulation (1.5) + Explanation of +1 term (1.5)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of a basic residual block and a bottleneck residual block with neat diagrams. Compare their computational efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Derive the gradient flow equation through a residual block and show mathematically why skip connections prevent vanishing gradients in deep networks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q(a) — Basic vs. Bottleneck Block (7 marks)

**Basic Residual Block:**

A basic block contains **two 3x3 convolutional layers** with batch normalization and ReLU activations:

$$F(x) = W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(\text{BN}(W_1 \cdot x + b_1)) + b_2$$

The output is:

$$y = \text{ReLU}(F(x) + x)$$

**Bottleneck Residual Block:**

A bottleneck block uses three convolutions: **1x1 → 3x3 → 1x1**. The 1x1 convolutions reduce and then restore dimensions, creating a "bottleneck":

$$F(x) = W_3 \cdot \text{ReLU}\bigl(\text{BN}(W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(\text{BN}(W_1 \cdot x + b_1)) + b_2)\bigr) + b_3$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Basic Block | Bottleneck Block |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Convolutions | Two 3x3 | One 1x1, one 3x3, one 1x1 |
| Parameter count (relative) | High | Lower by ~factor of 4 |
| Used in | ResNet-18, ResNet-34 | ResNet-50, 101, 152 |
| Purpose | Simple feature learning | Deep, parameter-efficient learning |
| FLOPs for C=256 channels | 2 x 256^2 x 9 = 1.18M | 256x64x1 + 64x64x9 + 64x256x1 = 0.29M |

The bottleneck design reduces computational cost by **~75%** while preserving representational power, enabling the construction of much deeper networks (50+ layers).

**Mark Distribution:** Basic block explanation (2) + Bottleneck block (2) + Comparison (3)

---

#### Solution to Q(b) — Gradient Flow Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Consider a residual block with input $x$ and output $y = F(x) + x$ (ignoring ReLU for derivation clarity).

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule to compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $y = F(x) + x$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1$$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1\right)$$

**Step 5:** For a chain of $L$ residual blocks, by the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_{l+1}} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial F_{l+1}}{\partial x_{l+1}} + 1\right)$$

Iterating for $L$ blocks:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{L} \left(\frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_i} + 1\right)$$

**Step 6:** Take the magnitude to analyze:

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \right\vert \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{L} \left\vert \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_i} + 1 \right\vert$$

Since $\left\vert \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_i} + 1 \right\vert \geq 1$ for each block (because of the +1), the product is always $\geq 1$.

**Step 7:** **Conclusion:** Therefore, $\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} \right\vert \geq \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \right\vert$. The gradient **does not shrink** as it propagates backward through any number of residual blocks. This is the mathematical proof that skip connections prevent vanishing gradients, enabling training of networks with hundreds or thousands of layers.

**Mark Distribution:** Derivation steps 1-4 (3) + Stacking and magnitude analysis (2.5) + Conclusion (1.5)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Compare the architectures of ResNet-18, ResNet-50, and ResNet-152. Explain why deeper variants use bottleneck blocks. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the difference between pre-activation and post-activation residual blocks. What are the advantages of pre-activation ResNet? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q(a) — ResNet Variants Comparison (7 marks)

| Property | ResNet-18 | ResNet-50 | ResNet-152 |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Block type | Basic (2-conv) | Bottleneck (3-conv) | Bottleneck (3-conv) |
| Stage 1 blocks | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| Stage 2 blocks | 2 | 4 | 8 |
| Stage 3 blocks | 2 | 6 | 36 |
| Stage 4 blocks | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| Total conv layers | 17 | 49 | 151 |
| Total parameters | 11.7M | 25.6M | 60.2M |
| Top-5 Error (ImageNet) | 10.76% | 7.02% | 6.16% |

**Why deeper variants use bottleneck blocks:**

1. **Parameter efficiency:** A 3x3 conv with 256 channels costs $256 \times 256 \times 9 = 589{,}824$ parameters per layer. A bottleneck reduces the 256 channels to 64 via 1x1 conv, performs 3x3 conv on 64 channels, then expands back to 256. Total cost: $256 \times 64 \times 1 + 64 \times 64 \times 9 + 64 \times 256 \times 1 = 16{,}384 + 36{,}864 + 16{,}384 = 69{,}632$ parameters — a **~88% reduction**.

2. **Computational savings:** The FLOPs reduction allows deeper networks to train within practical time/memory budgets.

3. **Deeper non-linear feature combinations:** Even with bottleneck dimensions, the stacking of 50+ blocks yields richer representations than shallow basic-block networks.

4. **Deeper without degradation:** Bottleneck blocks combined with skip connections have enabled networks of 152+ layers to outperform shallower ones consistently.

**Mark Distribution:** Table comparison (3) + Bottleneck rationale (4)

---

#### Solution to Q(b) — Pre-activation vs. Post-activation (7 marks)

**Post-activation ResNet (Original 2015):**

The order of operations is: Conv → BN → ReLU → Conv → BN → Add → ReLU.

$$x_{l+1} = \text{ReLU}\bigl(F_l(x_l, \{W_i\}) + x_l\bigr)$$

**Issue:** The ReLU after the addition modifies the signal on the identity path, meaning the gradient on the skip connection is not a pure identity but is "filtered" by the ReLU derivative.

**Pre-activation ResNet (ResNet-v2, 2016):**

The order is: BN → ReLU → Conv → BN → ReLU → Conv → Add.

$$x_{l+1} = x_l + F_l(\text{ReLU}(\text{BN}(x_l)))$$

**Advantages of Pre-activation:**

1. **Cleaner gradient flow:** The gradient on the identity path is exactly $I$ (no ReLU interference), satisfying:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_{l+1}} \cdot \left(I + \frac{\partial F_l}{\partial x_l}\right)$$

2. **Easier optimization:** Experiments show ~0.2-0.3% accuracy improvement on CIFAR-10/100 for very deep networks (>100 layers).

3. **Better regularization:** BN and ReLU at the input side act as additional regularization.

4. **Eliminates "over-activation":** Prevents the problem where all signals become non-negative and ReLU becomes effectively linear.

5. **Improved convergence on extremely deep networks** (e.g., 1001-layer ResNet on CIFAR-10).

**Mark Distribution:** Post-activation explanation (2) + Pre-activation explanation (2) + Advantages (3)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in ResNet Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Vanishing Gradient with Degradation Problem (–2 marks):** Many students incorrectly state that ResNet solves the *vanishing gradient problem*. While skip connections do help with this, the **primary** problem ResNet solves is the **degradation problem** (training error increasing with depth). Be explicit about this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Omitting the "+1" Term in Derivations (–2 marks):** When asked to derive gradient flow, students frequently write $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}$ and forget the **+1** term. This is the most critical step; missing it loses substantial marks.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Basic and Bottleneck Blocks (–1.5 marks):** Basic blocks use 2x(3x3) convs; bottleneck blocks use 1x1 → 3x3 → 1x1. The "bottleneck" is the dimension reduction at the 1x1 stage. Mixing these up costs marks in architecture questions.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting Activation Placement:** Always state whether you are using **pre-activation** or **post-activation**. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous answers.
> 
> 5. **Not Stating Assumptions in Derivations:** When deriving gradient flow, explicitly state that the +1 arises from the identity mapping $x \to x$ having derivative 1. This shows conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist for ResNet:**

- ✅ **Core Innovation:** ResNet introduces **identity skip connections** that reformulate layer learning as residual mapping $F(x) = H(x) - x$.

- ✅ **Forward Pass:** $y = \text{ReLU}(F(x) + x)$ where $F(x)$ is a stack of 2 (basic) or 3 (bottleneck) conv layers.

- ✅ **Primary Problem Solved:** **Degradation problem** (training error increases with depth), not just vanishing gradients.

- ✅ **The Magic "+1":** Backpropagated gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} \cdot (\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1)$ — the +1 prevents vanishing gradients regardless of network depth.

- ✅ **Two Block Types:** Basic block (2x 3x3 convs, for ResNet-18/34) and Bottleneck block (1x1 → 3x3 → 1x1, for ResNet-50/101/152).

- ✅ **ResNet Variants:** ResNet-18, 34, 50, 101, 152. Deeper = better accuracy, all using skip connections.

- ✅ **Performance Benchmark:** ResNet-152 achieved **3.57% top-5 error** on ImageNet (ILSVRC 2015 winner).

- ✅ **Shortcut Types:** Identity (matching dims), Projection (mismatched dims using 1x1 conv), Zero-padding (cheap dimension matching).

- ✅ **Pre-activation vs. Post-activation:** Pre-activation (BN → ReLU → Conv) provides cleaner gradient flow and better performance on ultra-deep networks.

- ✅ **Key Applications:** Object detection, image classification, medical imaging, transfer learning, generative models (GANs), face recognition.

- ✅ **Originating Paper:** He et al., "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition," CVPR 2016 (best paper award).

- ✅ **Architectural Pattern:** Stem (7x7 conv) → 4 stages with downsampling → Global AvgPool → FC → Softmax.

- ✅ **Stacking Depth Formula:** $D = 2n_1 + 3n_2 + 3n_3 + 3n_4 + 1$ where $n_i$ is the number of blocks in stage $i$.

- ✅ **Bottleneck Compression Ratio:** Typically 4:1 (e.g., 256 → 64 → 256 channels), reducing parameters by ~75%.

- ✅ **Gradient Preservation:** $\left\vert \frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_i} + 1 \right\vert \geq 1$ for every block, ensuring no exponential decay of gradient signal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of CNNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of CNNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) Applications** refer to the deployment of hierarchical, weight-sharing, translation-invariant neural architectures to extract **spatially-correlated local features** from grid-structured data (images, video frames, spectrograms) and use them to perform high-level perception tasks such as **classification, localization, detection, segmentation, recognition, and generation**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT832 – Deep Learning, Module 2)**, "Applications of CNNs" is a descriptive-application module that maps CNN primitives (convolution, pooling, fully-connected layers) onto **industry-grade computer vision problems**. The focus is on understanding *which architectural variant solves which visual task*, and the corresponding *loss functions, evaluation metrics, and inference pipelines*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnifying Glass Detective"

Imagine a detective investigating a large crime scene photograph.

- **First**, she uses a *low-power magnifying glass* to scan for *broad patterns* (room layout, color tones). This is the **early convolutional layer** — it captures *low-level features* (edges, gradients).
- **Next**, she switches to a *medium-power lens* to spot *shapes* (a door, a window). This is a **middle layer** — it captures *mid-level features* (textures, parts).
- **Finally**, she uses a *high-power lens* to identify *specific objects* (a face, a weapon). This is a **deeper layer** — it captures *high-level semantic features*.
- She writes a *report* (a 1-D feature vector) summarizing what she saw — this is the **fully-connected classifier head**.

> The detective never memorizes the *exact location* of the magnifying glass sweep — she re-applies the same lens across the whole image. This is **weight sharing**, the core reason CNNs work so well on images.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of CNN Applications

Every CNN application sits on **three operational pillars**:

| Pillar | Technical Term | What It Does |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar I** | **Backbone** (Feature Extractor) | Convolutional + pooling stack that converts raw pixels into a semantic feature map |
| **Pillar II** | **Neck / Head** (Task Adapter) | Task-specific layers (e.g., RoI pooling for detection, transposed conv for segmentation) |
| **Pillar III** | **Loss Function** | Objective that drives learning (Cross-Entropy, IoU Loss, L2, Contrastive) |

### 1.4 Syllabus-Mapped Taxonomy of Applications

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly lists the following CNN application families for Module 2:

1. **Image Classification** — assigning a single label to a whole image (e.g., cat / dog).
2. **Object Detection** — drawing bounding boxes *and* classifying each box.
3. **Semantic Segmentation** — labelling **every pixel** with a class.
4. **Instance Segmentation** — labelling every pixel *and* separating individual object instances.
5. **Face Recognition & Verification** — 1:1 or 1:N identity matching.
6. **Medical Image Analysis** — tumor detection, organ segmentation in MRI/CT.
7. **Image Style Transfer & Generation** — artistic stylization, super-resolution, GANs.
8. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** & Document Analysis.
9. **Video Analytics** — action recognition, tracking.
10. **Transfer Learning & Pre-trained Models** — using backbones (VGG, ResNet) as feature extractors for downstream tasks.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always **state the task, name the canonical architecture, and mention the loss function** in the first line. This instantly signals examiner-level competence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Application 1 — Image Classification

**Task:** Map an input image $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ to a class probability vector $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ where $K$ is the number of classes.

**Canonical Pipeline (End-to-End):**

- **Input Image** → resized to a fixed shape, e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3$.
- **Convolutional Backbone** (e.g., VGG-16, ResNet-50) → produces a $7 \times 7 \times 2048$ feature map.
- **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** → collapses spatial dims to a $2048$-D vector.
- **Fully-Connected Softmax Layer** → outputs class probabilities.
- **Argmax** → predicted class.

**Why it works:** Deeper backbones capture *hierarchical features*; GAP reduces parameters (preventing overfitting) compared to flattening.

**Loss:** Categorical Cross-Entropy,

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \, \log \hat{y}_{i,k}$$

**Evaluation Metric:** **Top-1 Accuracy** and **Top-5 Accuracy**.

> [!NOTE]
> Top-5 accuracy is the official ImageNet metric — the correct label is considered "predicted" if it appears in the model's top 5 probability outputs.

### 2.2 Application 2 — Object Detection

**Task:** Output a set of $(x, y, w, h, \text{class}, \text{confidence})$ tuples for *all* objects in the image.

Two architectural paradigms:

| Paradigm | How It Works | Pros / Cons | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-Stage Detectors** | Stage 1: *Region Proposal* (find ~2000 candidate boxes). Stage 2: *Classify + Refine* each box. | High accuracy, slow | **R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN** |
| **One-Stage Detectors** | Single CNN pass; predict boxes + classes directly on a feature grid. | Real-time, slightly lower accuracy | **YOLO (v1–v8), SSD, RetinaNet** |

**Critical Concepts:**

- **Anchor Boxes:** Pre-defined box templates of various scales & aspect ratios tiled across the feature map.
- **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** Removes redundant overlapping boxes using the **Intersection over Union (IoU)** threshold.
- **mAP (mean Average Precision):** The gold-standard detection metric — average of per-class AP values across IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95.

**Loss (Faster R-CNN):** Multi-task loss combining classification + box regression,

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}(p, p^{*}) + \lambda \, [p^{*} \geq 1] \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(t, t^{*})$$

where $p$ is predicted class probability, $p^{*}$ is the ground-truth class indicator, $t$ and $t^{*}$ are box-coordinate parameters, and $\lambda$ balances the two losses.

### 2.3 Application 3 — Semantic & Instance Segmentation

**Task:** Produce a per-pixel class map of the same spatial size as the input.

- **Semantic Segmentation** → all "cars" share one label, indistinguishable.
- **Instance Segmentation** → each "car" is a separate mask.

**Canonical Architectures:**

- **FCN (Fully Convolutional Network, 2015):** Replaces FC layers with $1 \times 1$ convolutions, then *upsamples* via transposed convolutions.
- **U-Net (2015):** Symmetric *encoder–decoder* with **skip connections** that fuse low-level spatial details with high-level semantic features. Dominant in medical imaging.
- **Mask R-CNN (2017):** Extends Faster R-CNN by adding a parallel *mask branch* that outputs a binary mask per detected object → enables **instance segmentation**.

**Loss:** Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy + **Dice Loss** for class imbalance,

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i} p_i + \sum_{i} g_i + \epsilon}$$

where $p_i$ is the predicted probability, $g_i$ is the ground-truth label, and $\epsilon$ is a small smoothing constant (e.g., $10^{-7}$).

### 2.4 Application 4 — Face Recognition

**Task:** Map a face image to a compact **embedding vector** such that vectors of the *same identity* are close, and vectors of *different identities* are far apart.

**Pipeline:**

- **Backbone** (e.g., FaceNet uses Inception, ArcFace uses ResNet-100) → 512-D embedding.
- **Loss Function:** **Triplet Loss** (FaceNet) or **ArcFace Loss** (state-of-the-art).

**Triplet Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{triplet}} = \max\Big(0, \, d(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{P}) - d(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{N}) + \alpha\Big)$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ = anchor, $\mathbf{P}$ = positive (same identity), $\mathbf{N}$ = negative (different identity), $d$ = Euclidean distance, and $\alpha$ is a margin.

### 2.5 Application 5 — Medical Image Analysis

- **Tumor Detection** (MRI/CT) → typically a **classification or detection** problem.
- **Organ / Lesion Segmentation** → **U-Net** is the de-facto standard.
- **Disease Classification** (e.g., diabetic retinopathy, pneumonia) → **transfer learning** with ImageNet-pretrained backbones (ResNet, EfficientNet).
- **Survival Prediction** → CNNs extract features from pathological slides, fused with clinical metadata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Medical datasets are *tiny* (hundreds, not millions of images). KTU examiners expect students to mention **data augmentation (rotation, flip, elastic deformation)** and **transfer learning** as standard remedies.

### 2.6 Application 6 — Style Transfer & Generative Models

**Neural Style Transfer (Gatys et al., 2015):** Optimizes a *white-noise* image to simultaneously match

- **Content** of a photo (via high-layer Gram matrices of a pre-trained VGG)
- **Style** of a painting (via low-layer Gram matrices)

**Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \alpha \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{content}} + \beta \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}$$

**GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks):** A *Generator* (often a CNN with transposed convolutions, e.g., **DCGAN**) fabricates images; a *Discriminator* (a standard CNN classifier) tries to distinguish real from fake. The two networks train in adversarial lockstep.

**Other Generative CNN Applications:**

- **Super-Resolution:** SRCNN, ESPCAN — map low-res to high-res images.
- **Image Inpainting:** Fill missing regions.
- **DeepFakes:** Face-swap via encoder–decoder CNNs.

### 2.7 Application 7 — Optical Character Recognition (OCR) & Document AI

- **CNN Feature Extractor** → text region features.
- **RNN / LSTM / Transformer** → sequence modelling of characters.
- **CTC Loss (Connectionist Temporal Classification)** → trains without needing character-level alignment.

Architecture: **CRNN (CNN + RNN + CTC)** is the textbook example.

### 2.8 Application 8 — Video Analytics

- **Action Recognition:** 3-D CNN (C3D, I3D) processes *volumes* of frames $(H \times W \times T)$ to learn spatio-temporal features.
- **Object Tracking:** Siamese CNNs (e.g., **SiamMask**) re-detect the object in each frame.
- **Video Captioning:** CNN encodes each frame, RNN/Transformer decodes a sentence.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table verbatim for KTU ESE — these equations appear every year.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conv Output Size** | $O = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $I$ = input, $K$ = kernel, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride |
| 2 | **Transposed Conv Output** | $O = (I - 1) \cdot S - 2P + K$ | Used in segmentation / GANs |
| 3 | **Receptive Field** | $r_l = r_{l-1} + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i$ | Recursive; deeper = larger |
| 4 | **Conv Parameters** | $P = (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}}$ | The "+1" is the bias term |
| 5 | **Receptive Field (alt)** | $r = r_{\text{prev}} + (K - 1) \cdot j$ | $j$ = jump (product of previous strides) |
| 6 | **IoU (Jaccard)** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $0 \leq \text{IoU} \leq 1$ |
| 7 | **Dice Coefficient** | $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $2 \cdot \text{IoU} / (1 + \text{IoU})$ |
| 8 | **mAP** | $\text{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{AP}_k$ | $\text{AP}_k$ = area under PR-curve of class $k$ |
| 9 | **FLOPs per Conv Layer** | $\text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot K_h K_w C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}} H' W'$ | Multiply by 2 for MAC+FMA |
| 10 | **Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | Softmax + log-loss |
| 11 | **Triplet Loss** | $\max(0, \, d(A,P) - d(A,N) + \alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = margin, typically 0.2 |
| 12 | **Softmax** | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Outputs sum to 1 |
| 13 | **Batch Norm** | $\hat{x} = \gamma \dfrac{x - \mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$ | $\mu_B, \sigma_B$ per mini-batch |
| 14 | **Global Avg Pool** | $z_c = \dfrac{1}{H W} \sum_{i,j} a_{i,j,c}$ | Replaces FC, prevents overfit |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Architecture in Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Pedestrian / sign detection | YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN |
| **Healthcare** | Tumor segmentation | U-Net, nnU-Net |
| **Agriculture** | Leaf disease detection | ResNet + transfer learning |
| **Retail** | Shelf / checkout vision | EfficientDet |
| **Security** | Face unlock | ArcFace, FaceNet |
| **Satellite** | Land-cover mapping | FCN, DeepLabv3+ |
| **Entertainment** | Deepfake generation | StyleGAN, autoencoders |
| **Industry 4.0** | Defect detection on assembly lines | Custom CNN, EfficientNet |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Dimension of a Convolutional Layer

**Problem:** Compute the output feature-map size for an input image of size $32 \times 32 \times 3$ processed with a kernel of size $5 \times 5$, stride $S = 1$, padding $P = 0$.

**Step 1 — Start with the general formula.**

$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(0)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{27}{1} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$O = 27 + 1 = 28$$

**Answer:** The output feature map is of size $\mathbf{28 \times 28}$ per channel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Trainable Parameters in a CNN Layer

**Problem:** A conv layer takes an input of shape $224 \times 224 \times 3$, applies $64$ filters each of size $3 \times 3$, with stride $1$, padding $1$. Compute the parameter count.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$K_h = K_w = 3, \quad C_{\text{in}} = 3, \quad C_{\text{out}} = 64$$

**Step 2 — Apply the parameter formula.**

$$P = (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$P = (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 64 = (27 + 1) \cdot 64$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final result.**

$$P = 28 \cdot 64 = 1792$$

**Answer:** The layer has $\mathbf{1792}$ trainable parameters.

> [!TIP]
> Notice how *tiny* this is compared to a fully-connected layer of the equivalent size (which would have $\sim 10^7$ parameters). This is the **parameter efficiency** of convolutions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parameter Count Comparison: FC vs. Conv

**Problem:** Show that a fully-connected layer flattening a $7 \times 7 \times 512$ feature map to 4096 units has *more* parameters than a $3 \times 3$ conv layer with 512 input channels and 512 output channels.

**Step 1 — FC layer parameter count.**

$$P_{\text{FC}} = (7 \cdot 7 \cdot 512 + 1) \cdot 4096 = (25088 + 1) \cdot 4096 = 102{,}760{,}448$$

**Step 2 — Conv layer parameter count.**

$$P_{\text{conv}} = (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 512 + 1) \cdot 512 = (4608 + 1) \cdot 512 = 2{,}359{,}296$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{P_{\text{FC}}}{P_{\text{conv}}} = \frac{102{,}760{,}448}{2{,}359{,}296} \approx 43.6$$

**Answer:** The FC layer has **~43.6× more parameters** than the equivalent $3 \times 3$ conv layer — this is precisely why modern architectures (ResNet, MobileNet) replace large FC heads with **Global Average Pooling**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Intersection over Union (IoU) for Object Detection

**Problem:** A predicted bounding box $B_p$ and the ground-truth box $B_{gt}$ have the following geometry:

- $B_p = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (10, 10, 60, 80)$, so $A_p = (60-10)(80-10) = 3500$.
- $B_{gt} = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (20, 30, 70, 90)$, so $A_{gt} = (70-20)(90-30) = 3000$.

Compute the IoU.

**Step 1 — Find the intersection rectangle.**

$$x_{\text{left}} = \max(10, 20) = 20$$
$$y_{\text{top}} = \max(10, 30) = 30$$
$$x_{\text{right}} = \min(60, 70) = 60$$
$$y_{\text{bot}} = \min(80, 90) = 80$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intersection area.**

$$A_{\cap} = (60 - 20)(80 - 30) = 40 \cdot 50 = 2000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the union area.**

$$A_{\cup} = A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\cap} = 3500 + 3000 - 2000 = 4500$$

**Step 4 — Compute the IoU.**

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{\cap}}{A_{\cup}} = \frac{2000}{4500} = 0.444$$

**Answer:** $\text{IoU} = 0.444$. Since this is *below* the typical threshold of $0.5$, NMS would discard this box as a poor match.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Transposed Convolution Output Size (U-Net Decoder)

**Problem:** In the decoder of a U-Net, a feature map of size $16 \times 16 \times 128$ is upsampled via a transposed convolution with kernel $K = 4$, stride $S = 2$, padding $P = 1$. Compute the output size.

**Step 1 — Recall the transposed-conv formula.**

$$O = (I - 1) \cdot S - 2P + K$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$O = (16 - 1) \cdot 2 - 2(1) + 4 = 15 \cdot 2 - 2 + 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$O = 30 - 2 + 4 = 32$$

**Answer:** The output is $\mathbf{32 \times 32 \times 128}$, exactly *doubling* the spatial resolution — the standard U-Net upsampling pattern.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Triplet Loss Margin Intuition

**Problem:** For a face-recognition embedding of dimension 128, show that enforcing a margin $\alpha = 0.2$ in triplet loss implies that the *positive* pair distance must be at least **0.2 units closer** to the anchor than the *negative* pair.

**Step 1 — Write the loss with the hinge active.**

$$\mathcal{L} = d(A, P) - d(A, N) + \alpha \quad \text{(loss active when } > 0\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — At the equilibrium boundary, $\mathcal{L} = 0$.**

$$d(A, P) - d(A, N) + \alpha = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the distance gap.**

$$d(A, N) - d(A, P) = \alpha = 0.2$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

The negative sample must be at least $\mathbf{0.2}$ *farther* from the anchor than the positive sample, in Euclidean space.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $\alpha$ is treated as a *hyperparameter* tuned on a validation set. Larger $\alpha$ ⇒ stricter clustering.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right CNN for the Right Task

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU answer** for "Compare and contrast" type questions (7-mark sub-parts).

| Application | Preferred Backbone | Preferred Head | Loss Function | Evaluation Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Image Classification** | ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B3 | Global Avg Pool + Softmax | Cross-Entropy | Top-1 / Top-5 Accuracy |
| **Object Detection** | ResNet-50 + FPN | RPN (Faster R-CNN) or Anchor grid (YOLO) | Smooth L1 + CE | mAP @ 0.5:0.95 |
| **Semantic Segmentation** | ResNet-101 (encoder) | FCN, DeepLabv3+ head | Pixel CE + Dice Loss | mIoU, Dice Score |
| **Instance Segmentation** | ResNet-101 + FPN | Mask R-CNN head | CE + Mask BCE + Box L1 | Mask mAP |
| **Face Recognition** | ResNet-100 (ArcFace) | 512-D embedding + ArcFace | ArcFace (angular margin) | TAR @ FAR = $10^{-4}$ |
| **Medical Imaging** | U-Net, ResNet (transfer) | U-Net decoder | Dice + BCE | Dice, Hausdorff Distance |
| **Style Transfer** | VGG-19 (frozen) | Gram-matrix matching | Content + Style MSE | Visual + Content/Style loss |
| **Super-Resolution** | ResNet / EDSR | Pixel-shuffle upsampler | L1 / Perceptual / Adversarial | PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS |
| **OCR (CRNN)** | VGG-style CNN | BiLSTM + CTC | CTC Loss | CER, WER |
| **Action Recognition (Video)** | I3D, SlowFast | 3-D conv + temporal pooling | CE | Top-1 Accuracy |

---

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End Forward Pass

**Problem:** Consider a 3-layer mini-CNN applied to a $32 \times 32 \times 3$ CIFAR-style image:

- **Layer 1:** Conv $5 \times 5$, 16 filters, $S = 1$, $P = 0$, then MaxPool $2 \times 2$.
- **Layer 2:** Conv $3 \times 3$, 32 filters, $S = 1$, $P = 0$, then MaxPool $2 \times 2$.
- **Layer 3:** Fully-Connected $\to 10$ classes.

**Step 1 — Layer 1 output (pre-pool).**

$$O_1 = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 0}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 28$$

**Step 2 — Layer 1 output (post-pool, $2 \times 2$ with stride 2).**

$$O_{1p} = \left\lfloor \frac{28 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 14$$

So after Layer 1: $14 \times 14 \times 16$.

**Step 3 — Layer 2 output (pre-pool).**

$$O_2 = \left\lfloor \frac{14 - 3 + 0}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 12$$

**Step 4 — Layer 2 output (post-pool).**

$$O_{2p} = \left\lfloor \frac{12 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 6$$

So after Layer 2: $6 \times 6 \times 32$.

**Step 5 — Layer 3 flattening + FC.**

Flattened vector length: $6 \times 6 \times 32 = 1152$. FC weights: $1152 \times 10 = 11520$ parameters.

**Step 6 — Total conv parameters.**

$$P_1 = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 16 = 1216$$
$$P_2 = (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 16 + 1) \cdot 32 = 4640$$
$$P_3 = 11520 \cdot 2 \text{ (W and b)} \approx 11530$$

**Total:** $P_{\text{total}} = 1216 + 4640 + 11530 \approx 17{,}386$ trainable parameters.

> [!TIP]
> This worked example illustrates the **scaling law of CNNs**: most parameters live in the *FC head*, not the conv stack — another reason to prefer **Global Average Pooling**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The CNN Application Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CNNApp[CNN Applications] --> Cls[Image Classification]
    CNNApp --> Det[Object Detection]
    CNNApp --> Seg[Semantic Segmentation]
    CNNApp --> ISeg[Instance Segmentation]
    CNNApp --> FR[Face Recognition]
    CNNApp --> Med[Medical Imaging]
    CNNApp --> Sty[Style Transfer]
    CNNApp --> Sup[Super Resolution]
    CNNApp --> OCR[OCR Document AI]
    CNNApp --> Vid[Video Analytics]
    CNNApp --> Gen[Generative Models GANs]

    Cls --> ClsA[ResNet]
    Cls --> ClsB[EfficientNet]
    Cls --> ClsC[Loss Softmax CE]

    Det --> DetA[Two Stage Faster RCNN]
    Det --> DetB[One Stage YOLO SSD]
    Det --> DetC[Loss Smooth L1 CE]
    Det --> DetD[Metric mAP IoU]

    Seg --> SegA[FCN]
    Seg --> SegB[U Net]
    Seg --> SegC[DeepLabv3+]
    Seg --> SegD[Loss Dice CE]

    ISeg --> ISegA[Mask RCNN]
    ISeg --> ISegB[Loss Mask BCE]

    FR --> FRA[FaceNet]
    FR --> FRB[ArcFace]
    FR --> FRC[Loss Triplet ArcFace]

    Med --> MedA[U Net for tumor]
    Med --> MedB[Transfer learning]

    Sty --> StyA[Neural Style Transfer Gatys]
    Sty --> StyB[Loss Content Style Gram]

    Sup --> SupA[SRCNN ESRGAN]

    OCR --> OCRA[CRNN]
    OCR --> OCRB[Loss CTC]

    Vid --> VidA[I3D SlowFast C3D]
    Vid --> VidB[Loss CE]

    Gen --> GenA[DCGAN StyleGAN]
    Gen --> GenB[Loss Adversarial]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Object Detector (Faster R-CNN)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input Image 224x224x3] --> Bn[CNN Backbone ResNet50]
    Bn --> FM[Shared Feature Map]
    FM --> RPN[Region Proposal Network]
    RPN --> PA[Proposed Anchors]
    PA --> RoIP[RoI Pooling Layer]
    FM --> RoIP
    RoIP --> ClsH[Classification Head]
    RoIP --> RegH[Box Regression Head]
    ClsH --> OutC[Class Probabilities]
    RegH --> OutB[Refined Box Coords]
    OutC --> NMS[Non Maximum Suppression]
    OutB --> NMS
    NMS --> Final[Final Detections Boxes Labels Scores]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — U-Net Architecture for Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input --> E1[Encoder Block 1 64 ch]
    E1 --> P1[MaxPool 2x2]
    P1 --> E2[Encoder Block 2 128 ch]
    E2 --> P2[MaxPool 2x2]
    P2 --> E3[Encoder Block 3 256 ch]
    E3 --> P3[MaxPool 2x2]
    P3 --> E4[Encoder Block 4 512 ch]
    E4 --> P4[MaxPool 2x2]
    P4 --> Bottleneck[Bottleneck 1024 ch]
    Bottleneck --> U1[Up Conv 2x2 to 512]
    E4 --> SC1[Skip Connection]
    SC1 --> U1
    U1 --> D1[Decoder Block 1 512 ch]
    D1 --> U2[Up Conv 2x2 to 256]
    E3 --> SC2[Skip Connection]
    SC2 --> U2
    U2 --> D2[Decoder Block 2 256 ch]
    D2 --> U3[Up Conv 2x2 to 128]
    E2 --> SC3[Skip Connection]
    SC3 --> U3
    U3 --> D3[Decoder Block 3 128 ch]
    D3 --> U4[Up Conv 2x2 to 64]
    E1 --> SC4[Skip Connection]
    SC4 --> U4
    U4 --> D4[Decoder Block 4 64 ch]
    D4 --> Out[1x1 Conv Softmax per pixel]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — CNN Transfer Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Pretrain[Pre trained Backbone ImageNet] --> Freeze[Freeze Convolutional Weights]
    Freeze --> Replace[Replace FC Head with Custom Classifier]
    Replace --> FT1[Feature Extraction Phase]
    FT1 --> Unfreeze[Optionally Unfreeze Last Few Layers]
    Unfreeze --> FT2[Fine Tuning Phase Low LR]
    FT2 --> Deploy[Deploy Model on Target Task]
    Target[Small Target Dataset e.g. Medical] --> FT1
    Target --> FT2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*List any three popular CNN architectures used for image classification and state one distinguishing feature of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **LeNet-5 (1998):** One of the earliest CNNs; uses alternating conv + pool layers, designed for $32 \times 32$ digit recognition (MNIST). Distinguishing feature — *introduced the conv–pool–FC template still used today*.
2. **AlexNet (2012):** Deeper (8 layers) than LeNet; uses **ReLU** activation, **dropout**, and **GPU training**. Distinguishing feature — *won ImageNet 2012 with 15.3% top-5 error, more than 10 points better than the runner-up, triggering the deep-learning revolution*.
3. **ResNet (2015):** Introduces **residual (skip) connections** that allow training of networks with 50, 101, or even 152 layers. Distinguishing feature — *solves the vanishing-gradient problem via identity shortcuts, achieving 3.57% top-5 error on ImageNet*.

*(1 mark per correct architecture + feature. Full 3 marks if all three are clear and accurate.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the term "IoU" in the context of object detection. Why is it preferred over simple pixel-wise accuracy for evaluating bounding boxes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Intersection over Union (IoU) is the ratio of the area of overlap between the predicted bounding box $B_p$ and the ground-truth box $B_{gt}$ to the area of their union,

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$$

- **Why preferred (1.5 marks):** Simple pixel-wise accuracy is **scale-sensitive** — a small misaligned box on a large object gives nearly the same pixel accuracy as a perfect match. IoU is **scale-invariant** (a normalized ratio in $[0, 1]$), directly measures *localization quality*, and is the standard threshold for **Non-Maximum Suppression** (typically $\geq 0.5$) and the official COCO detection metric (averaged over $[0.5, 0.95]$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Detection + Segmentation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of the **Faster R-CNN** object detector with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the **YOLO** family of detectors in terms of speed, accuracy, and use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **U-Net** architecture for semantic segmentation. With a suitable diagram, explain the role of **skip connections** and the **Dice Loss** function. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Faster R-CNN vs. YOLO (7 marks)**

**Faster R-CNN Architecture (4 marks):**

- It is a **two-stage detector** introduced by Ren et al. (NeurIPS 2015).
- **Stage 1 — Region Proposal:** A **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** slides a small network over the CNN feature map (typically from a VGG-16 or ResNet-50 backbone). At each spatial location it places $k$ anchor boxes of different scales & aspect ratios and predicts an *objectness score* and *box offsets*.
- **Stage 2 — Detection:** The proposed regions are passed through **RoI Pooling** to obtain fixed-size feature vectors, which are then fed into a classification head (softmax over $K+1$ classes, including background) and a box-regression head (4 box coordinates per class).
- **Loss:** Multi-task loss $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}} + \lambda \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}$.
- **Inference:** Boxes are filtered by an objectness threshold, then refined by **Non-Maximum Suppression** using an IoU threshold (typically 0.7).

**YOLO — You Only Look Once (3 marks):**

- **One-stage detector** by Redmon et al. (CVPR 2016).
- The input image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid; each cell directly predicts $B$ bounding boxes, confidence scores, and class probabilities in a **single forward pass** of a CNN.
- No explicit region-proposal stage → **real-time** inference (45–155 FPS in later versions).
- Slightly **lower accuracy** on small objects than Faster R-CNN, but vastly faster.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Faster R-CNN | YOLO |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Two-stage | One-stage |
| **Speed** | ~7 FPS | 45–155 FPS |
| **mAP (VOC 2007)** | ~78% | ~63% (v1) → 78%+ (v4–v8) |
| **Best For** | High-accuracy offline tasks | Real-time video, autonomous driving |
| **Proposal Mechanism** | RPN | Grid-based direct prediction |

---

**(b) U-Net Architecture (7 marks)**

**Architecture (3.5 marks):**

- U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is a **symmetric encoder–decoder** network designed for biomedical image segmentation.
- **Encoder (Contracting Path):** Repeated $3 \times 3$ convolutions + ReLU, each followed by $2 \times 2$ max-pooling (downsampling). Channel depth doubles at each stage: $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512$.
- **Bottleneck:** Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions at the lowest resolution with 1024 channels.
- **Decoder (Expanding Path):** Each step applies a $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolution** (up-conv) to halve the channel count and double the spatial resolution, followed by two $3 \times 3$ convolutions.

**Skip Connections (2 marks):**

- After each up-conv, the corresponding encoder feature map is **cropped and concatenated** with the decoder feature map.
- The encoder carries *high-resolution spatial information* (edges, textures) but is *semantically weak*; the decoder is *semantically rich* but *spatially coarse*.
- Concatenation allows the decoder to *recover fine-grained localization* lost during pooling — critical for accurate pixel-level masks (e.g., thin vessel boundaries in medical scans).

**Dice Loss (1.5 marks):**

For predicted probability $p_i$ and ground-truth $g_i$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i p_i + \sum_i g_i + \epsilon}$$

- It is **insensitive to class imbalance** (a small tumor occupies few pixels → CE is dominated by background).
- It directly **maximizes the overlap** between prediction and ground truth.
- Combined with **Binary Cross-Entropy** in most production pipelines.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Face Recognition + Style Transfer / Generative**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **FaceNet** architecture for face recognition. Describe the **Triplet Loss** function with a suitable diagram, and explain how it learns discriminative embeddings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Neural Style Transfer** (Gatys et al., 2015). Formulate the content loss, style loss, and total loss. Mention any two CNN applications in the **generative / super-resolution** domain. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) FaceNet + Triplet Loss (7 marks)**

**FaceNet Architecture (3 marks):**

- Proposed by Schroff et al. (CVPR 2015) at Google.
- The input is a face image (e.g., $160 \times 160 \times 3$). It is passed through a deep CNN backbone (FaceNet uses an **Inception-style** network, though later variants use ResNet).
- The CNN is followed by an **L2-normalization** layer that projects features onto the unit hypersphere; the final output is a **128-dimensional embedding** $\mathbf{f}(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{128}$.
- At inference, two faces are compared by computing the **Euclidean distance** between their embeddings; a threshold (typically 1.2) decides match / no-match.

**Triplet Loss (4 marks):**

- A *triplet* consists of three images: an **anchor** $A$, a **positive** $P$ (same identity as $A$), and a **negative** $N$ (different identity).
- The loss enforces that the positive is closer to the anchor than the negative by a margin $\alpha$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \max\left(0, \, \Vert \mathbf{f}(A_i) - \mathbf{f}(P_i) \Vert_2^2 - \Vert \mathbf{f}(A_i) - \mathbf{f}(N_i) \Vert_2^2 + \alpha \right)$$

- **Training trick:** *Hard-positive mining* and *hard-negative mining* — pick triplets where $d(A,P)$ is large and $d(A,N)$ is small, so the network actually learns something non-trivial per batch.
- **Why discriminative:** It shapes the embedding space so that the *geometry reflects identity*: same-person embeddings cluster together, different-person embeddings are pushed apart. The L2-normalization projects all embeddings onto a 128-D sphere, making distances comparable.

**Diagram (textual, for exam):** Draw a circle (the 128-D unit sphere); show two clusters (Anchor + Positive close; Negative far away on opposite side); annotate the margin $\alpha$.

---

**(b) Neural Style Transfer (7 marks)**

**Pipeline (3 marks):**

- Inputs: a **content image** $\vec{c}$ and a **style image** $\vec{s}$.
- A pre-trained VGG-19 is used as a *fixed feature extractor* (no training).
- A **white-noise image** $\vec{x}$ is *initialized* and iteratively *optimized* via gradient descent to minimize a weighted sum of content and style losses.
- At each step, $\vec{x}$ is updated so that its feature responses match the content image at deep layers (content) and the *correlations* between feature responses (Gram matrices) match the style image at shallow layers (style).

**Losses (3 marks):**

- **Content Loss** (typically layer `conv4_2`):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{content}}(\vec{x}, \vec{c}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} (F_{ij}^{l} - P_{ij}^{l})^2$$

where $F^{l}$ and $P^{l}$ are the feature maps of $\vec{x}$ and $\vec{c}$ at layer $l$.

- **Style Loss** (across multiple layers):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}(\vec{x}, \vec{s}) = \sum_{l} w_l \, \frac{1}{4 N_l^2 M_l^2} \sum_{i,j} (G_{ij}^{l} - A_{ij}^{l})^2$$

where $G^{l}_{ij} = \sum_k F^{l}_{ik} F^{l}_{jk}$ is the **Gram matrix** of feature responses (captures *feature co-occurrence*, i.e., texture).

- **Total Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \alpha \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{content}} + \beta \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}$$

The ratio $\alpha / \beta$ controls the *stylization strength* — higher $\beta$ ⇒ more painterly, lower $\beta$ ⇒ more content-faithful.

**Two Generative / Super-Resolution Applications (1 mark):**

1. **SRCNN / ESRGAN** — single-image super-resolution using a deep CNN trained with L1 + perceptual + adversarial loss.
2. **DCGAN / StyleGAN** — generating photorealistic images from random noise using transposed-conv generators and CNN discriminators.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** *semantic* and *instance* segmentation — semantic assigns one class per pixel, instance separates each object.
> 2. **Do not omit the loss function** in any application question — examiners almost always deduct 1–2 marks if the loss is missing.
> 3. **Do not call a CNN "fully connected"** — the weight-sharing property is *the* defining trait.
> 4. **Always specify the kernel size, stride, and padding** in numerical problems — vague answers lose partial marks.
> 5. **Do not forget skip connections in U-Net** — they are the *most-tested* architectural element in the segmentation section.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — memorize before the exam:**

- ✅ CNNs exploit **local connectivity + weight sharing + spatial pooling** for translation-invariant feature learning.
- ✅ Image Classification uses **backbone + GAP + Softmax**, evaluated by **Top-1 / Top-5 Accuracy**.
- ✅ Object Detection is of two types: **Two-Stage (Faster R-CNN, mAP ~78%)** and **One-Stage (YOLO, real-time)**.
- ✅ **mAP @ 0.5:0.95** is the COCO standard detection metric.
- ✅ **IoU** = $|A \cap B| / |A \cup B|$; threshold of **0.5** for NMS, threshold of **0.7** for NMS-discard.
- ✅ **Semantic Segmentation** → FCN, U-Net, DeepLabv3+; loss = **Pixel CE + Dice**; metric = **mIoU**.
- ✅ **Instance Segmentation** → Mask R-CNN = Faster R-CNN + parallel mask branch.
- ✅ **U-Net skip connections** fuse low-level spatial details with high-level semantics — vital for medical imaging.
- ✅ **Face Recognition** uses **metric learning** (Triplet Loss, ArcFace); embedding dimension typically 128–512.
- ✅ **Transfer learning** = freeze pre-trained backbone + replace FC head + (optionally) fine-tune.
- ✅ **Neural Style Transfer** = content loss (deep layer MSE) + style loss (Gram matrices) optimized over a noise image.
- ✅ **GANs** = generator (transposed conv) vs. discriminator (conv classifier), trained in adversarial lockstep.
- ✅ **3-D CNNs (C3D, I3D)** extend 2-D convolutions to the time axis for **video / action recognition**.
- ✅ **CRNN + CTC Loss** is the textbook architecture for **scene text / OCR**.
- ✅ Output-size formula: $O = \lfloor (I - K + 2P)/S \rfloor + 1$ (regular conv), $O = (I-1)S - 2P + K$ (transposed).
- ✅ Parameter count: $P = (K_h K_w C_{\text{in}} + 1) C_{\text{out}}$.
- ✅ **Always mention the loss function** when describing a CNN application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RNN Basics - RNN Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RNN Basics - RNN Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data, such as time series, speech, text, or video, by maintaining an internal **hidden state (memory)** that captures information from previous time steps. Unlike feedforward networks, RNNs apply the **same set of weight matrices recurrently across all time steps**, enabling parameter sharing and temporal dynamic modelling.

Mathematically, an RNN at time step $t$ computes its hidden state and output as:

$$h_t = f_h(W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$
$$y_t = f_y(W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y)$$

where:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the input vector at time $t$
- $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the hidden state (memory) at time $t$
- $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ is the output vector at time $t$
- $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are the **shared weight matrices**
- $b_h, b_y$ are the bias vectors
- $f_h$ and $f_y$ are the activation functions (typically **tanh**, **ReLU**, or **softmax**)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Think of an RNN as a Human Reading a Story**
> Imagine you are reading a novel. To understand the current sentence, you do not just look at the words in front of you — you remember the plot, characters, and events from **previous pages**. Your brain continuously updates a mental "summary" (hidden state) as you read.
> 
> - **Input ($x_t$):** the current word/sentence you are reading
> - **Hidden state ($h_t$):** your evolving mental summary
> - **Output ($y_t$):** your reaction (e.g., emotion, prediction of the next word)
> - **Recurrent loop ($W_{hh}$):** how strongly your past summary influences your current understanding
> 
> An RNN does exactly this with numbers — it **remembers** the past through its hidden state and uses that memory, together with new input, to produce the next output.

---

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of RNN Architecture:**
> 1. **Sequential Processing** — processes inputs one time step at a time
> 2. **Hidden State (Memory)** — $h_t$ acts as a compact summary of all past inputs
> 3. **Parameter Sharing** — the **same weights** $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused at every time step, drastically reducing the number of learnable parameters

### 1.4 Why Vanilla Feedforward Networks Fail for Sequences

A standard **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** assumes:
- Inputs and outputs are **independent** of each other
- Inputs/outputs have **fixed dimensions**

But in **real-world sequential data** (audio, text, ECG signals):
- The current observation **depends on history**
- The sequence length **varies** (variable-length input)

**RNNs solve this** by introducing a recurrent connection (self-loop) that feeds the previous hidden state back into the network, allowing the model to retain temporal context.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of RNN Forward Pass

> [!NOTE]
> The RNN forward pass is executed **sequentially for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$**, where $T$ is the total number of time steps. Below is the structured logic flow.

#### Step 1: Initialization
- Hidden state is initialized to zero: $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (a vector of zeros)
- All weight matrices $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ and biases $b_h, b_y$ are initialized (commonly using **Xavier/Glorot** or **He initialization**).

#### Step 2: Input Processing at Time $t$
- Receive the current input vector $x_t$.
- Compute the **pre-activation** of the hidden state:
  $$z_t = W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

#### Step 3: Apply Hidden Activation
- Apply the non-linear activation $f_h$ (typically **tanh** for stable gradient flow):
  $$h_t = f_h(z_t) = \tanh(z_t)$$
- The **tanh** function squashes the pre-activation to the range $(-1, 1)$.

#### Step 4: Compute Output
- Project the hidden state to the output space:
  $$o_t = W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y$$
- Apply the output activation $f_y$:
  - **Regression:** $f_y$ = identity
  - **Binary classification:** $f_y$ = sigmoid
  - **Multi-class classification:** $f_y$ = softmax

#### Step 5: Recurrence — Pass the Hidden State Forward
- The current hidden state $h_t$ is fed back as input to the next time step, creating the **recurrent loop**.

#### Step 6: Repeat for All Time Steps
- Steps 2–5 are repeated for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$.

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Component

| Component | Why It Exists | How It Works |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{xh}$ | To transform current input into hidden-space representation | Multiplies with $x_t$ to project input features |
| $W_{hh}$ | To propagate past information to current time step | Multiplies with $h_{t-1}$ to inject memory |
| $W_{hy}$ | To map hidden state to desired output (e.g., class probabilities) | Multiplies with $h_t$ to produce prediction |
| $h_{t-1}$ | To maintain context of all previous inputs | Acts as a compressed memory of the past |
| $\tanh$ | To introduce non-linearity and bound activations | Squashes pre-activations to $(-1, 1)$ |
| Parameter sharing | To handle variable-length sequences and reduce parameters | Same $W$ used at every time step |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations for the KTU Board Exam.**

| # | Formula | Description | Shape / Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Hidden state update | $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 2 | $y_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$ | Output prediction | $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ |
| 3 | $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ | Initial hidden state | Vector of zeros |
| 4 | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Gradient accumulation in **BPTT** | Sum over time steps |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial y_t} \cdot W_{hy} + \sum_{t' > t} \frac{\partial L_{t'}}{\partial h_t}$ | Gradient flow through time | Recursive relation |
| 6 | $\text{Loss} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t^{\text{true}} \log(y_t^{\text{pred}})$ | Cross-entropy loss for sequences | Scalar |
| 7 | $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$ | Input-to-hidden weight shape | $m$ = hidden size, $d$ = input size |
| 8 | $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ | Hidden-to-hidden weight shape | Square matrix |
| 9 | $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$ | Hidden-to-output weight shape | $k$ = output size |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications of RNNs

> [!NOTE]
> **Where RNNs Shine in Industry:**
> - **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** machine translation, sentiment analysis, text generation, named entity recognition
> - **Speech Recognition:** converting audio waveforms to text (e.g., Google Voice, Siri)
> - **Time-Series Forecasting:** stock price prediction, weather forecasting, energy load prediction
> - **Music Generation:** composing melodies note-by-note
> - **Video Analysis:** action recognition, frame-by-frame captioning
> - **Bioinformatics:** DNA sequence analysis, protein structure prediction
> - **Medical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG signal classification
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Forward Pass Derivation (Multi-Step Unrolling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the output sequence $y_1, y_2, y_3$ for a 3-time-step RNN given inputs $x_1, x_2, x_3$ and initial state $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

#### Step 0: Setup and Notation
- Hidden size $m = 2$, input size $d = 2$, output size $k = 1$
- Weight matrices (small toy values for illustration):

$$W_{xh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad W_{hh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad W_{hy} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

- Biases: $b_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad b_y = 0.0$
- Input sequence: $x_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad x_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad x_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$
- Hidden activation: $f_h = \tanh$, Output activation: $f_y = \text{identity}$

---

#### Step 1: Compute Hidden State at $t = 1$

Substitute $h_0 = \mathbf{0} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$z_1 = W_{xh} x_1 + W_{hh} h_0 + b_h$$

$$z_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$z_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Apply $\tanh$ element-wise:

$$h_1 = \tanh\begin{pmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \tanh(0.6) \\ \tanh(0.2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5370 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute output at $t = 1$:

$$y_1 = W_{hy} \, h_1 + b_y = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5370 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix} + 0$$

$$y_1 = (0.6)(0.5370) + (0.5)(0.1974) = 0.3222 + 0.0987 = 0.4209$$

---

#### Step 2: Compute Hidden State at $t = 2$

Use $h_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5370 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix}$ and $x_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$z_2 = W_{xh} x_2 + W_{hh} h_1 + b_h$$

$$z_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5370 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$z_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.4 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} (0.3)(0.5370) + (0.1)(0.1974) \\ (0.2)(0.5370) + (0.4)(0.1974) \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$z_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.4 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1611 + 0.0197 \\ 0.1074 + 0.0790 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5808 \\ 0.6864 \end{pmatrix}$$

Apply $\tanh$:

$$h_2 = \tanh\begin{pmatrix} 0.5808 \\ 0.6864 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5231 \\ 0.5972 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute output:

$$y_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5231 \\ 0.5972 \end{pmatrix} = 0.3139 + 0.2986 = 0.6125$$

---

#### Step 3: Compute Hidden State at $t = 3$

Use $h_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5231 \\ 0.5972 \end{pmatrix}$ and $x_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$z_3 = W_{xh} x_3 + W_{hh} h_2 + b_h$$

$$z_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5231 \\ 0.5972 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$z_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1569 + 0.0597 \\ 0.1046 + 0.2389 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$z_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.2166 \\ 0.3435 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0166 \\ 0.9435 \end{pmatrix}$$

Apply $\tanh$:

$$h_3 = \tanh\begin{pmatrix} 1.0166 \\ 0.9435 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7703 \\ 0.7359 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute output:

$$y_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.7703 \\ 0.7359 \end{pmatrix} = 0.4622 + 0.3680 = 0.8302$$

---

#### Step 4: Final Output Sequence

$$\boxed{y_1 = 0.4209, \quad y_2 = 0.6125, \quad y_3 = 0.8302}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice how the output at each time step depends on **all previous inputs** through the recurrent hidden state. The final output $y_3$ is influenced by $x_1, x_2, x_3$ — this is the **memory mechanism** of an RNN in action.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Parameter Sharing Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison of Parameter Count: RNN vs. Unshared Sequence Model**

| Aspect | RNN (Shared Weights) | Sequence Model Without Sharing |
|---|---|---|
| Number of unique weight matrices | **3** ($W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$) | $3T$ (different weights per time step) |
| Total parameters | $O(m^2 + md + mk)$ | $O(T \cdot (m^2 + md + mk))$ |
| Generalization to unseen lengths | **Yes** (same function applied) | No (model is length-specific) |
| Memory footprint | **Low** | High |
| Risk of overfitting | Lower | Higher |
| Implementation in code | A single `for` loop over time | Separate weight per time step |

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Parameter sharing is not just a mathematical elegance — it is the **key enabler** that allows RNNs to generalize across variable-length sequences and train on shorter sequences while being deployed on longer ones.

---

### 3.3 Hidden State Recurrence — Algebraic Unrolling

Starting from the recurrence relation $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$, we can **explicitly unroll** the recursion to expose how $h_3$ depends on all past inputs:

$$h_1 = \tanh(W_{xh} x_1 + W_{hh} h_0 + b_h) = \tanh(W_{xh} x_1 + b_h)$$

$$h_2 = \tanh(W_{xh} x_2 + W_{hh} \cdot \tanh(W_{xh} x_1 + b_h) + b_h)$$

$$h_3 = \tanh\!\Big(W_{xh} x_3 + W_{hh} \cdot \tanh\!\big(W_{xh} x_2 + W_{hh} \cdot \tanh(W_{xh} x_1 + b_h) + b_h\big) + b_h\Big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each $h_t$ is a **nested non-linear function** of all previous inputs $x_1, \ldots, x_t$. The depth of this nesting grows linearly with $T$, which is what gives rise to the **vanishing/exploding gradient problem** in vanilla RNNs — a key motivation for LSTM and GRU architectures (covered later in Module 3).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Folded (Recurrent) Representation of RNN

> [!NOTE]
> The **folded diagram** shows the RNN as a single repeating cell with a self-loop. This is the compact, conceptual view.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT x_t"] --> B["RNN CELL"]
    C["HIDDEN STATE h_t-1"] --> B
    B --> D["OUTPUT y_t"]
    B --> E["HIDDEN STATE h_t"]
    E -.-> C
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Unfolded (Unrolled Through Time) Representation

> [!NOTE]
> The **unfolded diagram** explicitly shows the same cell replicated for each time step, with the hidden state passed from one step to the next. This is the view used for **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    X1["x1"] --> N1["RNN CELL t1"]
    H0["h0 ZERO"] --> N1
    N1 --> Y1["y1"]
    N1 --> H1["h1"]
    X2["x2"] --> N2["RNN CELL t2"]
    H1 --> N2
    N2 --> Y2["y2"]
    N2 --> H2["h2"]
    X3["x3"] --> N3["RNN CELL t3"]
    H2 --> N3
    N3 --> Y3["y3"]
    N3 --> H3["h3"]
    X4["x4"] --> N4["RNN CELL t4"]
    H3 --> N4
    N4 --> Y4["y4"]
    N4 --> H4["h4"]
    style X1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style X2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style X3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style X4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style N1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style N2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style N3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style N4 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style Y1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style Y2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style Y3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style Y4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style H0 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style H2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style H3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style H4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Internal Anatomy of a Single RNN Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram shows the **computational graph inside one RNN cell**, illustrating how the input, previous hidden state, weight matrices, and activation function combine to produce the new hidden state and output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT x_t"] --> M1["MULTIPLY Wxh"]
    M1 --> S1["SUM PLUS b_h"]
    C["HIDDEN h_t-1"] --> M2["MULTIPLY Whh"]
    M2 --> S1
    S1 --> T["TANH ACTIVATION"]
    T --> H["NEW HIDDEN h_t"]
    H --> M3["MULTIPLY Why"]
    M3 --> S2["SUM PLUS b_y"]
    S2 --> O["OUTPUT y_t"]
    H -.-> C
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#e2d5f0,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#e2d5f0,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    style T fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    style O fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of RNN Training Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This high-level block diagram shows the **end-to-end flow** of an RNN during training, including forward pass, loss computation, and BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT SEQUENCE x1 x2 ... xT"] --> B["RNN FORWARD PASS"]
    B --> C["HIDDEN SEQUENCE h1 h2 ... hT"]
    C --> D["OUTPUT PREDICTIONS y1 y2 ... yT"]
    D --> E["LOSS COMPUTATION CROSS ENTROPY"]
    F["GROUND TRUTH LABELS"] --> E
    E --> G["BPTT BACKPROPAGATION THROUGH TIME"]
    G --> H["GRADIENT WRT Wxh Whh Why"]
    H --> I["WEIGHT UPDATE OPTIMIZER ADAM SGD"]
    I --> B
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
```
