<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks per question, expects concise but complete answers (3–5 sentences + equation/diagram).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). What is the role of the hidden state in an RNN?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of neural networks designed for processing sequential data by maintaining an internal **hidden state** that captures information from previous time steps. **(1 mark)**

Unlike feedforward networks, RNNs have **recurrent connections** that allow information to persist across time steps through the hidden state vector $h_t$. **(1 mark)**

**Role of hidden state:** The hidden state $h_t$ acts as a compact **memory** of all past inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_t$. It is updated at each time step as $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$, enabling the network to make predictions that depend on the entire input history. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of parameter sharing in RNNs. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Parameter sharing** refers to the use of the **same weight matrices** ($W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$) at every time step of the RNN. **(1 mark)**

**Importance:**
1. **Variable-length sequences:** Allows the RNN to process input sequences of any length using the same learned function. **(1 mark)**
2. **Reduced parameters:** Drastically reduces the number of learnable parameters from $O(T \cdot m^2)$ to $O(m^2)$, improving memory efficiency and reducing overfitting. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **Generalization:** Enables the model to generalize patterns learned at one time position to other positions in the sequence. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either OR** (internal choice between Q-A and Q-B).

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a basic RNN**. Show both the **folded** and **unfolded (through-time)** representations. Clearly label all inputs, hidden states, outputs, and weight matrices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward pass equations** of an RNN for **3 time steps**. Given:
- $W_{xh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}$, $W_{hh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}$, $W_{hy} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$
- $b_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$, $b_y = 0.0$
- $x_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, $x_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, $x_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$
- $h_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, activation = $\tanh$
- Compute $h_1, h_2, h_3$ and $y_1, y_2, y_3$. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — RNN Architecture Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks)

> **Step 1: Folded Representation (3 marks)**

The **folded RNN** is shown as a single cell with a self-recurrent loop on the hidden state:

```
x_t ──► [ RNN CELL ] ──► y_t
         ▲     │
         │     ▼
         └── h_t
```

- **Input** $x_t$ enters the cell at time $t$
- The cell maintains a **hidden state** $h_t$ that gets fed back to itself at the next time step
- **Output** $y_t$ is produced at each step

> **Step 2: Unfolded Representation (2 marks)**

Unrolling through $T = 3$ time steps reveals the same cell repeated, with the hidden state passed forward:

```
x_1 ──► [RNN] ──► y_1      x_2 ──► [RNN] ──► y_2      x_3 ──► [RNN] ──► y_3
         │ ▲                   │ ▲                   │ ▲
         ▼ │                   ▼ │                   ▼ │
         h_0                    h_1                    h_2
                              (passed)              (passed)
```

> **Step 3: Labeling and Equations (2 marks)**

- **Weight matrices:** $W_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $W_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden), $W_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output)
- **Biases:** $b_h$ (hidden), $b_y$ (output)
- **Key equations:**

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$$
$$y_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$$

**Mark Distribution:** Folded diagram (3) + Unfolded diagram (2) + Labeling + equations (2) = **7 marks**

---

#### Part (b) — Forward Pass Derivation (7 Marks)

> **Time Step $t = 1$ (2 marks)**

$$z_1 = W_{xh} x_1 + W_{hh} h_0 + b_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$h_1 = \tanh\begin{pmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5370 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$y_1 = W_{hy} h_1 = (0.6)(0.5370) + (0.5)(0.1974) = \mathbf{0.4209}$$

> **Time Step $t = 2$ (2.5 marks)**

$$z_2 = W_{xh} x_2 + W_{hh} h_1 + b_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.4 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1808 \\ 0.1864 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5808 \\ 0.6864 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$h_2 = \tanh\begin{pmatrix} 0.5808 \\ 0.6864 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5231 \\ 0.5972 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$y_2 = (0.6)(0.5231) + (0.5)(0.5972) = \mathbf{0.6125}$$

> **Time Step $t = 3$ (2.5 marks)**

$$z_3 = W_{xh} x_3 + W_{hh} h_2 + b_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.2166 \\ 0.3435 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0166 \\ 0.9435 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$h_3 = \tanh\begin{pmatrix} 1.0166 \\ 0.9435 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7703 \\ 0.7359 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$y_3 = (0.6)(0.7703) + (0.5)(0.7359) = \mathbf{0.8302}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{h_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5370 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix}, \quad h_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5231 \\ 0.5972 \end{pmatrix}, \quad h_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7703 \\ 0.7359 \end{pmatrix}}$$

$$\boxed{y_1 = 0.4209, \quad y_2 = 0.6125, \quad y_3 = 0.8302}$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **forward propagation** in a basic RNN with the help of necessary equations. What is the significance of the **activation functions** used? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare RNN with a **feedforward neural network** for sequence processing tasks. Discuss the advantages and limitations of RNNs. Mention **four real-world applications** of RNNs. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Forward Propagation in RNN (7 Marks)

> **Forward Propagation Steps (5 marks):**

The forward propagation in an RNN proceeds sequentially through time:

1. **Initialize** hidden state: $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ **(0.5 marks)**
2. **For each time step** $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:
   - Compute pre-activation: $z_t = W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$ **(1 mark)**
   - Apply hidden activation: $h_t = f_h(z_t)$ **(1 mark)**
   - Compute output: $o_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$ **(1 mark)**
   - Apply output activation: $y_t = f_y(o_t)$ **(1 mark)**

**Key Equations:**

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

$$y_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$$

> **Significance of Activation Functions (2 marks):**

- **$\tanh$ for hidden state:** Squashes activations to $(-1, 1)$, prevents exploding values, provides zero-centered output (better gradient flow than sigmoid). **(1 mark)**
- **Softmax for output (classification):** Converts raw scores into a valid probability distribution summing to 1. **(0.5 marks)**
- **Sigmoid for output (binary):** Maps output to $(0, 1)$ for binary classification. **(0.5 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — RNN vs Feedforward NN, Applications (7 Marks)

> **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Feedforward NN | RNN |
|---|---|---|
| Input type | Fixed-size, independent | Variable-length, sequential |
| Memory | None | Hidden state (memory) |
| Parameter sharing | No (separate per layer) | **Yes** (same $W$ across time) |
| Handles variable length | No | **Yes** |
| Suitable for sequences | Poor | Excellent |
| Computational complexity | Lower (parallel) | Higher (sequential) |
| Training algorithm | Backpropagation | **BPTT** (Backpropagation Through Time) |
| Handles temporal dependencies | No | **Yes** |

> **Advantages of RNNs (1.5 marks):**
- Can process **variable-length** input/output sequences
- **Parameter sharing** reduces memory footprint
- Maintains **memory** of past inputs via hidden state
- Suitable for **temporal/sequential** data

> **Limitations of RNNs (1.5 marks):**
- **Vanishing/Exploding gradients** during BPTT for long sequences
- **Slow training** due to sequential nature (cannot parallelize across time)
- Difficulty capturing **long-range dependencies** (motivates LSTM/GRU)
- Struggles with very long sequences

> **Four Real-World Applications (1 mark – 0.25 each):**
1. **Sentiment Analysis** — classify text as positive/negative
2. **Machine Translation** — English → French sequence-to-sequence
3. **Speech Recognition** — audio waveform → text transcript
4. **Stock Price Prediction** — time-series forecasting

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Don't confuse $W_{hh}$ and $W_{xh}$** — students often interchange them in derivations. $W_{hh}$ multiplies the **previous** hidden state $h_{t-1}$, while $W_{xh}$ multiplies the **current** input $x_t$.
> - **Always initialize $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$** unless specified otherwise. Skipping this initialization loses 1 mark.
> - **Mention BPTT** when discussing RNN training — examiners specifically look for this keyword in 14-mark answers.
> - **Label diagrams completely** — partial labels in the folded/unfolded diagram can cost 1–2 marks.
> - **Show the $\tanh$ application explicitly** in derivations — writing only the linear combination $W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$ without the activation function is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ RNN = neural network with **recurrent connections** for sequential data
- ✅ Core equations: $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ and $y_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$
- ✅ **Hidden state** $h_t$ is the "memory" of all past inputs
- ✅ **Parameter sharing**: same $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ used at every time step
- ✅ **Two views**: **Folded** (compact self-loop) vs **Unfolded** (replicated across time)
- ✅ **Weight matrix shapes**: $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$, $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$, $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$
- ✅ **Default hidden activation** = $\tanh$; **output activation** = softmax (classification) / identity (regression)
- ✅ **Training algorithm** = BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time) — NOT standard backpropagation
- ✅ **Initialization**: $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ unless stated otherwise
- ✅ **Limitation**: vanilla RNNs suffer from **vanishing/exploding gradients** for long sequences → motivates LSTM/GRU
- ✅ **Applications**: NLP, speech recognition, time-series forecasting, music generation, video captioning
- ✅ **Why not feedforward?** FFNNs cannot handle variable-length input or capture temporal dependencies
- ✅ **Keyword for examiners**: always mention **"weight sharing across time steps"** in answers
- ✅ **Diagram must show**: input $x_t$, hidden state $h_t$, output $y_t$, recurrent arrow from $h_t$ to next cell, all three weight matrices labeled
- ✅ **RNN ≠ CNN**: CNNs are for spatial data (images); RNNs are for **temporal/sequential** data
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** is the standard gradient-based learning algorithm used to train **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)**. It is mathematically defined as the application of the standard backpropagation algorithm to an **unrolled computational graph** of an RNN, where the network is treated as a deep feedforward network with one layer per time step, and shared parameters across all temporal layers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the OEITT832 (Deep Learning) syllabus, BPTT operates on three governing principles:

- **Temporal Unrolling:** The recurrent connection $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ is "unrolled" across $T$ time steps to create a static directed acyclic graph (DAG).
- **Parameter Sharing:** The weight matrices $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, and $W_{hy}$ are **tied (shared)** across every unrolled time step.
- **Chain Rule Across Time:** The total gradient with respect to a parameter is the sum of contributions from every time step where that parameter is used.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Time-Traveling Student"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Diary of a Student**
> Imagine a student who keeps a **diary** every day. On Day $t$, the student reads the previous day's diary (hidden state $h_{t-1}$), glances at today's news ($x_t$), and writes a new diary entry ($h_t$) along with a prediction about tomorrow's exam ($y_t$).

- **Forward Pass (Writing the Diary):** The student writes entries day by day, where every new entry depends on the previous one. By the end, the teacher gives a final grade based on all predictions — this is the **loss $L$**.
- **Backward Pass (Blaming the Past):** When the grade is bad, the student must figure out **which past entries were responsible**. To do this, he walks backward, asking: "How much did Day 3's entry affect the final grade? How much did Day 2's entry affect Day 3's entry?" He keeps multiplying these influences backward in time — this multiplication chain is the **chain rule of BPTT**.
- **Problem:** If the student has 365 days of diary, the blame signal either **vanishes (becomes 0)** because each day's influence was small, or **explodes (becomes huge)** because the student amplified his mistakes. This is the famous **Vanishing / Exploding Gradient Problem**.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Terminology

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus-Critical Terminology (Board-Examiner Favorites):**
> - **$T$:** Sequence length / Number of unrolled time steps.
> - **$h_t$:** Hidden state vector at time $t$ (the "memory").
> - **$x_t$:** Input vector at time $t$.
> - **$y_t$:** Output vector at time $t$.
> - **$\eta$:** Learning rate used during weight updates.
> - **$\gamma$:** The spectral radius $\rho$ of the recurrent weight matrix; governs gradient stability ($\gamma < 1$ ⇒ vanishing; $\gamma > 1$ ⇒ exploding).
> - **Truncated BPTT ($k$):** A variant where gradients are back-propagated for only $k$ steps to bound computational cost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of BPTT

The BPTT algorithm proceeds in **four logical phases**. Each phase is essential and frequently tested as a short answer.

### Phase 1 — Network Unrolling
- The RNN is duplicated $T$ times, where $T$ is the length of the input sequence.
- Each copy uses the **same** weight matrices: $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$.
- The unrolled graph is a feedforward DAG suitable for standard backpropagation.

### Phase 2 — Forward Pass Through Time
- For $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, the hidden state is computed sequentially:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

- The output at each step is:

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$$

- The total loss is the sum (or average) of per-step losses:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

### Phase 3 — Backward Pass Through Time
- Starting from $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_T}$, the error is propagated backward in time.
- At each time step $t$, the gradient with respect to the hidden state $\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$ receives contributions from:
  1. The output at time $t$: $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t}$.
  2. The hidden state at time $t+1$: $\frac{\partial L_{t+1,\dots,T}}{\partial h_{t+1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t}$.

### Phase 4 — Parameter Update
- The accumulated gradient w.r.t. each weight matrix is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}$$

- Weights are updated using gradient descent: $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day reference**. Memorize the column headers and the corresponding row symbols — questions are often asked as "Write the loss function for an RNN" or "State the BPTT weight update equation."

| Symbol | Meaning | Associated Equation / Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h_t$ | Hidden state at time $t$ | $h_t = f(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ |
| $\hat{y}_t$ | Predicted output at $t$ | $\hat{y}_t = g(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$ |
| $L$ | Total sequence loss | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ |
| $\delta_t^{(h)}$ | Error signal at hidden layer $t$ | $\delta_t^{(h)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$ |
| $\delta_t^{(y)}$ | Error signal at output layer $t$ | $\delta_t^{(y)} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$ |
| $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Recurrent weight gradient | $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \, h_{t-1}^{\top}$ |
| $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{xh}}$ | Input weight gradient | $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \, x_t^{\top}$ |
| $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}}$ | Output weight gradient | $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(y)} \, h_t^{\top}$ |
| $\rho$ | Spectral radius of $W_{hh}$ | $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1 \Rightarrow$ vanishing; $\rho > 1 \Rightarrow$ exploding |
| $k$ | Truncation window | Limits gradient backflow to last $k$ steps |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition:** Google Assistant, Siri — temporal acoustic features.
- **Financial Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price, sensor telemetry prediction.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Language modeling, machine translation.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG, EEG sequence classification.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy networks in partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU loves this topic:** BPTT bridges the gap between classical feedforward backpropagation (Module 2) and modern sequence models (LSTM/GRU in Module 4). It is the conceptual foundation that makes LSTM gates and gradient-clipping heuristics **meaningful**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup — RNN Forward Equations

Consider a single-layer RNN processing a sequence of length $T$. We define:

- Input at time $t$: $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$
- Hidden state: $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$
- Output: $\hat{y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$

The governing equations are:

$$h_t = f(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

$$\hat{y}_t = g(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$$

where $f$ is typically $\tanh$ and $g$ is the softmax function for classification.

## 3.2 Loss Function Definition

The total loss over the entire sequence is the sum of per-time-step cross-entropy losses:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{t,c} \,\log(\hat{y}_{t,c})$$

**Step 1:** Total loss is **additive over time** because each prediction $\hat{y}_t$ depends on $h_t$, which itself depends on the past — so the per-step losses are coupled.

## 3.3 Gradient w.r.t. Output Weights $W_{hy}$ (Direct Path)

The output at time $t$ depends on $W_{hy}$ only through $h_t$, **not through any future state**. Hence the gradient is local in time:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial (W_{hy} h_t)} \cdot \frac{\partial (W_{hy} h_t)}{\partial W_{hy}}$$

**Step 2:** Using the property of cross-entropy + softmax, $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial z_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = (\hat{y}_t - y_t)\, h_t^{\top}$$

**Step 3:** Summing over all time steps:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat{y}_t - y_t)\, h_t^{\top} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(y)} \, h_t^{\top}$$

This is the **cleanest** of the three gradients — KTU examiners love to ask this derivation for full marks.

## 3.4 Gradient w.r.t. Recurrent Weights $W_{hh}$ (The "Through Time" Path)

The difficulty arises because $W_{hh}$ influences $h_t$ **and** every future hidden state $h_{t+1}, h_{t+2}, \dots, h_T$ via the recurrent connection.

**Step 4:** Apply the **multivariate chain rule** by treating $L$ as a function of every hidden state:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the hidden-state derivative using the chain rule across time. The key identity is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \left(\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}\right) \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

Substituting the RNN equation $h_j = f(W_{xh} x_j + W_{hh} h_{j-1} + b_h)$, the Jacobian is:

$$\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(f'(W_{xh} x_j + W_{hh} h_{j-1} + b_h)\right) W_{hh}$$

**Step 6:** Combining, we obtain the full BPTT gradient for the recurrent weight:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \left(\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(f'(z_j)\right) W_{hh}\right) \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

where $z_j = W_{xh} x_j + W_{hh} h_{j-1} + b_h$.

**Step 7 (Compact form):** Define the back-propagated error at hidden state $t$:

$$\delta_t^{(h)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + W_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}\!\left(f'(z_{t+1})\right)\, \delta_{t+1}^{(h)}$$

This recursive equation (evaluated backward from $t = T$ to $t = 1$) is the **heart of BPTT**. The compact BPTT weight gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \, h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

## 3.5 Gradient w.r.t. Input Weights $W_{xh}$

By symmetry with the $W_{hh}$ derivation, the input weight depends on $x_t$ at every time step:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{xh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \, x_t^{\top}$$

**Step 8 (Final Update):** Once all gradients are accumulated, gradient descent is applied with learning rate $\eta$:

$$W_{hh} \leftarrow W_{hh} - \eta\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

$$W_{xh} \leftarrow W_{xh} - \eta\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{xh}}$$

$$W_{hy} \leftarrow W_{hy} - \eta\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}}$$

## 3.6 Why Gradients Vanish or Explode — Analytical Justification

**Step 9:** Consider the term $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f'(z_j)) W_{hh}$ appearing in the BPTT gradient. Assume $f = \tanh$, so $\vert f'(\cdot) \vert \leq 1$. Let $\rho$ be the spectral radius (largest absolute eigenvalue) of $W_{hh}$. Then:

$$\left\Vert \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f'(z_j)) W_{hh} \right\Vert \leq \rho^{\,(t-k)}$$

- If $\rho < 1$: as $(t-k) \to \infty$, this term $\to 0$ ⇒ **vanishing gradient**.
- If $\rho > 1$: this term $\to \infty$ ⇒ **exploding gradient**.

This analytical bound is the **canonical justification** and is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU Module 3.

## 3.7 Truncated BPTT (TBPTT)

To bound computational cost and mitigate exploding gradients, TBPTT cuts the backward pass to a window of $k$ steps:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} \approx \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=\max(1, t-\tau)}^{t} \delta_t^{(h)} \, h_{k-1}^{\top}$$

where $\tau$ is the truncation horizon. This is the practical algorithm used in real systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common student mistake is to confuse **truncation** (stopping backprop after $k$ steps) with **truncating the forward pass** (limiting sequence length). TBPTT **truncates the backward pass only**; the forward pass still uses the full sequence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Unrolled RNN with BPTT Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input x1] --> B1[Hidden h1]
    A2[Input x2] --> B2[Hidden h2]
    A3[Input x3] --> B3[Hidden h3]
    A4[Input x4] --> B4[Hidden h4]
    A5[Input x5] --> B5[Hidden h5]

    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5

    B1 --> C1[Output y1]
    B2 --> C2[Output y2]
    B3 --> C3[Output y3]
    B4 --> C4[Output y4]
    B5 --> C5[Output y5]

    C1 --> D1[Loss L1]
    C2 --> D2[Loss L2]
    C3 --> D3[Loss L3]
    C4 --> D4[Loss L4]
    C5 --> D5[Loss L5]

    D1 --> E[Total Loss L Sum]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    E --> F[Backward Pass BPTT]
    F --> G[Gradient dL by dWh]
    F --> H[Gradient dL by dWx]
    F --> I[Gradient dL by dWy]
    G --> J[Weight Update W new]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — BPTT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Phase 1 Unroll RNN over T steps] --> S2[Phase 2 Forward Pass compute ht and yt for t in 1 to T]
    S2 --> S3[Phase 3 Compute Total Loss L sum of Lt]
    S3 --> S4[Phase 4 Backward Pass propagate delta t backward from T to 1]
    S4 --> S5[Phase 5 Accumulate Gradients sum over all t]
    S5 --> S6[Phase 6 Update Weights using Gradient Descent]
    S6 --> S7{Convergence Check Loss stable}
    S7 -- No --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Trained RNN Model]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Recurrent Weight Gradient Flow (Cause of Vanishing/Explosion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Time t equals 1] -->|W_hh product| N2[Time t equals 2]
    N2 -->|W_hh product| N3[Time t equals 3]
    N3 -->|W_hh product| N4[Time t equals T]

    N1 --> P1[Gradient at t equals 1]
    N2 --> P2[Gradient at t equals 2]
    N3 --> P3[Gradient at t equals 3]
    N4 --> P4[Gradient at t equals T]

    P4 --> Q[Product of T factors of W_hh]
    Q --> R{Spectral Radius rho of W_hh}
    R -- rho less than 1 --> S[Vanishing Gradient]
    R -- rho greater than 1 --> T[Exploding Gradient]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). List any two challenges associated with training RNNs using BPTT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the training algorithm for Recurrent Neural Networks in which the recurrent network is unrolled across $T$ time steps into a feedforward computational graph, and standard backpropagation is applied on this unrolled graph to compute gradients of the loss with respect to the shared parameters $W_{hh}$, $W_{xh}$, and $W_{hy}$.

> **Two Challenges (1 mark):**
> 1. **Vanishing Gradient Problem** — when the spectral radius of $W_{hh}$ is less than 1, gradients shrink exponentially as they propagate backward in time, preventing the network from learning long-range dependencies.
> 2. **Exploding Gradient Problem** — when the spectral radius exceeds 1, gradients grow exponentially, causing unstable weight updates and divergence.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "unrolling" an RNN. Why is parameter sharing important in BPTT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Unrolling (2 marks):** Unrolling is the process of converting a recurrent neural network's recurrent loop into a sequence of $T$ identical feedforward layers, where layer $t$ represents the network at time step $t$. This converts the cyclic graph into a directed acyclic graph (DAG), enabling the use of standard backpropagation.

> **Parameter Sharing (1 mark):** Parameter sharing is important because the same weight matrices $W_{hh}$, $W_{xh}$, and $W_{hy}$ are used at every time step. This reduces the total number of learnable parameters, makes the model generalize across all positions in the sequence, and ensures that the gradient is the sum of contributions from all time steps: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}$.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) update rule for the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ in a simple RNN. Clearly state the forward equations, loss function, and show all intermediate gradient expressions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable mathematical justification, explain the **Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Problem** in BPTT. Discuss two techniques used to mitigate these problems. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution (a) — Derivation of BPTT Update for $W_{hh}$ (7 marks)**

**Forward Equations:**

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$$

**Loss Function:**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

**Step 1 — Total gradient by chain rule:** Apply the multivariate chain rule, summing contributions from every time step where $W_{hh}$ is used:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

**Step 2 — Back-propagated error $\delta_t^{(h)}$:** The error at hidden state $t$ has two sources — direct contribution from output at $t$ and contribution propagated from $t+1$:

$$\delta_t^{(h)} = (\hat{y}_t - y_t)^{\top} W_{hy} + W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}\!\left(1 - h_{t+1}^{2}\right) \delta_{t+1}^{(h)}$$

where $\delta_T^{(h)} = (\hat{y}_T - y_T)^{\top} W_{hy}$ (terminal condition).

**Step 3 — Local gradient w.r.t. $W_{hh}$:** Since $h_t$ depends on $W_{hh}$ via $W_{hh}\, h_{t-1}$:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - h_t^{2}\right)\, h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

**Step 4 — Final BPTT gradient:** Combining:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

**Step 5 — Weight update (Gradient Descent):**

$$W_{hh} \leftarrow W_{hh} - \eta\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

> All 5 steps earn full 7 marks; the **terminal condition** in Step 2 is the most commonly missed item.

#### **Solution (b) — Vanishing/Exploding Gradient & Mitigation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall the Jacobian:** The Jacobian of the hidden state transition is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - h_t^{2}\right) W_{hh}$$

**Step 2 — Bound the norm:** For $\tanh$, $\vert 1 - h_t^{2} \vert \leq 1$. Let $\rho$ be the spectral radius of $W_{hh}$. Then:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{k}} \right\Vert \leq \rho^{\,(t-k)}$$

**Step 3 — Vanishing case ($\rho < 1$):** As $(t-k) \to \infty$, the gradient norm $\to 0$. Early time steps receive negligible gradient updates, so the RNN **fails to learn long-range dependencies**.

**Step 4 — Exploding case ($\rho > 1$):** As $(t-k) \to \infty$, the gradient norm $\to \infty$. Weight updates oscillate or diverge, and the loss becomes NaN.

**Step 5 — Mitigation Technique 1 (Gradient Clipping):** Rescale gradients whose norm exceeds a threshold $\theta$:

$$g \leftarrow \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \vert\vert g \vert\vert \leq \theta \\ \dfrac{\theta\, g}{\vert\vert g \vert\vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Mitigation Technique 2 (Orthogonal / Identity Initialization):** Initialize $W_{hh}$ as an orthogonal matrix so that $\rho(W_{hh}) = 1$, balancing the gradient flow.

**Step 7 — Optional: Gated Architectures** (LSTM/GRU) directly address vanishing gradients by introducing additive memory cells with controlled gating.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four operational phases of the BPTT algorithm. For each phase, identify the mathematical operation performed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Truncated BPTT (TBPTT)? Derive the modified gradient expression and discuss the trade-off between truncation window $k$ and the network's ability to learn long-range dependencies. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution (a) — Four Phases of BPTT (7 marks)**

**Phase 1 — Unrolling (1 mark):** Duplicate the RNN into $T$ feedforward copies; create a DAG.

**Phase 2 — Forward Pass (2 marks):** For $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, compute sequentially:

$$h_t = f(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h), \quad \hat{y}_t = g(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$$

**Phase 3 — Backward Pass (2 marks):** Compute $\delta_T^{(h)}$, then recursively propagate backward using:

$$\delta_t^{(h)} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}\!\left(f'(z_{t+1})\right)\, \delta_{t+1}^{(h)}$$

**Phase 4 — Parameter Update (2 marks):** Accumulate gradients across all time steps and apply gradient descent:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}, \qquad W \leftarrow W - \eta\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$$

#### **Solution (b) — Truncated BPTT (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Truncated BPTT limits the backward pass to a window of $k$ previous time steps, reducing computational cost from $O(T)$ to $O(k)$ per update.

**Step 2 — Modified Recursion (2 marks):** The back-propagated error becomes:

$$\delta_t^{(h,\,k)} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}\!\left(f'(z_{t+1})\right)\, \delta_{t+1}^{(h,\,k)}$$

with the boundary condition $\delta_{t+k}^{(h,\,k)} = 0$ for all $t$ such that $t + k > T$.

**Step 3 — Modified Gradient (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} \approx \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{j=\max(1,\,t-k+1)}^{t} \delta_t^{(h)} \, h_{j-1}^{\top}$$

**Step 4 — Trade-off (2 marks):**
- **Small $k$** (e.g., 5): Fast training, but the network **cannot capture dependencies longer than $k$ steps** — it behaves like a short-memory model.
- **Large $k$** (close to $T$): Closer to full BPTT, captures long-range dependencies, but **computational cost grows linearly** with $k$ and **vanishing/exploding gradients re-emerge**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TBPTT with sequence truncation:** TBPTT truncates the *gradient flow*, not the input sequence. Writing "TBPTT limits the input length" will cost full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the terminal condition** $\delta_T^{(h)} = (\hat{y}_T - y_T)^{\top} W_{hy}$ in full BPTT — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if omitted.
> 3. **Stating "$\rho = 1$" without justification:** Examiners want the spectral radius bound $\rho^{t-k} \to 0$ or $\infty$ as the **mathematical** reason for vanishing/exploding gradients.
> 4. **Mixing up $\delta_t^{(h)}$ and $\delta_t^{(y)}$:** $\delta_t^{(h)}$ is for the *hidden* layer (involves $W_{hh}^{\top}$ back-propagation), $\delta_t^{(y)}$ is for the *output* layer (a direct difference $\hat{y}_t - y_t$).
> 5. **Not mentioning parameter sharing:** Even if you derive the gradient correctly, omitting that $W_{hh}$ is the *same* matrix at every time step loses a mark.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BPTT = Backpropagation on an unrolled RNN** — the unrolling converts recurrence into a feedforward DAG of depth $T$.
- **Three parameter sets to learn:** $W_{xh}$ (input), $W_{hh}$ (recurrent), $W_{hy}$ (output). All are **shared** across time.
- **Forward pass is sequential** ($h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$); **backward pass is also sequential** ($\delta_t^{(h)}$ depends on $\delta_{t+1}^{(h)}$).
- **Total gradient = sum over time:** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}$.
- **Output weight gradient is the simplest:** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat{y}_t - y_t)\, h_t^{\top}$.
- **Vanishing/Exploding gradient root cause:** the term $\rho^{t-k}$ where $\rho$ is the spectral radius of $W_{hh}$.
- **Two standard fixes:** gradient clipping (for explosion) and orthogonal initialization / LSTM (for vanishing).
- **TBPTT** truncates the backward pass to $k$ steps — fast but loses long-range credit assignment.
- **Computational cost of full BPTT:** $O(T)$ per weight update, which is why TBPTT is used in practice.
- **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** "**U**nroll, **F**orward, **B**ackward, **U**pdate" — the four phases of BPTT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vanishing and Exploding Gradients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vanishing and Exploding Gradients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Deep Learning** and the training of deep neural networks (DNNs) via **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** and standard **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**, the **Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Problem** is a fundamental pathological phenomenon where the partial derivatives (gradients) of the loss function with respect to the network parameters either decay exponentially toward zero (vanishing) or grow exponentially toward infinity (exploding) as they are propagated backward from the output layer to the earlier (input) layers of the network.

Mathematically, during the backward pass, the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to an early weight $W^{(l)}$ (where $l$ is a shallow layer) is computed via the chain rule as:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The vanishing/exploding gradient problem occurs primarily in deep networks and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) when **repeated multiplication of Jacobians** during backpropagation causes the gradient signal to either shrink to zero or blow up to infinity, halting effective learning in shallow layers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whispering in a Tunnel" Metaphor

Imagine you are standing at the end of a long, winding tunnel (the **output layer**) and you need to pass a message (the **error signal / gradient**) back to a friend standing 100 meters away at the tunnel entrance (the **input layer**). 

- **Vanishing Gradient**: Every 10 meters, a tunnel wall absorbs **95% of your voice's energy**. By the time your message travels 100 meters, your friend hears absolute silence. The friend (the early layer) receives **no learning signal** and never updates their weights. 

- **Exploding Gradient**: Conversely, imagine a powerful public address (PA) system in the tunnel that **amplifies your voice 50 times at every wall**. By the time the signal reaches the entrance, the resulting sound is so deafening (numerically infinite / `NaN`) that it destroys the eardrums (causes **weight overflow**, `Inf` values, and numerical instability).

In both cases, the **message fails to reach its destination usefully**, and the network cannot learn long-range dependencies or train deep architectures reliably.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The root cause is **multiplicative compounding**. When the per-layer Jacobian magnitude $\vert \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \vert$ is consistently less than **1.0**, the product collapses to zero. When consistently greater than **1.0**, the product diverges to infinity. This is strictly an architectural instability of gradient-based learning in deep networks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Gradient Norm Threshold:** The **L2 norm** $\vert \vert \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L} \vert \vert_2$ is used to monitor gradient health.
- **Spectral Radius ($\rho$):** The largest absolute eigenvalue of the recurrent weight matrix; the dominant control parameter for stability in RNNs.
- **Effective Gradient Range:** For stable training, gradients should ideally remain in the order of magnitude $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Pathology Breakdown

The vanishing and exploding gradient problem manifests in two distinct mathematical regimes, both rooted in the **Chain Rule of Calculus** applied across deep/long architectures.

### 2.1.1 The Vanishing Gradient Regime

- **Definition:** Gradients shrink to an extremely small magnitude (e.g., $10^{-9}$ or smaller), causing weight updates $\Delta W = -\eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$ to become effectively **zero**.
- **Root Cause 1 — Activation Function Saturation:** Functions like the **Sigmoid** $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ and **Tanh** have derivatives bounded by:
  
  $$\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x)) \leq \frac{1}{4} \approx 0.25$$
  
  $$\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x) \leq 1$$

  When these derivatives are repeatedly multiplied across $L$ layers, the gradient magnitude scales as $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^L$, which for $L=10$ gives $\approx 9.1 \times 10^{-7}$.
  
- **Root Cause 2 — Poor Weight Initialization:** Initializing weights with values that are systematically too small (e.g., $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.01)$) compounds the multiplicative shrinkage.

### 2.1.2 The Exploding Gradient Regime

- **Definition:** Gradients grow uncontrollably large (e.g., $10^{3}$ or larger), causing weight updates to overshoot minima and leading to numerical `NaN`/`Inf` values.
- **Root Cause 1 — Large Recurrent Weights in RNNs:** When the spectral radius $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, the hidden state propagation $h^{(t)} = \tanh(W_{hh} h^{(t-1)} + W_{xh} x^{(t)})$ amplifies signals at every time step.
- **Root Cause 2 — Deep Feedforward Networks:** Without normalization, repeated matrix multiplications amplify the singular values of the input.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas for examination purposes. **Note:** Vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Critical Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Chain Rule Gradient (Deep Net) | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \prod_{l=2}^{L} \frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial a^{(l-1)}} \frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}}$ | Product of $L-1$ Jacobians | Foundation of backpropagation |
| 2 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ | $\max \sigma'(x) = 0.25$ | Bounds vanishing rate |
| 3 | Tanh Derivative | $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$ | $\max \tanh'(x) = 1.0$ at $x=0$ | Better than sigmoid |
| 4 | ReLU Derivative | $\text{ReLU}'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | Constant; no vanishing when active | Default activation choice |
| 5 | Gradient Clipping (Exploding Fix) | $g \leftarrow \frac{g \cdot \tau}{\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2}$ if $\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 > \tau$ | Norm threshold $\tau$ (e.g., 5.0) | RNN training stability |
| 6 | Xavier Initialization | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}\right)$ | Maintains variance $\approx 1$ | Prevents vanishing/exploding |
| 7 | He Initialization | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{2}{n_{in}}\right)$ | Optimized for ReLU | Default for ReLU networks |
| 8 | Batch Norm Normalization | $\hat{x} = \frac{x - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \epsilon}}$ | $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ (typical) | Stabilizes activations |
| 9 | LSTM Gate (Forget) | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Output $\in [0,1]$ | Mitigates vanishing in RNNs |
| 10 | Residual Connection | $a^{(l+1)} = \mathcal{F}(a^{(l)}) + a^{(l)}$ | Skip gradient pathway | Enables very deep nets |

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Long Sequence Modeling (NLP, Time-Series):** RNNs/LSTMs without gradient control fail to learn dependencies beyond $\approx 10$ time steps.
- **Computer Vision (Very Deep CNNs):** ResNet-152, EfficientNet require skip connections specifically to combat vanishing gradients in 100+ layer architectures.
- **Generative AI (Transformers, Diffusion Models):** Deep Transformers (GPT-4, BERT) rely on **Layer Normalization** and **residual streams** to train stably.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods suffer from vanishing/exploding rewards in long-horizon tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Magnitude Through an $L$-Layer Sigmoid Network

We derive analytically why a stack of sigmoid activations causes the gradient to vanish exponentially with depth.

**Setup:** Consider a fully-connected feedforward network of depth $L$ where every hidden layer uses the sigmoid activation. The forward propagation rule is:

$$a^{(l+1)} = \sigma(z^{(l+1)}) = \sigma(W^{(l+1)} a^{(l)} + b^{(l+1)})$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Chain Rule for the Output Layer Gradient:**

By the backpropagation chain rule, the gradient of loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the weight $W^{(1)}$ of the first layer is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L-1)}}{\partial a^{(L-2)}} \cdots \frac{\partial a^{(2)}}{\partial a^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}}$$

**Step 2 — Expand Each Local Derivative Using the Sigmoid Identity:**

For any layer $l$, by the chain rule applied to $a^{(l+1)} = \sigma(W^{(l+1)} a^{(l)} + b^{(l+1)})$:

$$\frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(z^{(l+1)})) \cdot W^{(l+1)}$$

Substituting the derivative of the sigmoid $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$\frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} = \text{diag}(\sigma(z^{(l+1)})(1-\sigma(z^{(l+1)}))) \cdot W^{(l+1)}$$

**Step 3 — Bound the Magnitude of Each Local Derivative:**

Taking the absolute value of each factor, we use two key bounds:

- Sigmoid output is bounded: $0 < \sigma(z) < 1$, hence $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \leq \frac{1}{4}$ (peak at $z=0$).
- Weight matrix norm: For Xavier-initialized weights, $\vert \vert W^{(l)} \vert \vert_2 \leq 1$ approximately.

Thus, the **magnitude** of the local derivative is bounded by:

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \right\vert \leq \frac{1}{4} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the Bounds Across All Layers:**

The full product of the $L-1$ local Jacobians is bounded by:

$$\left\vert \prod_{l=1}^{L-1} \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \right\vert \leq \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-1}$$

Therefore, the **complete gradient magnitude** satisfies:

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} \right\vert \leq \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-1} \cdot \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \right\vert \cdot \left\vert \frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}} \right\vert$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification:**

| Network Depth $L$ | Upper Bound on Gradient | Practical Implication |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 5 | $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{4} = 3.9 \times 10^{-3}$ | Slow but possible learning |
| 10 | $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{9} = 3.7 \times 10^{-6}$ | Severe vanishing |
| 20 | $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{19} = 3.6 \times 10^{-12}$ | Effectively zero updates |
| 50 | $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{49} \approx 10^{-30}$ | Total training failure |

This is the **vanishing gradient** pathology in its full rigor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Exploding Gradient in Vanilla RNNs

**Setup:** A vanilla RNN has the recurrence relation:

$$h^{(t)} = \tanh(W_{hh} h^{(t-1)} + W_{xh} x^{(t)} + b)$$

**Step 1 — Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) Loss Gradient:**

The gradient of the loss at time $T$ with respect to a hidden state at time $t < T$ requires unrolling:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T)}} \cdot \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Local Recurrent Jacobian:**

Differentiating $h^{(k+1)} = \tanh(W_{hh} h^{(k)} + W_{xh} x^{(k+1)} + b)$ with respect to $h^{(k)}$:

$$\frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z^{(k+1)})\right) \cdot W_{hh}$$

where $z^{(k+1)} = W_{hh} h^{(k)} + W_{xh} x^{(k+1)} + b$.

**Step 3 — Take the Norm and Apply the Spectral Radius Bound:**

The L2 norm of the Jacobian is bounded by:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq \vert \vert \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)) \vert \vert_2 \cdot \vert \vert W_{hh} \vert \vert_2$$

Since the diagonal term has entries in $(0, 1]$:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq \rho(W_{hh})$$

where $\rho(W_{hh}) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i \vert$ is the **spectral radius** (largest absolute eigenvalue).

**Step 4 — Compound Across $T - t$ Time Steps:**

The total gradient norm is bounded by:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq \left[\rho(W_{hh})\right]^{T-t} \cdot \left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2$$

**Step 5 — Critical Stability Condition:**

| Spectral Radius $\rho(W_{hh})$ | Behavior | Time-Horizon |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$ | Vanishing gradients | Short-term learning only |
| $\rho(W_{hh}) = 1$ | Ideal (but unstable) | Marginal |
| $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$ | **Exploding gradients** | Causes `NaN` loss |

This explains why **Truncated BPTT**, **Gradient Clipping**, and **LSTM gating** were developed.

## 3.3 Derivation: Gradient Clipping Formula

To bound exploding gradients, Pascanu et al. (2013) proposed clipping when the gradient norm exceeds a threshold $\tau$:

**Step 1 — Compute the unclipped gradient norm:**

$$g = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}, \quad \vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_i g_i^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the clipping operation:**

$$g_{\text{clipped}} = \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 \leq \tau \\[4pt] \dfrac{\tau \cdot g}{\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2} & \text{if } \vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 > \tau \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Rescaled update rule:**

The weight update becomes:

$$W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot g_{\text{clipped}}$$

This guarantees $\vert \vert g_{\text{clipped}} \vert \vert_2 \leq \tau$ always, eliminating the overflow risk.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gradient Flow Block Diagram (Vanishing vs. Exploding)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input Layer X"] --> nodeB["Hidden Layer 1: sigma / tanh"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Hidden Layer 2: sigma / tanh"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Hidden Layer 3: sigma / tanh"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Hidden Layer L-1: sigma / tanh"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Output Layer Y"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Loss Function L"]
    
    nodeG --> nodeH["Backward Gradient Flow"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Per-Layer Jacobian Norm"}
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Norm less than 1.0"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Norm greater than 1.0"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["GRADIENT VANISHES: Approaches 0"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["GRADIENT EXPLODES: Approaches Infinity / NaN"]
```

## 4.2 Residual Connection Architecture (Solution to Vanishing Gradients)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input a to the power l"] --> nodeB["Weight Layer: Conv or FC"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Batch Normalization"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ReLU Activation"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Weight Layer: Conv or FC"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Batch Normalization"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Identity Skip Connection"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Element-wise Addition: F a to the power l + a to the power l"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["ReLU Activation"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Output a to the power l plus 1"]
```

## 4.3 LSTM Cell — Mitigating Vanishing Gradients in RNNs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input x to the power t"] --> nodeB["Forget Gate f: sigmoid"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Input Gate i: sigmoid"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Candidate Cell tilde C: tanh"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Output Gate o: sigmoid"]
    
    nodeB --> nodeF["Cell State C to the power t minus 1"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Element-wise Multiply"]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH["Element-wise Multiply"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Add: New Cell State C to the power t"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["tanh: Hidden Output"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["Element-wise Multiply"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Output h to the power t"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Insight:** The forget gate $f_t$ in the LSTM performs an **additive** (not multiplicative) update to the cell state. This means the gradient can flow through long sequences with a near-constant magnitude, because the cell state acts as a **gradient highway** analogous to the residual connection in ResNets.

## 4.4 Gradient Clipping Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Compute Gradient g"] --> nodeB["Calculate L2 Norm: norm of g"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Is norm of g greater than threshold tau?"}
    nodeC --> nodeD["NO: Use g as is"] --> nodeF["Apply Weight Update W minus eta times g"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["YES: Rescale g to g times tau divided by norm of g"] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Network Parameters Updated Safely"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the vanishing gradient problem in deep neural networks. Why does it primarily affect sigmoid-based architectures?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The vanishing gradient problem is a pathological condition in which the partial derivatives of the loss function with respect to the early-layer weights become extremely small (approaching zero) during backpropagation, preventing those layers from learning effectively.
- **(1 Mark)** It occurs due to the repeated multiplication of small-valued Jacobian terms across many layers (the chain rule). When the per-layer derivative magnitude is less than 1, the product shrinks exponentially with depth.
- **(1 Mark)** The sigmoid activation function $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ has a maximum derivative of only $\frac{1}{4}$. For an $L$-layer network, the gradient magnitude scales as $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-1}$, leading to $\approx 10^{-7}$ for just 10 layers. This makes the sigmoid particularly susceptible to vanishing gradients compared to ReLU (which has a constant derivative of 1 for positive inputs).

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List and briefly explain any three techniques used to mitigate the exploding gradient problem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** **Gradient Clipping:** When the L2 norm of the gradient exceeds a threshold $\tau$, rescale the gradient to $\frac{\tau \cdot g}{\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2}$. This bounds updates and prevents overflow.
- **(1 Mark)** **Proper Weight Initialization (Xavier/He):** Initializing weights with a variance scaled by the layer width ($\frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$ for Xavier, $\frac{2}{n_{in}}$ for He) keeps the signal variance stable across layers.
- **(1 Mark)** **Use of LSTM/GRU Cells:** These gated architectures employ additive cell-state updates, allowing gradients to flow across long time sequences without multiplicative decay.

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse vanishing with regularization.** Vanishing gradient is an *optimization failure*, not a generalization mechanism.
> 2. **Always state the bound explicitly** when asked "why does gradient vanish." Writing only "because of chain rule" is worth only 1 mark out of 7.
> 3. **For exploding gradient derivations,** you must mention the **spectral radius $\rho(W_{hh})$** of the recurrent weight matrix — this is the most-missed high-value term.
> 4. **Show numerical examples.** Examiners reward substituting $L=10$ or $L=20$ into the bound to demonstrate exponential decay.
> 5. **Distinguish ReLU from sigmoid derivatives** in any mitigation question — generic "use better activation" answers are penalized.

---

### **Part B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question: (a)** Explain in detail the vanishing gradient problem with reference to the chain rule of calculus. Derive an upper bound for the gradient magnitude in an $L$-layer sigmoid network. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of ReLU activation, Batch Normalization, and Residual Connections as mitigation strategies for the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 Mark):**
The vanishing gradient problem occurs during backpropagation in deep networks when the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to early-layer weights $W^{(1)}$ becomes negligibly small, halting learning in those layers.

**Step 2 — Chain Rule Formulation (2 Marks):**
By the chain rule, the gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \prod_{l=1}^{L-1} \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}}$$

The product term contains $L-1$ local Jacobians.

**Step 3 — Bounding the Sigmoid Derivative (2 Marks):**
For layer $l$, $\frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} = \text{diag}(\sigma'(z^{(l+1)})) W^{(l+1)}$.
Since $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \leq \frac{1}{4}$ and $\vert \vert W^{(l+1)} \vert \vert_2 \leq 1$ (with Xavier init), we have:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Final Bound (2 Marks):**
Multiplying across all $L-1$ layers:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-1} \left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial a^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2$$

For $L=20$, this gives an upper bound of $\approx 10^{-12}$, demonstrating severe vanishing.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — ReLU Activation (2 Marks):**
The ReLU function $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ has derivative:

$$\text{ReLU}'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

When active, the derivative is exactly **1**, preventing the multiplicative decay seen with sigmoid. However, it introduces the "dying ReLU" problem (zero gradient for negative inputs), addressed by variants like Leaky ReLU and ELU.

**Step 2 — Batch Normalization (2.5 Marks):**
Batch Norm normalizes pre-activations within a mini-batch:

$$\hat{z}^{(l)} = \frac{z^{(l)} - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \epsilon}}, \quad a^{(l)} = \gamma \hat{z}^{(l)} + \beta$$

By keeping activations in a stable distribution (zero mean, unit variance), it prevents the gradient from being attenuated by extreme pre-activation values, effectively acting as a regularizer and gradient stabilizer.

**Step 3 — Residual Connections (2.5 Marks):**
ResNets (He et al., 2015) introduce skip connections:

$$a^{(l+1)} = \mathcal{F}(a^{(l)}, W^{(l)}) + a^{(l)}$$

The gradient through the skip path is:

$$\frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial a^{(l)}} + I$$

The **identity term $I$** ensures that even when $\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial a^{(l)}}$ vanishes, the gradient magnitude is at least **1**. This enabled training of networks with 100+ layers (ResNet-152) and is foundational to modern architectures.

---

### **Part B — Option B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question: (a)** With necessary mathematical derivation, explain the exploding gradient problem in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). Define the spectral radius and state the stability condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain gradient clipping with its mathematical formulation. Compare the vanishing and exploding gradient problems in terms of their causes, symptoms, and remedies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — RNN Forward and Backward Setup (1 Mark):**
A vanilla RNN updates its hidden state as $h^{(t)} = \tanh(W_{hh} h^{(t-1)} + W_{xh} x^{(t)} + b)$.
The loss gradient with respect to a past hidden state $h^{(t)}$ is computed via Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).

**Step 2 — BPTT Chain Rule (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T)}} \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}}$$

**Step 3 — Recurrent Jacobian (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z^{(k+1)})\right) W_{hh}$$

Taking the L2 norm and using the spectral radius definition $\rho(W_{hh}) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i \vert$:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq \rho(W_{hh})$$

**Step 4 — Final Bound and Stability Condition (2 Marks):**
After $T-t$ multiplications:

$$\left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2 \leq [\rho(W_{hh})]^{T-t} \left\vert \left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T)}} \right\vert \right\vert_2$$

**Stability Condition:** The RNN is **stable** if and only if $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$. When $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, the gradient grows exponentially with the time horizon $T - t$, causing numerical overflow and `NaN` losses. This is the **exploding gradient** pathology.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Gradient Clipping Derivation (3 Marks):**
Given gradient $g = \nabla_W \mathcal{L}$ and threshold $\tau$:

$$g_{\text{clipped}} = \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 \leq \tau \\[4pt] \dfrac{\tau \cdot g}{\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2} & \text{if } \vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 > \tau \end{cases}$$

**Why it works:** The rescaled gradient $g_{\text{clipped}}$ lies on the surface of a hypersphere of radius $\tau$, preserving the gradient direction while bounding its magnitude. The weight update $W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot g_{\text{clipped}}$ is therefore numerically safe.

**Step 2 — Comparative Analysis Table (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | Vanishing Gradient | Exploding Gradient |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Symptom** | Loss stops decreasing; early layers don't learn | Loss becomes `NaN`; weights overflow |
| **Primary Cause** | Activation saturation (sigmoid/tanh) or small init | Recurrent weights with $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$ or large init |
| **Math. Bound** | $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-1}$ for sigmoid | $[\rho(W_{hh})]^{T-t}$ for RNN |
| **Remedy 1** | ReLU / Leaky ReLU activations | Gradient clipping (norm or value-based) |
| **Remedy 2** | Xavier / He initialization | Spectral radius regularization |
| **Remedy 3** | Batch/Layer Normalization | LSTM / GRU with gated cell states |
| **Remedy 4** | Residual / Skip connections | Truncated BPTT |
| **Common in** | Deep feedforward networks, RNNs (long seq.) | Vanilla RNNs, deep un-normalized nets |
| **Detection** | Monitor $\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 \to 0$ | Monitor $\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 \to \infty$ or `NaN` |

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vanishing & Exploding Gradients**

- **Definition:** Pathological condition where backpropagated gradients shrink to zero (vanishing) or grow to infinity (exploding), preventing effective learning in deep/long architectures.
- **Root Mechanism:** Repeated multiplication of per-layer Jacobians via the **chain rule** causes exponential behavior depending on whether the per-layer norm is below or above **1.0**.
- **Vanishing — Sigmoid Bound:** $\sigma'(x) \leq \frac{1}{4}$, so the gradient scales as $\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-1}$ in an $L$-layer sigmoid net.
- **Exploding — Spectral Radius:** RNN stability requires $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$; otherwise, $[\rho(W_{hh})]^{T-t}$ diverges exponentially with the time horizon.
- **Mitigation Toolkit:**
  1. **ReLU / Leaky ReLU** activations (derivative $\in \{0, 1\}$).
  2. **Xavier ($\frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$)** and **He ($\frac{2}{n_{in}}$)** initialization schemes.
  3. **Batch Normalization / Layer Normalization** to stabilize activations.
  4. **Residual (skip) connections** providing gradient highways.
  5. **Gradient Clipping** with threshold $\tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 5$).
  6. **LSTM/GRU** cells with additive forget-gate updates.
  7. **Truncated BPTT** for RNN training.
- **Numerical Heuristic:** Stable gradients remain in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ magnitude range. Outside this range, training is compromised.
- **KTU Exam Focus Points:**
  - Always derive the bound explicitly.
  - Always mention the spectral radius for RNN questions.
  - Always quantify with $L=10$ or $L=20$ numerical examples.
  - Always state that **vanishing $\neq$ regularization**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advanced RNN Architectures - Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture introduced by **Sepp Hochreiter and Jürgen Schmidhuber (1997)**. It is engineered to overcome the fundamental **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in standard RNNs by introducing a **memory cell** regulated by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms**: the **forget gate**, the **input gate**, and the **output gate**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, LSTM is positioned as an *advanced sequential modelling architecture* that enables the network to selectively retain, update, or discard information over arbitrarily long time horizons, making it the de-facto standard for tasks such as speech recognition, machine translation, time-series forecasting, and video captioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEITT832 – Module 3):**
> LSTM is treated as a core architecture under "Advanced RNN Architectures". Students must be able to derive the forward pass, explain the role of each gate, and articulate *why* LSTM mitigates the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Reading Room" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a student preparing for an exam. You have a **notebook (cell state)** where you write down important facts. A standard RNN is like someone who **rewrites the entire notebook from scratch every time a new page of textbook arrives** — old, important notes gradually get overwritten and lost.
>
> LSTM, however, has three "decision-makers" (gates) that act like a smart librarian:
> - **Forget Gate (f_t)** — decides *which old notes to erase* (e.g., discard last week's grocery list).
> - **Input Gate (i_t)** — decides *which new facts to write down* (e.g., only record exam-relevant material).
> - **Output Gate (o_t)** — decides *which part of the notebook to read aloud right now* (e.g., only the answer to the question being asked).
>
> The **cell state (C_t)** is the persistent notebook that carries knowledge across time, while the **hidden state (h_t)** is the answer you speak aloud at each moment.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Cell State | $C_t$ | Long-term memory highway |
| Hidden State | $h_t$ | Short-term / output state |
| Forget Gate | $f_t$ | Controls erasure of past memory |
| Input Gate | $i_t$ | Controls writing of new memory |
| Output Gate | $o_t$ | Controls reading of memory |
| Candidate Memory | $\tilde{C}_t$ | Proposed new memory content |
| Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma$ | Squashes values to $[0, 1]$ |
| Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh$ | Squashes values to $[-1, 1]$ |

### 1.4 Standard Architectural Metrics

- **Default gate activation:** $\sigma$ (sigmoid), squashing values into the open interval **(0, 1)**.
- **Default state activation:** $\tanh$ (hyperbolic tangent), squashing values into **[-1, 1]**.
- **Parameter count per gate:** $n^2 + nm + n$ weights + $n$ biases, where $n$ is the hidden size and $m$ is the input size.
- **Total trainable parameters per LSTM cell:** approximately **$4 \times (n^2 + nm + n)$** (because there are four parallel linear transformations feeding into the cell).
- **Cell state initialization:** typically the **zero vector $\mathbf{0}$** at $t = 0$.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Problem with Vanilla RNNs

A standard RNN updates its hidden state using:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

During **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, gradients are multiplied repeatedly by the same Jacobian. When the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is less than 1, gradients **shrink exponentially** → **vanishing gradient**. When greater than 1, gradients **grow exponentially** → **exploding gradient**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** Vanilla RNNs cannot learn dependencies spanning more than ~10 time-steps in practice. LSTM resolves this by introducing an **additive cell-state update pathway**, which keeps gradient flow near-constant.

### 2.2 LSTM Cell — Logical Step-by-Step

The LSTM cell processes input $(x_t, h_{t-1}, C_{t-1})$ and produces output $(h_t, C_t)$ in **four distinct logical stages**:

**Stage 1 — Forget Gate (Erasure Decision):**
The forget gate examines $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ and outputs a vector $f_t \in [0, 1]^n$ that scales each dimension of the previous cell state.

**Stage 2 — Input Gate (Write Decision):**
The input gate $i_t$ decides *which values to update*, while a $\tanh$ layer creates a vector of new candidate values $\tilde{C}_t$ that *could* be added to the state.

**Stage 3 — Cell State Update (Memory Refresh):**
The old cell state $C_{t-1}$ is multiplied by $f_t$ (forgetting irrelevant parts), then new candidates $\tilde{C}_t$ scaled by $i_t$ are added.

**Stage 4 — Output Gate (Read Decision):**
The output gate $o_t$ controls which parts of the (tanh-squashed) cell state become the next hidden state $h_t$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The "FIT-O" Memory Aid (Forget, Input, Tilde, Output)** — used by KTU toppers.

| # | Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Forget gate vector |
| 2 | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Input gate vector |
| 3 | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | Candidate cell state |
| 4 | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | New cell state |
| 5 | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Output gate vector |
| 6 | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | New hidden state |

**Where:**
- $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes vector concatenation.
- $\odot$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product**.
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+m)}$ are the trainable weight matrices.
- $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ are bias vectors.
- $n$ = hidden size, $m$ = input size.

### 2.4 Why LSTM Solves Vanishing Gradients — The "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **additive** structure of $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is the mathematical key. In vanilla RNNs, the hidden state is a *nested function* of all previous states (multiplicative chain). In LSTMs, the cell state is a *summation chain*:

$$C_t = C_{t-1} + \sum_{k=1}^{t} i_k \odot \tilde{C}_k \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} f_j$$

The forget gate product acts as a **learnable decay factor**, preventing the runaway shrinkage that plagues vanilla RNNs.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | LSTM Application |
|---|---|
| **Natural Language Processing** | Machine translation, sentiment analysis, named-entity recognition |
| **Speech Systems** | Acoustic modelling, voice activity detection, speaker verification |
| **Finance** | Stock-price forecasting, algorithmic trading, credit scoring |
| **Healthcare** | ECG/EEG sequence classification, ICU mortality prediction |
| **Industrial IoT** | Predictive maintenance on sensor time-series |
| **Autonomous Systems** | Trajectory prediction for vehicles and drones |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass of an LSTM Cell

We derive the complete forward pass from the input concatenation through to the hidden state output.

**Step 1 — Concatenate the previous hidden state and current input.**

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+m) \times 1}$$

This concatenated vector carries the entire information context available at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Compute the four pre-activation linear transformations in parallel.**

For each of the four gates/candidates, a linear projection is performed:

$$\begin{aligned}
z_f &= W_f \, z_t + b_f \\\\
z_i &= W_i \, z_t + b_i \\\\
z_C &= W_C \, z_t + b_C \\\\
z_o &= W_o \, z_t + b_o
\end{aligned}$$

Each of $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ has dimensions $n \times (n+m)$, and each bias $b_*$ has dimensions $n \times 1$. This is why the **total parameter count is $4 \times (n^2 + nm + n)$**.

**Step 3 — Apply element-wise non-linearities to obtain the four control vectors.**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(z_f), && f_t \in (0, 1)^n \\\\
i_t &= \sigma(z_i), && i_t \in (0, 1)^n \\\\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(z_C), && \tilde{C}_t \in (-1, 1)^n \\\\
o_t &= \sigma(z_o), && o_t \in (0, 1)^n
\end{aligned}$$

The sigmoid $\sigma$ is the *gate* function (an open/close dial), and $\tanh$ is the *content* function (a bounded candidate value).

**Step 4 — Update the cell state with the additive memory refresh.**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

This is the core LSTM equation. The Hadamard product $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$ performs *selective erasure*, and $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ performs *selective writing*.

**Step 5 — Compute the new hidden state (the "read-out").**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

The output gate decides which parts of the squashed cell state propagate forward as the network's response at time $t$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Unrolled Cell State Across Time

Expanding the recurrence $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ across $t$ steps from $0$ to $T$:

**Step 1.** Write the recurrence for $t = 1$:

$$C_1 = f_1 \odot C_0 + i_1 \odot \tilde{C}_1$$

**Step 2.** Substitute into the recurrence for $t = 2$:

$$C_2 = f_2 \odot (f_1 \odot C_0 + i_1 \odot \tilde{C}_1) + i_2 \odot \tilde{C}_2$$

**Step 3.** Distribute the Hadamard product (associative over $\odot$):

$$C_2 = (f_2 \odot f_1) \odot C_0 + (f_2 \odot i_1) \odot \tilde{C}_1 + i_2 \odot \tilde{C}_2$$

**Step 4.** Inductively generalize to arbitrary $T$:

$$C_T = \left(\bigodot_{k=1}^{T} f_k\right) \odot C_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{T} \left[ \left(\bigodot_{j=k+1}^{T} f_j\right) \odot i_k \odot \tilde{C}_k \right]$$

**Step 5.** Interpret the result:
- The first term is the *linearly decayed* initial state — the forget gates act as a learnable, time-varying decay factor.
- The sum is the *gated contribution* of every historical input — older contributions are attenuated by the multiplicative chain of forget gates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If all forget gates are set to $f_t = 1$, the cell state becomes a perfect *additive accumulator*, and gradient flow is **constant** across time — the ideal regime for long-range dependency learning.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Standard RNN vs LSTM

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory mechanism** | Single hidden state | Cell state + hidden state |
| **Update rule** | Purely multiplicative | Additive + multiplicative |
| **Vanishing gradient** | Severe (exponential) | Mitigated (constant path) |
| **Parameters per cell** | $n^2 + nm + n$ | $4(n^2 + nm + n)$ |
| **Long-range dependencies** | Limited (~10 steps) | Hundreds to thousands of steps |
| **Gating** | None | 3 gates (f, i, o) |
| **Interpretability** | Low | Moderate (gate values diagnostic) |
| **Computational cost** | Low | $\sim$4× higher |

### 3.4 Derivation: Backpropagation Gradient on Cell State

The gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial C_{t-1}}$ flowing back through the cell uses the **chain rule** with the cell-state equation.

**Step 1.** Start from the cell state update:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$ (treating $f_t$ as depending on $h_{t-1}$, not directly on $C_{t-1}$):

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t + \frac{\partial f_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \odot C_{t-1} + \cdots$$

**Step 3.** Approximate under the assumption that gate gradients are small (Hochreiter's original analysis):

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \approx f_t$$

**Step 4.** Compose the gradient across $T$ steps:

$$\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial C_0} \approx \bigodot_{k=1}^{T} f_k$$

**Step 5.** Conclusion — the *learnable* forget-gate product governs gradient flow. If $f_k \to 1$ consistently, gradient magnitude is preserved; if $f_k \to 0$, the network *intentionally* forgets, and gradient vanishes for those dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the precise mathematical reason LSTM outperforms vanilla RNN on long sequences: the gradient flow is *gated and learnable*, not blindly multiplicative.

### 4.1 LSTM Cell — Mermaid Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input
        XT["Input x_t"]
        HT1["Prev Hidden h_t-1"]
        CT1["Prev Cell C_t-1"]
    end

    subgraph Gates
        FG["Forget Gate f_t = sigmoid Wf concat ht-1,xt + bf"]
        IG["Input Gate i_t = sigmoid Wi concat ht-1,xt + bi"]
        CC["Candidate Cell tildeC_t = tanh WC concat ht-1,xt + bC"]
        OG["Output Gate o_t = sigmoid Wo concat ht-1,xt + bo"]
    end

    subgraph CellUpdate
        MUL1["Hadamard Product f_t * C_t-1"]
        MUL2["Hadamard Product i_t * tildeC_t"]
        ADD["Add to form new Cell State C_t"]
    end

    subgraph Output
        TAH["tanh C_t"]
        MUL3["Hadamard Product o_t * tanh C_t"]
        HT["New Hidden h_t"]
        CT["New Cell C_t"]
    end

    HT1 --> FG
    XT --> FG
    HT1 --> IG
    XT --> IG
    HT1 --> CC
    XT --> CC
    HT1 --> OG
    XT --> OG

    CT1 --> MUL1
    FG --> MUL1
    MUL1 --> ADD
    IG --> MUL2
    CC --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> ADD
    ADD --> CT
    ADD --> TAH
    TAH --> MUL3
    OG --> MUL3
    MUL3 --> HT
```

### 4.2 Unrolled LSTM Across Time Steps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0["x_0"] --> L0["LSTM Cell t0"]
    H0["h_0"] --> L0
    C0["C_0"] --> L0

    L0 --> H1["h_1"]

    H1 --> L1

    L1 --> H2["h_2"]

    L2 --> C3["C_3"]

    H3 --> L3
    C3 --> L3

    L3 --> H4["h_4"]
    L3 --> C4["C_4"]
```
