```mermaid
    L4 --> C5["C_5"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The horizontal arrows along the top represent the **hidden state** propagation (short-term), and the arrows along the bottom represent the **cell state** propagation (long-term). The thick cell-state path is the "memory highway" that is largely uninterrupted, allowing gradients to flow efficiently during BPTT.

### 4.3 Information Flow Matrix

| Stage | Mathematical Form | Information Type |
|---|---|---|
| Forget decision | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | What to discard |
| Write decision | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | What to record |
| Content proposal | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | New candidate facts |
| State update | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Refreshed memory |
| Read decision | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | What to expose |
| Hidden output | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Exposed memory |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`
**Explain the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs and state how LSTM mitigates it.**

**Model Answer:**

Vanishing gradient in standard RNNs arises during **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, where the gradient of the loss with respect to early time-step weights involves repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$. When the dominant singular value of $W_{hh}$ is less than 1, the gradient magnitude **shrinks exponentially** with sequence length, preventing the network from learning long-range dependencies.

LSTM mitigates this through its **additive cell-state update**:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

The cell-state gradient path is approximately $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \approx f_t$, a *learnable* scalar per dimension. When $f_t \approx 1$, gradient flow is preserved across arbitrarily long time horizons. *(3 marks)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`
**List and briefly describe the three gates in an LSTM cell.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Forget Gate ($f_t$):** Uses sigmoid activation to decide what fraction of the previous cell state to retain. Output values near 0 mean *erase*, near 1 mean *keep*. Equation: $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$.
2. **Input Gate ($i_t$):** Decides which new candidate values will be added to the cell state. Acts in conjunction with the $\tanh$ candidate layer $\tilde{C}_t$.
3. **Output Gate ($o_t$):** Decides which parts of the (tanh-squashed) cell state are emitted as the hidden state $h_t$ at the current time step.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the complete **forward pass** of a single LSTM cell, clearly stating the equations for the forget gate, input gate, candidate cell state, output gate, new cell state, and new hidden state. Mention the activation functions used at each stage. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **unrolled architecture** of an LSTM network across four time steps. Highlight the difference between the hidden-state pathway and the cell-state pathway, and explain why the cell-state pathway is critical for learning long-range dependencies. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

The forward pass of an LSTM cell takes the input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, the previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, and the previous cell state $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, and produces a new hidden state $h_t$ and a new cell state $C_t$.

**Step 1 — Concatenation:**

$$z_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+m)}$$

**Step 2 — Forget Gate (sigmoid activation):**

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f z_t + b_f), \quad f_t \in (0, 1)^n$$

**Step 3 — Input Gate (sigmoid activation):**

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i z_t + b_i), \quad i_t \in (0, 1)^n$$

**Step 4 — Candidate Cell State (tanh activation):**

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C z_t + b_C), \quad \tilde{C}_t \in (-1, 1)^n$$

**Step 5 — Cell State Update (additive + multiplicative):**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 6 — Output Gate (sigmoid activation):**

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o z_t + b_o), \quad o_t \in (0, 1)^n$$

**Step 7 — Hidden State Output:**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Activation summary:** Sigmoid ($\sigma$) for all three gates (squashes to (0,1) to act as soft switches). Hyperbolic tangent ($\tanh$) for candidate cell state and cell-state squashing (squashes to (-1,1) for signed memory content).

*(7 marks — 1 mark each for the conceptual setup of the four gates/candidate + 2 marks for the cell-state update + 1 mark for the hidden-state output.)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Unrolled Architecture:** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2)

An LSTM network unrolled across four time steps $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$ contains four identical LSTM cells sharing the *same* weight matrices $\{W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o\}$ and biases $\{b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o\}$. At each time step $t$:

- The cell receives inputs $x_t$, $h_{t-1}$, $C_{t-1}$.
- It produces $h_t$ and $C_t$ using the forward-pass equations derived in part (a).

**Two Distinct Pathways:**

| Pathway | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden state | $h_t \rightarrow h_{t+1}$ | Carries short-term / output information between cells |
| Cell state | $C_t \rightarrow C_{t+1}$ | Carries long-term memory between cells |

**Why the cell-state pathway is critical:**

The hidden state pathway alone (as in vanilla RNN) experiences multiplicative gradient decay because $h_t$ is a *nested non-linear function* of all prior hidden states. In contrast, the cell state is updated **additively**:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

During backpropagation, the gradient along the cell-state pathway is approximately the cumulative product of forget gates. When these forget gates are near 1, gradients are preserved without exponential decay, enabling the network to learn dependencies spanning hundreds or thousands of time steps.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for unrolled diagram explanation + 2 marks for the two pathways + 3 marks for the long-range dependency justification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **standard RNN** and **LSTM** architectures with respect to: (i) memory mechanism, (ii) vanishing gradient behaviour, (iii) gating, (iv) parameter count, and (v) typical applications. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A univariate time-series $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T\}$ is fed into an LSTM with hidden size $n = 3$ and input size $m = 1$. Assume all weight matrices are identity ($W = I$) and all biases are zero. Given $h_0 = [0.5, 0, 0]^T$, $C_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$, and input $x_1 = 1.0$, compute $f_1$, $i_1$, $\tilde{C}_1$, $C_1$, $o_1$, and $h_1$. Use $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ and $\tanh(0) = 0$ wherever applicable. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

| S.No. | Aspect | Standard RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Memory mechanism** | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Cell state $C_t$ + hidden state $h_t$ |
| 2 | **Vanishing gradient** | Severe — gradients decay exponentially with sequence length | Mitigated — additive cell-state path with learnable forget gates |
| 3 | **Gating** | No gates | Three gates: forget ($f_t$), input ($i_t$), output ($o_t$) |
| 4 | **Parameter count** | $n^2 + nm + n$ per cell | $4(n^2 + nm + n)$ per cell (≈4× more) |
| 5 | **Typical applications** | Short sequences, simple pattern matching | Long sequences — translation, speech, time-series forecasting |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per row + 2 marks for explanatory elaboration.)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Given:** $n = 3$, $m = 1$, $W_f = W_i = W_C = W_o = I_{4 \times 4}$, $b_* = 0$, $h_0 = [0.5, 0, 0]^T$, $C_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $x_1 = 1.0$.

**Step 1 — Concatenate $h_0$ and $x_1$:**

$$z_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the four pre-activations (since $W = I$ and $b = 0$, each is just $z_1$):**

$$z_f = z_i = z_C = z_o = z_1 = [0.5, 0, 0, 1.0]^T$$

**Step 3 — Apply non-linearities to obtain gate vectors and candidate:**

$$f_1 = \sigma([0.5, 0, 0, 1.0]^T) = [\sigma(0.5), \sigma(0), \sigma(0), \sigma(1.0)]^T$$

Using the given values $\sigma(0) = 0.5$, $\tanh(0) = 0$, and standard sigmoid values $\sigma(0.5) \approx 0.6225$, $\sigma(1.0) \approx 0.7311$:

$$f_1 \approx [0.6225, 0.5, 0.5, 0.7311]^T$$

$$i_1 = \sigma([0.5, 0, 0, 1.0]^T) \approx [0.6225, 0.5, 0.5, 0.7311]^T$$

$$\tilde{C}_1 = \tanh([0.5, 0, 0, 1.0]^T) \approx [0.4621, 0, 0, 0.7616]^T$$

(Using $\tanh(0.5) \approx 0.4621$ and $\tanh(1.0) \approx 0.7616$.)

**Step 4 — Update the cell state:**

$$C_1 = f_1 \odot C_0 + i_1 \odot \tilde{C}_1$$

Since $C_0 = [0, 0, 0, 0]^T$, the first term vanishes:

$$C_1 = i_1 \odot \tilde{C}_1$$

Computing element-wise:

$$C_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 \times 0.4621 \\ 0.5 \times 0 \\ 0.5 \times 0 \\ 0.7311 \times 0.7616 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2877 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.5568 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the output gate and final hidden state:**

$$o_1 \approx [0.6225, 0.5, 0.5, 0.7311]^T$$

$$h_1 = o_1 \odot \tanh(C_1)$$

$$\tanh(C_1) = \tanh([0.2877, 0, 0, 0.5568]^T) \approx [0.2811, 0, 0, 0.5047]^T$$

$$h_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 \times 0.2811 \\ 0.5 \times 0 \\ 0.5 \times 0 \\ 0.7311 \times 0.5047 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1750 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.3690 \end{bmatrix}$$

*(7 marks — 1 mark per quantity computed, partial credit for intermediate steps.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing up activations** — many students write $\tanh$ for the gate equations and $\sigma$ for the candidate. **Correct:** gates use $\sigma$, candidate uses $\tanh$. Lose **1 mark** if reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting the Hadamard product** — writing $C_t = f_t \cdot C_{t-1} + i_t \cdot \tilde{C}_t$ as scalar multiplication instead of element-wise $\odot$ loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Omitting bias terms** — KTU expects the bias $b$ in each gate equation. Omitting it loses **0.5 mark** per gate.
> 4. **Misidentifying dimensions** — the concatenation $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ has dimension $(n + m)$, not $n$ or $m$ alone.
> 5. **Numerical errors in sigmoid** — always show the working. $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ is a common confusion point with $\tanh(0) = 0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3**

- [ ] LSTM was introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)** to solve the **vanishing gradient** problem in RNNs.
- [ ] An LSTM cell has **three gates**: forget ($f_t$), input ($i_t$), output ($o_t$), plus a **candidate memory** $\tilde{C}_t$.
- [ ] All gates use **sigmoid ($\sigma$)** activation; the candidate uses **tanh** activation.
- [ ] The cell state update is **additive**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- [ ] The hidden state output is: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
- [ ] The operator $\odot$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product** — *not* matrix multiplication.
- [ ] Parameter count per LSTM cell = $4(n^2 + nm + n)$ where $n$ = hidden size, $m$ = input size.
- [ ] Forget gate **near 1** → retain memory; **near 0** → erase memory.
- [ ] Input gate **near 1** → accept new candidate; **near 0** → reject.
- [ ] Output gate **near 1** → expose memory; **near 0** → suppress output.
- [ ] Cell-state gradient path = product of forget gates (learnable, prevents vanishing).
- [ ] The **"FIT-O" mnemonic** (Forget, Input, Tilde, Output) is the most common KTU exam short-form.
- [ ] LSTM unrolling shares weights across time steps.
- [ ] LSTMs are superseded in some modern applications by **GRUs** (Gated Recurrent Units) and **Transformers**, but remain in the KTU 2024 syllabus as the canonical recurrent gated architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a streamlined variant of the standard Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, introduced by Cho et al. (2014), that incorporates a sophisticated **gating mechanism** to selectively regulate the flow of information through the recurrent hidden state. Unlike vanilla RNNs which suffer from the vanishing and exploding gradient problem, GRUs use two dedicated trainable gates — the **update gate** and the **reset gate** — to adaptively capture long-range temporal dependencies in sequential data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A GRU is mathematically defined by **two gating vectors** (update and reset), a **candidate activation**, and a **linear interpolation** between the previous hidden state and the candidate state. The total parameter count is roughly **75% of an equivalent LSTM**, making it computationally lighter while preserving comparable performance on many sequence-modeling benchmarks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Diary Reader

Imagine you are reading a long diary every day, and each day you must summarize the entry into a *one-line mental note* that helps you remember the story. As you read:

- The **Reset Gate** is like asking: *"Is the previous summary still relevant to this new paragraph? Should I ignore parts of my old memory while computing today's summary?"* If the new paragraph is on a completely new topic, the reset gate tells you to "forget" most of the old context.
- The **Update Gate** is like asking: *"How much of my old mental note should I retain versus how much of today's new summary should I write down?"* If today's event is critical, you overwrite the old memory; if it's a minor detail, you preserve the old memory.

This is exactly what a GRU does mathematically — it learns *when to remember, when to forget, and how much to update*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from LSTM:** A GRU combines the LSTM's *forget* and *input* gates into a single **update gate**, and merges the *cell state* and *hidden state* into one unified vector. This makes GRUs simpler, faster to train, and easier to deploy in resource-constrained environments.

### 1.3 Why GRUs Were Introduced — The Motivation

Standard RNNs (with $\tanh$ activations) have a well-documented inability to learn long-term dependencies. Information propagated across many time steps is repeatedly multiplied by Jacobian matrices, causing gradients to either decay to zero (**vanishing gradient**) or blow up exponentially (**exploding gradient**). GRUs directly address this by:

1. Creating **gradient "highways"** through additive updates (the $(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}$ term acts as a skip connection).
2. Allowing the network to **learn to ignore irrelevant past states** via the reset gate.
3. Allowing the network to **learn to preserve useful past states** via the update gate.

### 1.4 Standard Notation Used in KTU Examinations

| Symbol | Meaning | Shape (batch $B$, hidden $H$, input $I$) |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Input vector at time $t$ | $(B, I)$ |
| $h_{t-1}$ | Previous hidden state | $(B, H)$ |
| $h_t$ | Current hidden state (output) | $(B, H)$ |
| $z_t$ | Update gate vector | $(B, H)$ |
| $r_t$ | Reset gate vector | $(B, H)$ |
| $\tilde{h}_t$ | Candidate hidden state | $(B, H)$ |
| $\sigma(\cdot)$ | Logistic sigmoid | scalar / vector |
| $\tanh(\cdot)$ | Hyperbolic tangent | scalar / vector |
| $\odot$ | Element-wise (Hadamard) product | element-wise |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture Overview

A GRU cell receives two inputs at every time step $t$:
- The current input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{I}$
- The previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$

It produces one output:
- The new hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$, which is also passed to the next time step.

Internally, the cell computes **two gate vectors** and **one candidate activation**, all of the same dimension as $h_t$.

### 2.2 The Three Operational Stages

#### Stage 1 — Gate Computation
Both gates are computed in parallel by feeding the **concatenation** of $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$ through a fully-connected linear layer followed by a sigmoid activation.

- The sigmoid squashes values into the open interval $(0, 1)$, allowing the gates to act as soft, differentiable **switches**.

#### Stage 2 — Candidate Hidden State Generation
The candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ represents a "proposal" for the new memory. Crucially, the **reset gate $r_t$ is element-wise multiplied with $h_{t-1}$** before being concatenated with $x_t$. This multiplication is the "resetting" action — it can completely wipe out dimensions of the past hidden state if $r_t$ is close to **0**.

#### Stage 3 — Linear Interpolation (The Hidden State Update)
The final hidden state is a **convex combination** of the old state and the new candidate. Because $z_t \in (0,1)^{H}$ element-wise, the coefficients $(1 - z_t)$ and $z_t$ always sum to **1**, ensuring stable information flow.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they account for ~80% of all GRU derivation questions in KTU exams.**

| # | Equation | Component | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | Update gate | Decides how much past to keep |
| 2 | $r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Reset gate | Decides how much past to forget |
| 3 | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ | Candidate state | Proposed new memory |
| 4 | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | Hidden state output | Final output of cell |

**Matrix dimensions (critical for KTU derivations):**

- $W_z, W_r \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times (H+I)}$
- $W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times (H+I)}$
- $b_z, b_r, b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$
- **Total parameters per GRU layer:** $3 \times (H^2 + HI + H) = 3H(H + I + 1)$

**Comparison with LSTM (Total params):** $4 \times (H^2 + HI + H) = 4H(H + I + 1)$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the GRU uses **3 weight matrices** (for $z$, $r$, and $\tilde{h}$), whereas an LSTM uses **4** (for $f$, $i$, $o$, and $\tilde{c}$). This is the source of the parameter-efficiency claim.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

GRUs are widely deployed in:
- **Speech Recognition** (acoustic modeling, e.g., Google Voice Search's earlier iterations)
- **Music Generation** (modelling temporal melodic structure)
- **Time-Series Forecasting** (stock prices, weather, IoT sensor streams)
- **Machine Translation** (encoder modules in sequence-to-sequence models)
- **Anomaly Detection** in network traffic / industrial equipment
- **Lightweight Edge AI** — because of their smaller memory footprint, GRUs are preferred for mobile and embedded NLP applications.

### 2.5 Interpretive Behaviour of the Gates

| Gate Value | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $z_t \to 0$ | GRU copies $h_{t-1}$ to $h_t$ (skip connection dominates) — **long-term memory preserved** |
| $z_t \to 1$ | GRU overwrites with $\tilde{h}_t$ — **fully new memory** |
| $r_t \to 0$ | Past hidden state is ignored in candidate computation — **short-term focus** |
| $r_t \to 1$ | Full past state is used in candidate computation — **long-term focus** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Forward Pass Derivation — Full Expansion

The complete forward propagation through a single GRU cell at time $t$ is shown below. We denote the concatenation operation as $[a, b]$, which stacks $a$ and $b$ horizontally.

**Step 1: Concatenate previous state and current input.**

$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{H + I}$$

This stacks the hidden state and input into one combined vector that feeds into all three gating/projection layers.

**Step 2: Compute update gate $z_t$.**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

**Why sigmoid?** We need outputs strictly in $(0, 1)$ to interpret them as mixing coefficients. Any other range (e.g., ReLU output $[0, \infty)$) would break the convex-combination interpretation of the final update.

**Step 3: Compute reset gate $r_t$.**

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

**Step 4: Apply reset gate to previous hidden state (element-wise).**

$$r_t \odot h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$$

This is the "reset" action. The $\odot$ symbol is the Hadamard product — each element of $h_{t-1}$ is independently scaled by the corresponding element of $r_t$. If a particular $r_t[i] \approx 0$, that entire dimension of the past memory is suppressed.

**Step 5: Compute candidate hidden state $\tilde{h}_t$.**

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

**Why tanh?** The candidate must lie in $(-1, 1)$ to allow the final state to be expressive in both positive and negative directions. $\tanh$ is also zero-centered, which yields faster convergence during gradient descent.

**Step 6: Compute final hidden state $h_t$ via convex combination.**

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

This can be equivalently expanded as:

$$h_t = z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t + (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}$$

**Why this is a "gradient highway":** Even after many time steps, the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ with respect to $h_{t-k}$ contains a multiplicative chain of the form $\prod_i (1 - z_i)$ along the additive skip path. When $z_i \to 0$, the term $(1 - z_i) \to 1$, allowing gradients to flow backwards almost **unattenuated** — directly solving the vanishing gradient issue.

---

### 3.2 Backpropagation-Through-Time (BPTT) Gradient Analysis

Let the loss at the final time $T$ be $\mathcal{L}_T = \ell(h_T, y_T)$. We derive $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_t}$ by unrolling the recurrence.

**Step 1: Total derivative of $h_t$ with respect to $h_{t-1}$.**

Using the chain rule on $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial [(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}]}{\partial h_{t-1}} + \frac{\partial [z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t]}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

**Step 2: Expand each term.**

The first term gives:

$$\frac{\partial [(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}]}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - z_t) + \text{diag}(h_{t-1}) \cdot \frac{\partial z_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

The second term gives:

$$\frac{\partial [z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t]}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(\tilde{h}_t) \cdot \frac{\partial z_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} + \text{diag}(z_t) \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify (omitting cross-derivatives on $r_t$ for clarity).**

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \approx \text{diag}(1 - z_t) + \text{diag}(z_t) \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

**Step 4: Full backprop expression from $h_T$ to $h_t$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_T} \prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the simplified Jacobian.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_T} \prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \left[ \text{diag}(1 - z_k) + \text{diag}(z_k) \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \right]$$

**Why this defeats vanishing gradients:** If the model has learned $z_k \approx 0$ for most $k$, then $\text{diag}(1 - z_k) \approx I$, and the product stays close to the identity matrix. Gradients flow back over hundreds of steps without decay.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Conceptual)

Suppose $H = 2$, $I = 1$, and at some time step we have:
- $h_{t-1} = [0.5, -0.3]^{T}$
- $x_t = [1.0]$
- Trained weights and biases yield: $z_t = [0.2, 0.7]^{T}$, $r_t = [0.9, 0.1]^{T}$, $\tilde{h}_t = [0.6, -0.4]^{T}$

Then:

$$h_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.2 \\ 1 - 0.7 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \cdot 0.5 + 0.2 \cdot 0.6 \\ 0.3 \cdot (-0.3) + 0.7 \cdot (-0.4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.40 + 0.12 \\ -0.09 - 0.28 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.52 \\ -0.37 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** The first dimension barely changed (high $z \approx 0.2$ meant "keep old state"), while the second dimension was heavily overwritten (high $z \approx 0.7$ meant "trust the new candidate").

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GRU vs LSTM vs Vanilla RNN

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gates | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| State vectors | 1 ($h$) | 2 ($c$, $h$) | 1 ($h$) |
| Parameters per layer | $H(H+I+H) = H^2+HI+H$ | $4H(H+I+1)$ | $3H(H+I+1)$ |
| Vanishing gradient mitigation | None | Strong (cell-state skip) | Strong (update-gate skip) |
| Training speed | Fastest | Slowest | Intermediate |
| Long-term memory | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| Output non-linearity | $\tanh$ | $\tanh$ on $h_t$ only | $\tanh$ on $\tilde{h}_t$ only |
| Parameter efficiency | Best | Worst | Middle |
| KTU exam frequency | High | High | **Highest** (Module 3 focus) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GRU Cell — Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef gateNode fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stateNode fill:#caffbf,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actNode fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inNode fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#a21caf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    xt["Input x_t"]:::inNode
    hm1["Previous h_{t-1}"]:::stateNode
    concat1["Concatenate [h_{t-1}, x_t]"]:::actNode
    concat2["Concatenate [r_t (.) h_{t-1}, x_t]"]:::actNode

    Wz["W_z linear + b_z"]:::actNode
    Wr["W_r linear + b_r"]:::actNode
    Wh["W_h linear + b_h"]:::actNode

    zt["Update gate z_t = sigmoid"]:::gateNode
    rt["Reset gate r_t = sigmoid"]:::gateNode

    mul["Element-wise Multiply r_t (.) h_{t-1}"]:::actNode
    ht["Candidate h_tilde_t = tanh"]:::gateNode

    inter["Interpolation (1-z_t) h_{t-1} + z_t h_tilde_t"]:::actNode
    htOut["Output h_t"]:::outNode

    hm1 --> concat1
    xt --> concat1
    concat1 --> Wz
    concat1 --> Wr
    Wz --> zt
    Wr --> rt

    rt --> mul
    hm1 --> mul
    mul --> concat2
    xt --> concat2
    concat2 --> Wh
    Wh --> ht

    zt --> inter
    ht --> inter
    hm1 --> inter
    inter --> htOut
    htOut -->|"to next step"| hm1
```

### 4.2 Unrolled GRU Through Time — Sequence View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef cellNode fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stateNode fill:#caffbf,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inNode fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#a21caf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    x0["x_1"]:::inNode
    x1["x_2"]:::inNode
    x2["x_3"]:::inNode
    x3["x_4"]:::inNode

    c0["GRU Cell t=1"]:::cellNode
    c1["GRU Cell t=2"]:::cellNode
    c2["GRU Cell t=3"]:::cellNode
    c3["GRU Cell t=4"]:::cellNode

    h0["h_1"]:::stateNode
    h1["h_2"]:::stateNode
    h2["h_3"]:::stateNode
    h3["h_4"]:::stateNode

    o0["y_1"]:::outNode
    o1["y_2"]:::outNode
    o2["y_3"]:::outNode
    o3["y_4"]:::outNode

    h_init["h_0 = 0"]:::stateNode

    h_init --> c0
    x0 --> c0
    c0 --> h0
    h0 --> o0
    h0 --> c1

    x1 --> c1
    c1 --> h1
    h1 --> o1
    h1 --> c2

    x2 --> c2
    c2 --> h2
    h2 --> o2
    h2 --> c3

    x3 --> c3
    c3 --> h3
    h3 --> o3
```

### 4.3 Reset vs Update Gate — Functional Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef decision fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#caffbf,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    start["Receive h_{t-1} and x_t"]:::process
    gatecomp["Compute z_t and r_t via sigmoid"]:::process
    zQ{"z_t value?"}:::decision
    rQ{"r_t value?"}:::decision
    zLow["z_t approx 0: COPY h_{t-1} to h_t (long-term memory)"]:::output
    zHigh["z_t approx 1: OVERWRITE with candidate"]:::output
    rLow["r_t approx 0: ERASE h_{t-1} before computing candidate"]:::output
    rHigh["r_t approx 1: USE full h_{t-1} in candidate"]:::output
    out["Final h_t = (1-z_t) h_{t-1} + z_t h_tilde_t"]:::output

    start --> gatecomp
    gatecomp --> zQ
    gatecomp --> rQ
    zQ --> zLow
    zQ --> zHigh
    rQ --> rLow
    rQ --> rHigh
    zLow --> out
    zHigh --> out
    rLow --> out
    rHigh --> out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State the four forward-propagation equations of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) and briefly explain the role of each gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four forward-propagation equations of a GRU are:

1. **Update gate:** $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ — controls how much of the previous hidden state is retained.
2. **Reset gate:** $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ — controls how much of the previous hidden state is forgotten while computing the candidate.
3. **Candidate hidden state:** $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ — proposed new memory.
4. **Final hidden state:** $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ — interpolation between old and new memory.

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark for equations + 1 mark for naming both gates + 1 mark for the role of each gate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between GRU and LSTM. Mention any two advantages of GRU over LSTM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| State vectors | Cell state $c_t$ and hidden state $h_t$ | Single $h_t$ |
| Parameters | $4H(H+I+1)$ | $3H(I+H+1)$ |

**Two advantages of GRU over LSTM:**

1. **Fewer parameters** — a GRU has ~25% fewer parameters, leading to faster training and lower memory consumption.
2. **Simpler architecture** — only one state vector, easier to implement, debug, and deploy on edge devices, while delivering comparable accuracy on many tasks.

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark for differentiation table + 1 mark each for two advantages.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Forgetting the $\odot$ (element-wise) product in $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ and writing a matrix multiplication instead. **2 marks deducted.**
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting the bias terms $b_z, b_r, b_h$ in the forward equations. **1 mark deducted per equation.**
> - **Common mistake #3:** Confusing the candidate state equation of GRU (which uses $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$) with LSTM (which uses $h_{t-1}$ directly). **2 marks deducted.**
> - **Common mistake #4:** Writing $\sigma$ for the candidate activation — it must be $\tanh$. **1 mark deducted.**
> - **Common mistake #5:** In BPTT derivations, students often forget to expand the $(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}$ term fully when computing $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$. **3 marks deducted.**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Derive the complete set of forward-propagation equations for a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Clearly explain the intuition behind the **update gate** and **reset gate**, and state the dimension of every weight matrix and bias vector involved.

**(b) (7 marks)** Perform a Backpropagation-Through-Time (BPTT) analysis of a GRU and show mathematically how the update gate enables the network to mitigate the **vanishing gradient problem** during training of long sequences.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concatenation [1 mark]:**

$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{H + I}$$

**Step 2 — Update gate [1.5 marks]:**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z), \quad W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times (H+I)}, \quad b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$$

*Intuition:* $z_t$ decides how much of the old hidden state $h_{t-1}$ should be carried forward. When $z_t \to 0$, the network preserves past memory (acts as a skip connection); when $z_t \to 1$, the network overwrites with new information.

**Step 3 — Reset gate [1.5 marks]:**

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r), \quad W_r \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times (H+I)}, \quad b_r \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$$

*Intuition:* $r_t$ decides how much of the old hidden state should influence the candidate. When $r_t \to 0$, the network ignores the past while computing $\tilde{h}_t$, behaving like a "fresh start".

**Step 4 — Candidate hidden state [1.5 marks]:**

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h), \quad W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times (H+I)}, \quad b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$$

**Step 5 — Final hidden state [1.5 marks]:**

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$$

**Total parameters per layer:**

$$3 \times [H \cdot (H + I) + H] = 3H(H + I + 1)$$

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the loss [0.5 marks]:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t(h_t, y_t)$$

**Step 2 — Gradient chain [1 mark]:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_T} \prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative [2 marks]:**

Differentiating $h_k = (1 - z_k) \odot h_{k-1} + z_k \odot \tilde{h}_k$ with respect to $h_{k-1}$:

$$\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - z_k) + \text{diag}(z_k) \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} - \text{diag}(h_{k-1}) \cdot \frac{\partial z_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitution [1.5 marks]:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_T} \prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \left[ \text{diag}(1 - z_k) + \text{diag}(z_k) \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} - \text{diag}(h_{k-1}) \frac{\partial z_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Show vanishing gradient mitigation [2 marks]:**

If the network has learned $z_k \to 0$ for irrelevant time steps, then $\text{diag}(1 - z_k) \to I$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \approx I \quad \text{when } z_k \to 0$$

The product across $T - t$ steps becomes:

$$\prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \approx I$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t} \approx \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_T}$$

The gradient is preserved almost **unchanged** across the entire unrolled network — directly defeating the vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the architecture of a GRU cell. Compare GRU with LSTM in terms of **architecture, parameters, and memory cells**, supported by a clearly labeled diagram.

**(b) (7 marks)** Consider a GRU with input dimension $I = 3$ and hidden dimension $H = 4$. Calculate the **total number of trainable parameters** in one GRU layer. Show each component separately and verify the reduction compared to a same-sized LSTM layer.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — GRU architecture description [3 marks]:**

A GRU cell receives $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$, concatenates them, and feeds the result through two sigmoid-gated linear layers to produce $z_t$ and $r_t$. The reset gate multiplies $h_{t-1}$ element-wise before computing the candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ via $\tanh$. The final $h_t$ is a convex combination of $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$ weighted by $z_t$.

**Step 2 — Architectural comparison table [2 marks]:**

| Property | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| State vectors | 2 ($c_t$, $h_t$) | 1 ($h_t$) |
| Output non-linearity | $2 \tanh$ ($c_t$ and $h_t$) | $1 \tanh$ ($\tilde{h}_t$ only) |
| Total parameters | $4H(H+I+1)$ | $3H(H+I+1)$ |
| Memory type | Long + short (dual) | Unified single memory |
| Implementation complexity | Higher | Lower |

**Step 3 — Diagram [2 marks]:** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $I = 3$, $H = 4$.

**Step 1 — Parameters for update gate [1.5 marks]:**

- $W_z$: $H \times (H+I) = 4 \times 7 = 28$
- $b_z$: $H = 4$
- Subtotal: $28 + 4 = 32$

**Step 2 — Parameters for reset gate [1.5 marks]:**

- $W_r$: $4 \times 7 = 28$
- $b_r$: $4$
- Subtotal: $28 + 4 = 32$

**Step 3 — Parameters for candidate state [1.5 marks]:**

- $W_h$: $4 \times 7 = 28$
- $b_h$: $4$
- Subtotal: $28 + 4 = 32$

**Step 4 — Total GRU parameters [1 mark]:**

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 32 + 32 + 32 = 96 = 3 \times 4 \times (4 + 3 + 1) = 96 \text{ parameters}$$

**Step 5 — LSTM comparison [1.5 marks]:**

LSTM has 4 gates (forget, input, output, candidate), so:

$$P_{\text{LSTM}} = 4 \times 4 \times (4 + 3 + 1) = 128 \text{ parameters}$$

**Reduction:**

$$\frac{128 - 96}{128} \times 100\% = 25\%$$

The GRU uses **25% fewer parameters** than the equivalent LSTM, which is the source of its computational efficiency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — GRU**

- **GRU stands for Gated Recurrent Unit** — a simpler, faster alternative to LSTM for sequence modelling.
- **Two gates only:** Update gate $z_t$ and Reset gate $r_t$, both computed via **sigmoid** activation.
- **Four canonical equations** must be memorized verbatim: update, reset, candidate ($\tanh$ activation), and final interpolation.
- **Critical symbol:** $\odot$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard)** product, NOT matrix multiplication.
- **Candidate uses $\tanh$**, NOT $\sigma$. Forgetting this is a frequent error in KTU papers.
- **Final state is a convex combination** of old and new states — this is the "gradient highway" that solves vanishing gradients.
- **Total parameters per layer:** $3H(H + I + 1)$. For LSTM it is $4H(H + I + 1)$. Difference is exactly **25%**.
- **Update gate behaviour:** $z_t \to 0$ means "keep old memory"; $z_t \to 1$ means "use new candidate".
- **Reset gate behaviour:** $r_t \to 0$ means "forget past"; $r_t \to 1$ means "use full past".
- **GRU vs LSTM:** GRU merges cell-state and hidden-state into one vector; LSTM keeps them separate.
- **GRU vs vanilla RNN:** GRU has gated additive updates; vanilla RNN has no gating and suffers from vanishing gradients.
- **Practical tip:** When in doubt about LSTM vs GRU in exam, default to GRU if the question emphasizes **computational efficiency**; default to LSTM if it emphasizes **long-term memory depth**.
- **BPTT derivation key insight:** The $\text{diag}(1 - z_k)$ term in $\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ acts as a learnable skip connection; when $z_k \to 0$, the Jacobian approaches the identity, preserving gradient magnitude across time.
- **KTU module weightage reminder:** Module 3 (Sequence Models) carries high marks — combine GRU knowledge with LSTM and Attention mechanism to be fully prepared.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers and Attention Mechanisms - Attention Mechanism Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transformers and Attention Mechanisms - Attention Mechanism Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Attention Mechanism** is a computational technique used in deep learning that enables a neural network to dynamically weigh the relative importance of different elements within a sequence (or set) when producing an output. Formally, it computes a weighted sum of representations, where the weights (attention scores) are learned functions of the input.

In the context of the **Transformer architecture** (Vaswani et al., 2017), attention is expressed as a mapping from a **query** and a set of **key–value pairs** to an output. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$

where $Q$, $K$, $V$ are matrices of queries, keys, and values respectively, and $d_k$ is the dimensionality of the keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Attention mechanisms are the foundational building block of the Transformer. Understanding *self-attention*, *scaled dot-product attention*, and *multi-head attention* is mandatory, as they directly map to the End-Semester Examination (ESE) outcomes under **CO3**: *Apply transformer-based models for sequence learning tasks.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a student preparing for an exam from a textbook of 1,000 pages.

- **Without Attention (older RNN/LSTM models):** You are forced to read the book strictly page-by-page, linearly. You cannot jump back. By page 1,000, you have likely *forgotten* what was on page 10. The model suffers from the same problem — known as the **long-range dependency problem**.

- **With Attention:** You keep a *highlighted notebook*. Every time you read a new page, you glance back at your notebook and decide: *"Which older pages are *most relevant* to what I'm reading now?"* You then assign *more importance* (weight) to those pages.

The **attention mechanism does exactly this**: for every position in a sequence, it "looks back" at all previous positions and decides how much each one matters for the current prediction. The *soft notebook* is the matrix of attention scores.

### Another Analogy — Library Search

Think of **Google Search**:
- Your **search query** = **Query (Q)**
- The **indexed web page titles** = **Keys (K)**
- The **web page contents** = **Values (V)**

The search engine *compares your query against all keys*, computes a relevance score, and returns a *weighted blend* of the values. Attention is mathematically the same process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Attention replaces recurrence (RNNs) and convolution (CNNs) with a parallelizable, content-based retrieval operation. This single insight unlocked the modern era of Large Language Models (LLMs).

---

## 1.3 Why Was Attention Introduced? — Motivation

Before attention, **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)** models used an **encoder–decoder RNN architecture**. The encoder compressed the *entire input sequence* into a single fixed-size **context vector**. This had three critical flaws:

1. **Information Bottleneck:** One vector must summarize a sentence of any length.
2. **Long-Range Dependencies:** RNNs struggle to remember early tokens in long sequences (vanishing gradients).
3. **Sequential Processing:** RNNs cannot be parallelized across time steps.

Attention solved all three simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** "**Q**uery asks, **K**ey identifies, **V**alue informs." Keep this in mind for any question on attention.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Query (Q)** | A vector representing "what information am I looking for?" |
| **Key (K)** | A vector representing "what information do I contain?" |
| **Value (V)** | The actual content/embedding to be aggregated. |
| **Attention Score** | A scalar measuring the compatibility between a query and a key. |
| **Softmax** | Normalization function converting raw scores into a probability distribution $\sum_i = 1$. |
| **Self-Attention** | Attention where $Q$, $K$, $V$ all come from the *same* sequence. |
| **Cross-Attention** | Attention where $Q$ comes from one sequence and $K$, $V$ from another. |
| **Scaled Dot-Product** | The standard scoring function: $QK^{\top} / \sqrt{d_k}$. |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | Running $h$ parallel attention heads on projected subspaces. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Families of Attention

In KTU examinations, you are expected to know three families of attention that evolved chronologically. Understanding the *why* behind each transition is critical for marks.

### Family 1: Additive (Bahdanau) Attention — 2014

Proposed by **Bahdanau, Cho & Bengio** (2014) to solve the Seq2Seq bottleneck. It uses a *small feed-forward neural network* (additive layer) to compute the alignment score.

$$
\text{score}(q_i, k_j) = v_a^{\top} \tanh\!\big(W_a [q_i \,;\, k_j]\big)
$$

- **Why:** Non-linear alignment; flexible but slow.
- **Used in:** Early machine translation systems.

### Family 2: Multiplicative (Luong) Attention — 2015

Replaced the additive layer with a simpler *dot product*:

$$
\text{score}(q_i, k_j) = q_i^{\top} k_j
$$

- **Why:** Faster, easily parallelizable on GPUs.
- **Limitation:** Sensitive to vector magnitudes; performance degrades for high $d_k$.

### Family 3: Scaled Dot-Product Attention — 2017 (The Transformer)

The Vaswani et al. (2017) innovation. A dot product scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$
\text{score}(q_i, k_j) = \frac{q_i^{\top} k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

- **Why scale?** When $d_k$ is large, dot products grow in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of *extremely small gradients*. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the variance of the scores at **1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Exam Fact:** The factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is the *only* difference between Luong attention and Scaled Dot-Product attention. The Transformer paper empirically found this scaling crucial for $d_k > 64$.

---

## 2.2 The Self-Attention Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

In **self-attention**, $Q$, $K$, and $V$ are all derived from the *same* input sequence $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ via three learned linear projections:

$$
Q = X W_Q, \qquad K = X W_K, \qquad V = X W_V
$$

where $W_Q, W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ and $W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$.

**Step 1 — Compute raw compatibility scores:**

$$
S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

Each entry $S_{ij}$ measures how much *position $i$* attends to *position $j$*.

**Step 2 — Scale to stabilize gradients:**

$$
S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply softmax row-wise to get attention weights:**

$$
A = \text{softmax}(S') \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

Each row of $A$ is a probability distribution over all positions.

**Step 4 — Weighted aggregation of values:**

$$
O = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

The output $O$ has the same length as the input but each position is now a *context-aware* representation.

---

## 2.3 Multi-Head Attention — The "Ensemble of Perspectives"

Instead of performing one attention function with $d$-dimensional keys, queries, and values, the Transformer projects them $h$ times into $h$ different learned linear subspaces, runs attention in parallel, and concatenates the outputs:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W_O
$$

where each head is:

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\; K W_i^K,\; V W_i^V)
$$

- **Why multiple heads?** Different heads learn to attend to *different types of relationships* — e.g., one head may track syntactic dependencies, another coreference, another positional proximity.
- **Total parameter cost is the same** as a single-head attention with full $d$ dimensions, because each head uses $d_k = d/h$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the *exact* equations tested in KTU ESE under Module 3.**

| Concept | Formula | Dimensions | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $Q,K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ | Core equation of the Transformer. |
| Attention Weights | $A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Row-stochastic: rows sum to **1**. |
| Multi-Head Concat | $\text{MultiHead} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W_O$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ | $W_O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d}$. |
| Per-Head Dimension | $d_k = d_v = d / h$ | — | Keeps total parameters constant. |
| Additive (Bahdanau) Score | $v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a[q_i;k_j])$ | $\mathbb{R}^{1}$ | Used in pre-Transformer era. |
| Luong Dot Score | $q_i^{\top} k_j$ | $\mathbb{R}^{1}$ | Special case when $d_k = 1$. |
| Softmax Function | $\text{softmax}(x_i) = e^{x_i} \,/\, \sum_j e^{x_j}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Converts scores to probabilities. |
| Computational Complexity | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | — | Quadratic in sequence length $n$ — bottleneck for long sequences. |
| Number of Parameters (Self-Attn) | $4d^2$ | — | $W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O$ each $d \times d$. |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Attention Is Used

| Domain | Application | Attention Variant |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | English → French | Cross-attention (encoder → decoder) |
| **Text Generation (LLMs)** | GPT, LLaMA | Causal (masked) self-attention |
| **Image Classification** | Vision Transformer (ViT) | Patch-level self-attention |
| **Speech Recognition** | Whisper | Encoder-decoder attention |
| **Protein Folding** | AlphaFold | Pairwise attention over residues |
| **Recommender Systems** | Sequential recommenders | Self-attention over user history |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention the *quadratic complexity* $O(n^2)$ in any answer about attention. This is a favourite follow-up question and shows awareness of real-world constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation: From Raw Scores to Output

Let us derive the full scaled dot-product attention step-by-step with a worked numerical example, as KTU sometimes expects concrete computation.

### Step 0 — Setup

Suppose we have an input sequence of length $n = 3$, with embedding dimension $d = 4$. Thus $X \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$.

For simplicity, assume $d_k = d_v = d = 4$ (single-head). The projection matrices $W_Q, W_K, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$ are learned, but for this illustration we take them as identity:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = X W_Q = X, \quad K = X W_K = X, \quad V = X W_V = X
$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Raw Compatibility Matrix $S = QK^{\top}$

$$
K^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying $Q \cdot K^{\top}$ entry-by-entry:

$$
S_{11} = (1)(1)+(0)(0)+(1)(1)+(0)(0) = 2
$$

$$
S_{12} = (1)(0)+(0)(2)+(1)(0)+(0)(2) = 0
$$

$$
S_{13} = (1)(1)+(0)(1)+(1)(1)+(0)(1) = 2
$$

Continuing this pattern systematically:

$$
S = QK^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 8 & 4 \\ 2 & 4 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the $\sqrt{d_k}$ Scaling

Here $d_k = 4$, so $\sqrt{d_k} = 2$:

$$
S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 8 & 4 \\ 2 & 4 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This scaling keeps the variance of the scores close to **1**, preventing the softmax from saturating.

### Step 3 — Apply Row-wise Softmax

For row 1, $S'_{1\cdot} = [1, 0, 1]$:

$$
\sum e^{S'_{1j}} = e^{1} + e^{0} + e^{1} = 2.718 + 1.000 + 2.718 = 6.436
$$

$$
A_{11} = \frac{e^{1}}{6.436} = \frac{2.718}{6.436} \approx 0.422
$$

$$
A_{12} = \frac{e^{0}}{6.436} = \frac{1.000}{6.436} \approx 0.155
$$

$$
A_{13} = \frac{e^{1}}{6.436} = \frac{2.718}{6.436} \approx 0.422
$$

For row 2, $S'_{2\cdot} = [0, 4, 2]$:

$$
\sum = e^{0} + e^{4} + e^{2} = 1.000 + 54.598 + 7.389 = 62.987
$$

$$
A_{21} = \frac{1.000}{62.987} \approx 0.0159
$$

$$
A_{22} = \frac{54.598}{62.987} \approx 0.867
$$

$$
A_{23} = \frac{7.389}{62.987} \approx 0.117
$$

For row 3, $S'_{3\cdot} = [1, 2, 2]$:

$$
\sum = e^{1} + e^{2} + e^{2} = 2.718 + 7.389 + 7.389 = 17.496
$$

$$
A_{31} = \frac{2.718}{17.496} \approx 0.155
$$

$$
A_{32} = \frac{7.389}{17.496} \approx 0.422
$$

$$
A_{33} = \frac{7.389}{17.496} \approx 0.422
$$

Collecting the attention weight matrix:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.422 & 0.155 & 0.422 \\ 0.0159 & 0.867 & 0.117 \\ 0.155 & 0.422 & 0.422 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verification (Row 1):** $0.422 + 0.155 + 0.422 = 0.999 \approx 1.000$ ✓

### Step 4 — Compute the Output $O = A V$

$$
O = A \cdot V = \begin{bmatrix} 0.422 & 0.155 & 0.422 \\ 0.0159 & 0.867 & 0.117 \\ 0.155 & 0.422 & 0.422 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing row 1 of $O$:

$$
O_{11} = (0.422)(1) + (0.155)(0) + (0.422)(1) = 0.844
$$

$$
O_{12} = (0.422)(0) + (0.155)(2) + (0.422)(1) = 0.732
$$

$$
O_{13} = (0.422)(1) + (0.155)(0) + (0.422)(1) = 0.844
$$

$$
O_{14} = (0.422)(0) + (0.155)(2) + (0.422)(1) = 0.732
$$

Similarly computing rows 2 and 3, we obtain the final output:

$$
O = \begin{bmatrix} 0.844 & 0.732 & 0.844 & 0.732 \\ 0.133 & 1.851 & 0.133 & 1.851 \\ 0.577 & 1.266 & 0.577 & 1.266 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix $O$ is the **contextualized representation** of the input — each row now encodes not just the original token but *its relationship with all other tokens* in the sequence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Multi-Head Attention Complexity

Given $h$ heads and total model dimension $d$:

- Each head has $d_k = d/h$.
- Cost per head: $O(n^2 \cdot d_k) = O(n^2 \cdot d/h)$.
- Total over all heads: $O(h \cdot n^2 \cdot d/h) = O(n^2 \cdot d)$.
- Output projection $W_O$: $O(h \cdot d_v \cdot d) = O(d^2)$.

**Total parameter count per self-attention layer:**

$$
P_{\text{self}} = \underbrace{d \cdot d_k}_{W_Q} + \underbrace{d \cdot d_k}_{W_K} + \underbrace{d \cdot d_v}_{W_V} + \underbrace{d_v \cdot d}_{W_O}
$$

With $d_k = d_v = d$:

$$
P_{\text{self}} = 4d^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Insight:** A single recurrent layer has parameters $O(d^2)$ but **sequential** compute $O(n)$. Attention has $O(d^2)$ parameters but **parallel** compute $O(1)$ per layer and total $O(n^2 \cdot d)$. This trade-off — *parallelism vs. quadratic memory* — is the central design tension in modern Transformers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why $\sqrt{d_k}$ Specifically?

Let $q$ and $k$ be vectors of dimension $d_k$ whose components are *independent* random variables with mean **0** and variance **1**. Their dot product is:

$$
q^{\top} k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i
$$

Using the identity for the variance of a sum of independent products:

$$
\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[q_i]\mathbb{E}[k_i])^2 = (1)(1) - 0 = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k
$$

The standard deviation is $\sqrt{d_k}$, which grows with the embedding dimension. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to **1**:

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{q^{\top} k}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1
$$

This is the precise mathematical justification for the scaling factor in the Transformer paper.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Scaled Dot-Product Attention

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *complete data flow* of a single attention head, mapping every tensor transformation from raw input $X$ to contextualized output $O$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT X n by d matrix"] --> nodeB["LINEAR PROJECTION 1 WQ"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["LINEAR PROJECTION 2 WK"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["LINEAR PROJECTION 3 WV"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["QUERY MATRIX Q"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["KEY MATRIX K"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["VALUE MATRIX V"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["MATMUL Q times K transpose"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["SCALE divide by sqrt dk"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["OPTIONAL MASKING for causal or padding"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["SOFTMAX row wise normalization"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["ATTENTION WEIGHTS A n by n"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["MATMUL A times V"]
    nodeG --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["OUTPUT O n by d"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["MULTI HEAD CONCAT across h heads"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["OUTPUT PROJECTION WO"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["FINAL OUTPUT n by d"]
```

### Architectural Insights from the Diagram

1. The three projection matrices $W_Q$, $W_K$, $W_V$ are the **only learned parameters** in a single attention layer.
2. The $\text{softmax}$ is the *only non-linearity*. Without it, the operation collapses to a fixed linear transformation.
3. The $\text{mask}$ block is essential for **autoregressive (causal) models** — it prevents position $i$ from attending to positions $> i$.
4. Multi-head concatenation happens *after* all parallel heads complete their attention.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SA["SELF ATTENTION BLOCK"]
        nodeSA1["SAME INPUT X"] --> nodeSA2["PROJECT Q K V from X"]
        nodeSA2 --> nodeSA3["ATTENTION OUTPUT"]
    end
    subgraph CA["CROSS ATTENTION BLOCK"]
        nodeCA1["INPUT A decoder state"] --> nodeCA2["PROJECT Q from A"]
        nodeCA3["INPUT B encoder output"] --> nodeCA4["PROJECT K V from B"]
        nodeCA2 --> nodeCA5["ATTENTION OUTPUT"]
        nodeCA4 --> nodeCA5
    end
```

### Diagram Interpretation

| Block | $Q$ Source | $K, V$ Source | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-Attention** | Same sequence | Same sequence | Encoder & Decoder (same stream) |
| **Cross-Attention** | Decoder state | Encoder output | Decoder → Encoder bridge |
| **Masked Self-Attention** | Same sequence (causal mask) | Same sequence | GPT-style autoregressive decoding |

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Multi-Head Attention Parallelization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["INPUT X n by d"] --> nodeWQ["W Q"]
    nodeX --> nodeWK["W K"]
    nodeX --> nodeWV["W V"]
    nodeWQ --> nodeH1["HEAD 1"]
    nodeWQ --> nodeH2["HEAD 2"]
    nodeWQ --> nodeH3["HEAD h"]
    nodeWK --> nodeH1
    nodeWK --> nodeH2
    nodeWK --> nodeH3
    nodeWV --> nodeH1
    nodeWV --> nodeH2
    nodeWV --> nodeH3
    nodeH1 --> nodeCONCAT["CONCATENATE"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeCONCAT
    nodeH3 --> nodeCONCAT
    nodeCONCAT --> nodeWO["W O projection"]
    nodeWO --> nodeOUT["OUTPUT n by d"]
```

### Why Parallel Heads Work

- Each head operates on a **lower-dimensional subspace** ($d/h$).
- Different heads can specialize — empirically, **lower heads** track *short-range* (positional) relations, while **upper heads** track *long-range* (semantic) relations.
- The total parameter budget $4d^2$ is preserved, giving the model the *same expressiveness per layer* but distributed across multiple *representational subspaces*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

> Differentiate between **self-attention** and **cross-attention** with one suitable example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Q, K, V** | All three ($Q$, $K$, $V$) come from the *same* input sequence. | $Q$ comes from one sequence (e.g., decoder); $K$, $V$ from another (e.g., encoder). |
| **Purpose** | Captures *intra-sequence* dependencies. | Captures *inter-sequence* dependencies. |
| **Example** | The encoder block of the original Transformer paper on the source sentence. | The decoder block attending to the encoder output during translation. |

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for clear definitions; 1 mark for source-of-Q distinction; 1 mark for example.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

> Why is the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ used in the dot-product attention of the Transformer? What happens if it is omitted?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The dot product $q^{\top} k$ is the sum of $d_k$ products of zero-mean unit-variance variables. Its **variance grows linearly** as $d_k$:

$$
\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) = d_k
$$

When $d_k$ is large (e.g., 64 or 512), the dot products take *very large magnitudes*. The softmax function then drives its output toward a **one-hot distribution** (one score $\approx 1$, rest $\approx 0$), producing **vanishing gradients** during backpropagation.

Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to **1**, keeping the softmax in a region of healthy gradient flow. If omitted, training becomes *unstable* and may fail to converge for large $d_k$.

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for variance argument; 1 mark for softmax saturation; 1 mark for training impact.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 ESE]**

**(a)** Explain the **scaled dot-product attention** mechanism used in the Transformer architecture. Derive its mathematical formulation from first principles and explain the role of the scaling factor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe **multi-head attention**. Explain how it differs from single-head attention and why multiple heads are beneficial. Show the parameter count comparison. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — Scaled Dot-Product Attention

**Step 1 — Set up the Q, K, V framework.** Each input token embedding $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is projected into three vector spaces using learned matrices $W_Q, W_K, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$:

$$
q_i = x_i W_Q, \qquad k_i = x_i W_K, \qquad v_i = x_i W_V
$$

**Step 2 — Compute pairwise compatibility.** For a query $q_i$ attending to key $k_j$, compute the dot product:

$$
s_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j
$$

The full score matrix is $S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Step 3 — Apply scaling.** Since $\text{Var}(q_i^{\top} k_j) = d_k$, divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$
s'_{ij} = \frac{q_i^{\top} k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize via softmax.** Convert raw scores into a probability distribution:

$$
a_{ij} = \frac{\exp(s'_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(s'_{ik})}
$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate values.** The output for position $i$ is the weighted sum of all values:

$$
o_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} v_j
$$

**Compact form:**

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Role of Scaling:** Without $\sqrt{d_k}$, large $d_k$ pushes softmax into saturation, causing vanishing gradients and unstable training.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Multi-Head Attention

**Definition:** Multi-head attention runs $h$ parallel scaled dot-product attention "heads," each on its own learned linear projection of $Q$, $K$, $V$:

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\; K W_i^K,\; V W_i^V)
$$

where $W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ and $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$, with $d_k = d_v = d/h$.

**Concatenation and projection:**

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W_O
$$

where $W_O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d}$.

**Differences from Single-Head:**

| Aspect | Single-Head | Multi-Head |
|---|---|---|
| Subspaces | One $d$-dimensional space | $h$ parallel $d/h$-dimensional subspaces |
| Representation | Single attention pattern | Ensemble of $h$ attention patterns |
| Parameter count | $4d^2$ | $4d^2$ (same!) |
| Expressiveness | Limited — one "view" | Rich — multiple semantic perspectives |

**Why Multiple Heads Help:**
1. Each head can learn a *different relationship* (syntactic, semantic, positional).
2. Ensemble effect — like having multiple filters in a CNN.
3. Same parameter budget — no extra cost.

**Parameter Count Derivation:**

$$
P_{\text{single}} = d^2 + d^2 + d^2 + d^2 = 4d^2
$$

$$
P_{\text{multi}} = h \cdot \left(3 \cdot d \cdot \tfrac{d}{h}\right) + d^2 = 3d^2 + d^2 = 4d^2
$$

Both are identical, confirming multi-head attention is *parameter-neutral*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 ESE]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Bahdanau (additive) attention**, **Luong (multiplicative) attention**, and **Scaled Dot-Product attention**. State the equations and discuss the complexity trade-offs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given an input sequence $X$ with embeddings, write down the four equations that define self-attention and explain each step. Mention the role of **masking** in the decoder. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Comparison of Attention Families

| Feature | Bahdanau (2014) | Luong (2015) | Scaled Dot-Product (2017) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Score Function** | $v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a[q;k])$ | $q^{\top} k$ | $\dfrac{q^{\top} k}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ |
| **Type** | Additive (feed-forward NN) | Multiplicative (dot) | Scaled multiplicative |
| **Complexity** | $O(d^2 \cdot n)$ per step | $O(d \cdot n)$ per step | $O(d \cdot n^2)$ — parallelizable |
| **Best For** | Small $d_k$, sequential models | Mid-size embeddings, RNNs | Large $d_k$, parallel hardware |
| **Parallelizable** | No (tanh + concat) | Limited | **Yes** (matrix ops on GPU) |
| **Drawback** | Slow; cannot parallelize | Saturates for large $d_k$ | $O(n^2)$ memory for long sequences |

**Key Insight:** Scaled Dot-Product attention replaced Bahdanau's *slow additive* layer with a *fast dot product + scaling*. This single engineering choice enabled **GPU parallelism** and made the Transformer practical for training on massive datasets.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Self-Attention Equations and Masking

**The Four Equations of Self-Attention:**

**Equation 1 — Linear projections from the same input $X$:**

$$
Q = X W_Q, \qquad K = X W_K, \qquad V = X W_V
$$

Here, $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is the input embedding matrix (with positional encodings added in practice). The matrices $W_Q, W_K, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ are the *only learned parameters* of this layer.

**Equation 2 — Compute pairwise dot products:**

$$
S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

Each entry $S_{ij}$ measures how much the query at position $i$ matches the key at position $j$. This matrix captures *all possible pairwise interactions* in the sequence.

**Equation 3 — Scale and softmax-normalize:**

$$
A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

The rows of $A$ are *probability distributions* summing to 1. This is the **attention weight matrix**.

**Equation 4 — Aggregate values weighted by attention:**

$$
O = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

Each output row $o_i$ is a *context-aware embedding* for token $i$.

**Combined Compact Form:**

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Role of Masking in the Decoder:**

In **autoregressive** generation (e.g., GPT), the model must *not* peek at future tokens when predicting position $i$. A **causal mask** $M \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is applied before softmax:

$$
M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \le i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases}
$$

The modified attention is:

$$
\text{Attention}_{\text{masked}} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V
$$

Adding $-\infty$ to the upper triangle pushes those softmax outputs to **0**, ensuring each position only attends to itself and earlier positions. Padding masks use the same idea to ignore `<PAD>` tokens.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Attention Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling:** Many students write only $QK^{\top}$. Always include the scaling — it is worth at least 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V source in self vs. cross attention:** Self-attention uses *one* input for all three; cross-attention uses *two different* inputs.
> 3. **Stating complexity as $O(n^3)$:** The correct complexity of self-attention is $O(n^2 \cdot d)$, *not* $O(n^3)$.
> 4. **Mixing up head count with parameter count:** Multi-head attention has the *same* parameter count as single-head ($4d^2$). The benefit is representational diversity, not capacity.
> 5. **Ignoring masking in decoder attention:** Decoder self-attention is *always* causal. Forgetting the mask will cost 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not stating the row-stochastic property:** Always mention that rows of $A$ sum to 1 — this is a quick 0.5-mark win.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Attention = weighted aggregation** of values, where weights come from query–key compatibility.
- The **three pillars** of attention are **Query** (what to look for), **Key** (what is available), **Value** (the actual content).
- **Self-attention** uses $Q = K = V = X W$, drawn from the same sequence.
- **Cross-attention** uses $Q$ from one sequence, $K$, $V$ from another.
- The **core equation** is: $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$.
- The **scaling factor** $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance of dot products to **1**, preventing softmax saturation.
- **Softmax** converts scores into a probability distribution — each row sums to **1**.
- **Multi-head attention** runs $h$ parallel heads on $d/h$-dimensional projections; same parameter count as single-head ($4d^2$).
- **Bahdanau attention** is additive (slow); **Luong** is multiplicative (faster); **Scaled Dot-Product** is multiplicative with $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling (Transformer standard).
- **Causal masking** sets upper-triangular entries to $-\infty$ before softmax in decoders.
- **Complexity** is $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ — quadratic in sequence length, the main scalability bottleneck.
- **Row-stochastic property:** Each row of the attention weight matrix $A$ sums to 1.
- **Why attention won:** It is *parallelizable* (unlike RNNs), captures *long-range dependencies* (unlike CNNs), and is *interpretable* via attention weight visualization.
- **Total parameters per self-attention layer:** $4d^2$.
- **Per-head dimensions:** $d_k = d_v = d/h$ where $h$ is the number of heads.
- **Mask types:** *Causal mask* (decoder), *Padding mask* (ignore `<PAD>`), *Look-ahead mask* (training-time efficiency).
- **Engineering applications:** LLMs (GPT), Vision Transformers (ViT), speech recognition (Whisper), protein folding (AlphaFold).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer Architecture (BERT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Transformer Architecture (BERT) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Transformer Architecture (Formal Definition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transformer**: A deep learning architecture introduced in the landmark paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* (Vaswani et al., 2017) that relies **entirely on a self-attention mechanism** to compute representations of its inputs and outputs, completely **dispensing with recurrence (RNNs) and convolutions (CNNs)**. The Transformer follows an **encoder-decoder** structure using stacked identical layers, where each layer contains: - A **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer - A **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** - **Residual connections** + **Layer Normalization** around each sub-layer ### 1.2 BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) > [!IMPORTANT] > **BERT**: A **pre-trained language representation model** that uses only the **encoder stack** of the Transformer. It is designed to **pre-train deep bidirectional representations** from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU**: While the *original Transformer* (Vaswani et al.) is an **encoder-decoder** model used for sequence-to-sequence tasks (translation, summarization), **BERT uses ONLY the encoder half**, since its objective is to build rich contextual *representations* (not to generate sequences autoregressively). ### 1.3 Standard BERT Configurations | Variant | Layers (L) | Hidden Size (H) | Attention Heads (A) | Parameters | |---|---|---|---|---| | **BERT-base** | **12** | **768** | **12** | **~110 Million** | | **BERT-large** | **24** | **1024** | **16** | **~340 Million** | ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Reading Detective" 🔍 > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy**: Imagine a **detective solving a mystery novel**: > - **RNN/LSTM** is like a detective reading the book **one page at a time, in order** — they might forget early clues. > - **BERT** is like a detective who **places every page on a giant table simultaneously** and looks at **every page in relation to every other page** to understand each word's meaning in context. > - The **attention mechanism** is the detective's **flashlight**: for each word (query), it shines on the **most relevant other words (keys)** in the sentence to gather clues (values). > - **Bidirectionality** means BERT reads the sentence **both left-to-right AND right-to-left at the same time**, like the detective seeing both the "before" and "after" of each clue. ### 1.5 Why BERT is a Breakthrough 1. **Bidirectional context** — captures meaning from both sides simultaneously (unlike GPT which is unidirectional). 2. **Transfer Learning** — pre-train once on massive text, fine-tune for many downstream tasks. 3. **Universal feature extractor** — one architecture serves NLU tasks (QA, classification, NER, etc.). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Self-Attention Mechanism — Operational Logic The **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is the heart of the Transformer. For every input token, we project it into three vectors derived from learnable weight matrices: - **Query (Q)**: "What information am I looking for?" - **Key (K)**: "What information do I contain?" - **Value (V)**: "What information do I actually provide?" #### Step-by-Step Logic: 1. **Project** input embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$ into $Q$, $K$, $V$ matrices using learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$. 2. **Compute similarity scores** between every pair of tokens via dot product $QK^T$. 3. **Scale** the scores by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent gradient saturation in softmax (since dot products grow large in high dimensions). 4. **(Optional) Mask** — for BERT this blocks attention to padding tokens and (in pre-training) to "future" positions is NOT used because BERT is bidirectional. 5. **Softmax** to convert scores into a probability distribution (attention weights). 6. **Weighted sum** of value vectors $V$ using attention weights → output. ### 2.2 Multi-Head Attention — "Multiple Perspectives" Instead of one attention function, BERT runs **$h$ parallel attention heads**, each with its own learned $W^Q_i, W^K_i, W^V_i$. This lets the model **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces** at different positions. The outputs of all heads are concatenated and linearly projected back: $$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, ..., \text{head}_h) W^O$$ where each head is $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$. ### 2.3 Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN) Applied independently and identically to each position: $$\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, x W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2$$ Two linear transformations with a **ReLU** activation in between. The inner dimension is typically $4 \times d_{model}$ (e.g., 3072 for BERT-base, 4096 for BERT-large). ### 2.4 BERT's Pre-training Objectives > [!IMPORTANT] > BERT is pre-trained using **two unsupervised tasks simultaneously**: #### (a) Masked Language Modeling (MLM) — "Fill-in-the-Blank" - **15% of tokens** are randomly selected for prediction. - Of those 15%: **80% replaced with [MASK]**, **10% replaced with random word**, **10% kept unchanged**. - The model must predict the **original vocabulary ID** of the masked token using cross-entropy loss. - This forces **deep bidirectional** context understanding. #### (b) Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) — "Does B follow A?" - Given sentence pair (A, B), the model predicts whether B is the **actual next sentence** (IsNext) or a **random sentence** (NotNext) with 50/50 probability. - Helps BERT understand **sentence-level relationships** for tasks like QA and NLI. ### 2.5 BERT Input Representation Each input token's embedding = **Token Embedding + Segment Embedding + Position Embedding** - **[CLS]**: Special token prepended to every sequence; its final hidden state is used for classification. - **[SEP]**: Special separator token between sentence pairs. - **Segment Embeddings**: Distinguish sentence A from sentence B (learned). - **Position Embeddings**: Learned absolute position vectors (up to 512 positions). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **exam-critical** for KTU Numerical / Derivation questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $Q, K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ | | 2 | Scaling factor | $\sqrt{d_k}$ | Prevents softmax saturation | | 3 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(H_1,\dots,H_h)W^O$ | $h$ = number of heads | | 4 | Per-head dimension | $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$ | e.g., 768/12 = **64** for BERT-base | | 5 | FFN transform | $\text{FFN}(x)=\max(0,\,xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2$ | Inner dim $= 4 \times d_{model}$ | | 6 | Layer Norm | $\text{LN}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | $\mu, \sigma$ over feature dim | | 7 | Residual connection | $y = x + \text{Sublayer}(x)$ | Identity skip path | | 8 | Sinusoidal PE (original Transformer) | $PE_{(pos,2i)}=\sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$ | BERT uses **learned** PE | | 9 | MLM masking probability | $P(\text{mask}) = 0.15$ | 80/10/10 split within masked set | | 10 | BERT input embedding | $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{pos}}$ | Sum of three embeddings | | 11 | BERT-base parameter count | $\approx 110$M | L=12, H=768, A=12 | | 12 | BERT-large parameter count | $\approx 340$M | L=24, H=1024, A=16 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU LaTeX Rule**: Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value inside tables — never the vertical pipe `|` (it breaks markdown). Example: $d_{model} \mid 2i$ is acceptable; $d_{model} \vert 2i$ is safer. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Search engines** (Google uses BERT since 2019 for query understanding). - **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants** (intent classification, slot filling). - **Medical NLU** (clinical note analysis, BioBERT variant). - **Legal/Scientific document analysis**. - **Code intelligence** (e.g., CodeBERT for programming languages). ## 3.
