Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention **Step 1 — Start with input embeddings.** Given $n$ tokens with embedding dimension $d_{model}$, pack them into matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$. **Step 2 — Project to Q, K, V.** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= X W^Q \\ K &= X W^K \\ V &= X W^V \end{aligned} $$ where $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$ are **learnable parameters**. **Step 3 — Compute raw similarity scores via dot product.** The matrix $QK^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ contains, at entry $(i,j)$, the similarity between token $i$'s query and token $j$'s key. $$ (QK^\top)_{ij} = q_i \cdot k_j $$ **Step 4 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ — The "Why".** For random $q, k$ with zero mean and unit variance, the variance of $q \cdot k$ is $d_k$. Large $d_k$ pushes the softmax into regions of tiny gradient. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to $\approx 1$. **Step 5 — (Optional) Apply mask $M$.** For BERT, attention is bidirectional so no causal mask is applied. However, **padding tokens are masked** with $M_{ij} = -\infty$ so $\text{softmax} \to 0$. $$ \text{MaskedScores} = \frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M $$ **Step 6 — Softmax row-wise to get attention weights.** $$ A = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right), \quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp\!\big((QK^\top)_{ij}/\sqrt{d_k}\big)}{\sum_{l=1}^{n} \exp\!\big((QK^\top)_{il}/\sqrt{d_k}\big)} $$ **Step 7 — Weighted sum with Values.** $$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A \cdot V \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v} $$ **Final Compact Form:** $$ \boxed{\;\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V\;} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Head Attention **Step 1 — Split $d_{model}$ into $h$ heads, each of size $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$.** **Step 2 — Apply attention independently on each head** $i = 1, \dots, h$: $$ \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W_i^Q,\, X W_i^K,\, X W_i^V), \quad W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}\times d_k},\; W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}\times d_v} $$ **Step 3 — Concatenate all heads along the feature dimension.** $$ \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_v} $$ **Step 4 — Linear projection with output matrix** $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h \cdot d_v \times d_{model}}$. $$ \boxed{\;\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)\, W^O\;} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Why $\sqrt{d_k}$? (Variance Argument) Assume each component of $q$ and $k$ is independent with mean 0 and variance 1. Then for the dot product $s = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mean: } \mathbb{E}[s] &= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i]\mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0 \\ \text{Variance: } \text{Var}(s) &= \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} 1 = d_k \end{aligned} $$ So the standard deviation is $\sqrt{d_k}$. Dividing $s$ by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes standard deviation to 1, keeping softmax inputs well-conditioned. ### 3.4 Derivation: Sinusoidal Positional Encoding (Original Transformer) For position $pos$ and dimension index $2i$ (even) or $2i+1$ (odd): $$ \begin{aligned} PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} &= \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d_{model}}}\right) \\ PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} &= \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d_{model}}}\right) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall**: BERT uses **learned** absolute positional embeddings (trainable, max 512), **NOT** sinusoidal. The sinusoidal form is from the *original* Transformer. Many students lose marks by confusing the two. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Number of Parameters in BERT-base | Component | Calculation | Parameters | |---|---|---| | Token Embeddings (V=30522) | $30522 \times 768$ | $\approx 23.4$M | | Position Embeddings (P=512) | $512 \times 768$ | $\approx 0.39$M | | Segment Embeddings (S=2) | $2 \times 768$ | $\approx 0.0015$M | | **Per encoder block** | — | $\approx 7.0$M | | — Self-Attention (Q,K,V,O) | $4 \times 768^2$ | $\approx 2.36$M | | — FFN ($W_1, W_2$) | $768 \times 3072 + 3072 \times 768$ | $\approx 4.72$M | | **12 encoder blocks** | $12 \times 7.0$M | $\approx 84$M | | Pooler (for [CLS]) | $768 \times 768$ | $\approx 0.59$M | | **TOTAL (approx)** | — | **~110 M** ✓ | ### 3.6 Mathematical Check — Attention Output Shape $$ \begin{aligned} X &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}} \\ Q = XW^Q &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k} \\ K = XW^K &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k} \\ V = XW^V &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v} \\ QK^\top &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \\ \frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \\ \text{softmax}(\cdot) &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \\ \text{softmax}(\cdot)\,V &\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v} \end{aligned} $$ For **multi-head** with $h$ heads: the output is reshaped to $\mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_v}$ and projected back to $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Transformer vs BERT ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Text Tokens] --> B[Tokenizer
WordPiece] B --> C[Token + Segment +
Position Embeddings] C --> D[Encoder Stack
L times] D --> E[Output
Representations] E --> F[Task Specific
Head] F --> G[Prediction] classDef bert fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gen fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef head fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class B,C,D,E bert class A gen class F,G head ``` ### 4.2 BERT Encoder Block — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB IN[Input X
shape n x dmodel] --> MHA[Multi-Head
Self-Attention] MHA --> AN1[Add and Norm 1] AN1 --> FFN[Feed Forward
Network] FFN --> AN2[Add and Norm 2] AN2 --> OUT[Output to next block] IN -.residual.-> AN1 AN1 -.residual.-> AN2 classDef op fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef norm fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef io fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class MHA,FFN op class AN1,AN2 norm class IN,OUT io ``` ### 4.3 Self-Attention Flow — Q, K, V Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR X[Input Embeddings X] --> WQ[WQ] X --> WK[WK] X --> WV[WV] WQ --> Q[Query Q] WK --> K[Key K] WV --> V[Value V] Q --> SCORE[QK Transpose
Scaled by sqrt dk] K --> SCORE SCORE --> MASK[Optional Mask] MASK --> SM[Softmax] SM --> AW[Attention Weights] AW --> WTV[Weighted Sum with V] V --> WTV WTV --> OUT[Attention Output] classDef proj fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef math fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef io fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#558b2f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class WQ,WK,WV,Q,K,V proj class SCORE,MASK,SM,AW,WTV math class X,OUT io ``` ### 4.4 BERT Pre-Training vs Fine-Tuning — Two-Stage Paradigm ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PT[Stage 1 Pre-Training Unsupervised] P1[Large Corpus e.g. Wikipedia] --> P2[Mask 15 percent tokens] P2 --> P3[BERT MLM and NSP loss] P3 --> P4[Pre-Trained Weights] end subgraph FT[Stage 2 Fine-Tuning Supervised] F1[Labeled Task Data] --> F2[Add Task Specific Layer] F2 --> F3[Initialize with P4 Weights] F3 --> F4[Train on Downstream Task] end P4 -.transfer.-> F3 classDef pt fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ft fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tr fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class P1,P2,P3,P4 pt class F1,F2,F3,F4 ft class P4 tr ``` ### 4.5 BERT Input Embedding — Summation Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR TOK[Token Embedding
30522 x 768] --> SUM[Element wise sum] SEG[Segment Embedding
2 x 768] --> SUM POS[Position Embedding
512 x 768] --> SUM SUM --> FINAL[Final Input
Representation] classDef emb fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sum fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class TOK,SEG,POS emb class SUM,FINAL sum ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]** > > **Q1.** Differentiate between the original Transformer architecture and BERT in terms of which components are used. **Model Answer:** The original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) consists of **both an encoder and a decoder** stack, designed for sequence-to-sequence generation tasks such as machine translation. It uses **causal (masked) self-attention** in the decoder so positions can only attend to earlier positions. **BERT**, in contrast, uses **only the encoder stack** of the Transformer. It is **bidirectional** (no causal mask), and its purpose is to learn **contextual representations** of input text rather than to generate output sequences. BERT is **pre-trained** using **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** objectives, then fine-tuned for downstream tasks. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]** > > **Q2.** What is the purpose of the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula? **Model Answer:** The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents the softmax function from entering regions of **extremely small gradients** (saturation). When $d_k$ is large, the dot products $QK^\top$ tend to have large magnitudes (variance $\propto d_k$). A softmax over very large values produces outputs close to one-hot vectors, killing the gradient during back-propagation. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance of the dot products to approximately 1, ensuring stable training and meaningful attention weight distributions. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Confusing BERT with original Transformer**: BERT is **encoder-only**, NOT encoder-decoder. Marks deducted for stating "BERT has a decoder". > 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the attention formula** — this is a **mandatory component** in KTU derivations. > 3. **Mixing up MLM and NSP objectives**: MLM masks **tokens**, NSP predicts **sentence relationships**. > 4. **Confusing learned vs sinusoidal positional encodings**: BERT uses **learned** PE. > 5. **Forgetting input embedding formula**: must show $E_{token} + E_{segment} + E_{pos}$. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **self-attention mechanism** used in the Transformer architecture with the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula. Discuss the role of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices and derive the final attention output. State why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **BERT architecture** in detail. Explain its **pre-training objectives (MLM and NSP)** and how BERT differs from the original Transformer. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** The self-attention mechanism allows every position in a sequence to attend to every other position, capturing long-range dependencies in a single step. **Step 1 — Input Projections:** Given input embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$, we project them using three learned weight matrices: $$ Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V $$ where $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$. - **Query (Q)**: represents "what this token is looking for". - **Key (K)**: represents "what this token contains" — used for matching. - **Value (V)**: the actual content that gets aggregated. **Step 2 — Compute similarity scores:** $$ S = Q K^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} $$ $S_{ij}$ measures the dot-product similarity between token $i$'s query and token $j$'s key. **Step 3 — Scale and Softmax:** $$ A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) $$ The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is required because the dot product variance grows with $d_k$, leading to large softmax inputs and vanishing gradients. The softmax normalizes each row to a probability distribution (attention weights summing to 1). **Step 4 — Weighted aggregation:** $$ \boxed{\;\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\;} $$ The output has shape $n \times d_v$ where each row is a weighted sum of value vectors, weighted by attention scores. This allows each token's representation to be a context-aware blend of all other tokens. --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained language model that uses **only the encoder half** of the original Transformer. **Architecture Details (BERT-base):** - L = **12** encoder layers (transformer blocks) - Hidden size H = **768** - Attention heads A = **12** (each head: $d_k = 64$) - Total parameters: **~110 Million** - Max sequence length: **512 tokens** **Input Representation:** $$ E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}} $$ Special tokens: **[CLS]** (classification token, prepended), **[SEP]** (separator between sentence pairs). **Pre-training Objective 1 — Masked Language Modeling (MLM):** - 15% of tokens are randomly chosen for prediction. - Of those 15%: **80% replaced with [MASK]**, **10% replaced with a random word**, **10% left unchanged**. - The final hidden vectors of masked tokens are fed into a softmax over the vocabulary to predict the original token. Loss = **cross-entropy**. - This forces BERT to use **bidirectional context** (left AND right) for prediction. **Pre-training Objective 2 — Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):** - Given sentence pairs (A, B), BERT predicts whether B **actually follows** A in the corpus. - 50% of pairs are real (IsNext), 50% are random (NotNext). - The [CLS] token's final hidden state is used for binary classification. - Loss = **binary cross-entropy**. **Key Differences from Original Transformer:** | Aspect | Original Transformer | BERT | |---|---|---| | Structure | Encoder + Decoder | **Encoder only** | | Attention | Causal/masked (decoder) | **Bidirectional** | | Positional Encoding | Sinusoidal (fixed) | **Learned** embeddings | | Objective | Supervised translation | **MLM + NSP (unsupervised)** | | Output | Generated sequence | **Contextual representations** | | Usage | Translation, generation | Fine-tuned for NLU tasks | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain **Multi-Head Attention** in detail. Derive the multi-head attention output formula. Why is multi-head attention preferred over a single attention head? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **BERT input representation**. Discuss **tokenization using WordPiece**, and explain the role of the **[CLS]** and **[SEP]** tokens. Explain how BERT is **fine-tuned for a downstream classification task**. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Multi-Head Attention** allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions**. Instead of performing a single attention function, the model projects $Q, K, V$ into $h$ different learned subspaces and applies attention in parallel. **Step 1 — Project into $h$ heads:** For each head $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, h\}$: $$ Q_i = X W_i^Q, \quad K_i = X W_i^K, \quad V_i = X W_i^V $$ where $W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$, with $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$. **Step 2 — Apply scaled dot-product attention per head:** $$ \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q_i, K_i, V_i) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q_i K_i^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V_i $$ **Step 3 — Concatenate all heads along the feature dimension:** $$ \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \dots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_v} $$ **Step 4 — Final linear projection with $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h \cdot d_v \times d_{model}}$:** $$ \boxed{\;\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O\;} $$ **Why Multi-Head is Better than Single-Head:** 1. **Different subspaces**: Each head can focus on different linguistic features (e.g., one head on syntactic relations, another on semantic similarity, another on positional patterns). 2. **Multiple perspectives**: A single attention head can only average information in one way; multiple heads learn complementary relationships. 3. **Stabilizes training**: The ensemble effect reduces variance and improves gradient flow. 4. **Empirically superior**: Vaswani et al. showed multi-head attention outperforms single-head attention with the same total parameter budget. For **BERT-base**: $h = 12$, $d_{model} = 768$, $d_k = d_v = 64$. --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **BERT Input Representation — Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Tokens The cat sat] --> B[WordPiece Tokenizer] B --> C[Token IDs 101 1996 4937 7731] C --> D[Token Embedding Table 30522 x 768] C --> E[Segment Embeddings A=0 B=1] C --> F[Position Embeddings 0 1 2 3] D --> G[Element wise Sum] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Final Input to Encoder] classDef tok fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef emb fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef op fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C tok class D,E,F emb class G,H op ``` **WordPiece Tokenization:** - BERT uses **WordPiece** tokenization (vocabulary size = **30,522**). - Rare/unknown words are split into **subword units** prefixed with `##` (e.g., "playing" → "play" + "##ing"). - This balances vocabulary size with the ability to handle **out-of-vocabulary (OOV)** words. - The first token of every sequence is always **[CLS]** (id = 101). - Sentence pairs are separated by **[SEP]** (id = 102). **Role of [CLS] and [SEP]:** - **[CLS]**: A special classification token prepended to every input. Its **final hidden state** ($C \in \mathbb{R}^H$) is used as the aggregate sequence representation for **sentence-level classification tasks**. During NSP pre-training, [CLS] is fed into a classifier. - **[SEP]**: A separator token that marks the boundary between sentence pairs (A and B) in tasks like QA, NLI, and NSP. **Fine-Tuning for Classification:** 1. Take the **pre-trained BERT** model with weights $\theta_{\text{BERT}}$. 2. Add a **task-specific classification head** (a single linear layer + softmax) on top of the [CLS] output. 3. Initialize with pre-trained weights; **fine-tune end-to-end** on labeled task data. 4. Typical hyperparameters: learning rate $2 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$, 2–4 epochs, batch size 16 or 32. 5. Loss: **Cross-entropy** over class labels. **Formally, for $C$-way classification:** $$ P(y \mid x) = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{cls}}\, h_{\text{[CLS]}} + b_{\text{cls}}) $$ where $h_{\text{[CLS]}}$ is the final hidden state of [CLS] from BERT and $W_{\text{cls}} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times C}$ is the new classification matrix. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD KTU RECAP — Transformer & BERT** - ☐ **Transformer** = attention-only architecture; **no RNN, no CNN**. - ☐ **BERT** uses **only the encoder** stack of the original Transformer. - ☐ **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$. - ☐ **Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$** prevents softmax saturation; reduces variance of dot products to ~1. - ☐ **Multi-Head Attention**: $h$ parallel attention heads, then Concat + linear projection $W^O$. - ☐ **BERT-base**: L=12, H=768, A=12, ~**110M params**; **BERT-large**: L=24, H=1024, A=16, ~**340M params**. - ☐ **BERT input = Token + Segment + Position** embeddings (all summed element-wise). - ☐ **[CLS]** token output = sequence-level representation for classification. - ☐ **[SEP]** token separates sentence pairs. - ☐ **MLM** = 15% tokens masked (80% [MASK], 10% random, 10% kept); predicts original. - ☐ **NSP** = binary classification of whether B follows A; 50/50 IsNext/NotNext. - ☐ BERT uses **bidirectional** (non-causal) self-attention — no future masking. - ☐ BERT uses **learned** positional embeddings (not sinusoidal). - ☐ **FFN** = 2 linear layers with ReLU; inner dim = $4 \times d_{model}$. - ☐ **Add & Norm** = residual connection + LayerNorm around every sub-layer. - ☐ **Fine-tuning** initializes from pre-trained weights + adds task head, end-to-end training. - ☐ **WordPiece tokenizer** handles OOV via subword units (vocab = 30,522). - ☐ **Cross-entropy loss** is used for both MLM token prediction and NSP/fine-tuning classification. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Memory Trick for KTU**: **"Q asks, K answers, V delivers"** — Queries ask the question, Keys provide matching, Values carry the actual content. **"BERT is an encoder-only Bi-LM"** — Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, pre-trained via Masked Language Modeling.

topics.title: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) - GAN Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) - GAN Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep learning framework, introduced by **Ian Goodfellow and colleagues in 2014**, that learns to model the underlying data distribution $p_{data}$ of a training set by framing the problem as a **two-player minimax game** between two neural networks trained in an *adversarial* manner.

The two networks are:

- **Generator Network ($G$)** — Parameterized by $\theta_g$, it maps a random noise vector $z$ (sampled from a prior distribution $p_z(z)$, typically a uniform or Gaussian distribution) to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$ that aims to mimic samples drawn from the real data distribution $p_{data}$.

- **Discriminator Network ($D$)** — Parameterized by $\theta_d$, it acts as a binary classifier. For any input $x$, it outputs a scalar probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ representing the probability that $x$ is a **real** sample (drawn from $p_{data}$) rather than a **fake** sample (produced by $G$).

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** GANs are classified as a **generative model** in the unsupervised learning paradigm. Unlike discriminative models that learn $P(Y\mid X)$, generative models learn the joint distribution $P(X,Y)$ or just $P(X)$ to produce *new* data instances.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] 
> **Analogy: The Counterfeiter vs. The Police**
> 
> Imagine a **counterfeiter (Generator)** who tries to forge currency, and a **detective/police officer (Discriminator)** whose job is to catch the forger. Initially, the counterfeiter's notes are crude and easily identified. As the detective gets better, the counterfeiter is forced to study genuine notes more carefully and improve the quality. Over time, this adversarial pressure pushes the counterfeiter to produce notes that are **virtually indistinguishable** from real currency. The detective never stops improving, and the counterfeiter never stops adapting — this is the essence of *adversarial training*.

**Alternative Analogy — The Art Student vs. The Art Critic:** An art student (Generator) keeps producing paintings and a critic (Discriminator) keeps judging whether they look like genuine masterpieces from a particular era. The student's skill improves precisely *because* the critic is strict.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Notation |
|------|------------|----------|
| **Latent Space** | Low-dimensional space of noise vectors | $\mathbb{R}^{d_z}$ |
| **Prior Distribution** | Distribution from which $z$ is sampled (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0,I)$) | $p_z(z)$ |
| **Real Data Distribution** | True distribution we want to mimic | $p_{data}(x)$ |
| **Generator Distribution** | Implicit distribution induced by $G$ | $p_g(x)$ |
| **Adversarial Loss** | Min-max objective that drives training | $V(D,G)$ |
| **Jensen-Shannon Divergence** | **Symmetric** measure between two probability distributions (in nats or bits) | $JS(P \Vert Q)$ |
| **Kullback-Leibler Divergence** | **Asymmetric** measure between two distributions | $KL(P \Vert Q)$ |
| **Mode Collapse** | Failure where $G$ produces only a few samples | — |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Failure where $D$ becomes too strong, stalling $G$ | — |

> [!NOTE] 
> **Key Distinction:** GANs learn the data distribution *implicitly* (via sampling) rather than *explicitly* (by computing a tractable likelihood). This is why they are called **implicit generative models**, distinguishing them from VAEs and PixelCNN.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Framework

GAN training is formalized as a **two-player minimax game** with the following **value function** $V(D,G)$:

$$
\min_{G} \max_{D} \; V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)} \big[ \log D(x) \big] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}(z)} \big[ \log \big(1 - D(G(z))\big) \big]
$$

### Interpretation of Each Term

- **$\log D(x)$** → Maximize this to make the discriminator correctly classify *real* samples as $D(x) \to 1$ (i.e., $\log D(x) \to 0$).
- **$\log(1 - D(G(z)))$** → Maximize this to make the discriminator correctly classify *fake* samples as $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (i.e., $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \to 0$).
- The **Generator** is trained to *minimize* the same objective — making the discriminator's correct answer on fakes look *bad* for the discriminator.

## 2.2 The Roles in the Game

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Discriminator's objective:** Maximize $V(D,G)$ — i.e., distinguish real vs. fake correctly.
> **Generator's objective:** Minimize $V(D,G)$ — i.e., fool the discriminator by producing realistic samples.

The two networks have **opposing goals**, hence the term *adversarial*.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Training Logic

- **Step 1 — Sample Data:** Draw a mini-batch of $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{data}$ and $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z$.

- **Step 2 — Train the Discriminator (Inner Loop):** Update $\theta_d$ to **ascend** the stochastic gradient of:
$$
\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log\big(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))\big) \Big]
$$
This makes $D$ better at identifying fakes.

- **Step 3 — Train the Generator (Outer Loop):** Update $\theta_g$ to **descend**:
$$
\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log\big(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))\big)
$$

- **Step 4 — Repeat:** Alternate between Steps 2 and 3 for a number of epochs until convergence.

> [!WARNING] 
> **Practical Training Heuristic (KTU Frequently Asked):** Early in training, when the discriminator is far superior, $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ *saturates* (its gradient $\to 0$). Therefore, in practice, $G$ is trained to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead, which provides a stronger gradient signal at the start. This is called the **non-saturating heuristic** for the generator loss.

## 2.4 The Optimal Discriminator (Key Result)

For a *fixed* generator $G$, the optimal discriminator $D^*$ that maximizes $V(D,G)$ is:

$$
D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_{g}(x)}
$$

**Derivation sketch:** The objective w.r.t. $D$ becomes:

$$
V(D,G) = \int_x p_{data}(x)\log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz
$$

Applying the change of variable $x = G(z)$, this becomes a functional of $D(x)$ alone. Setting $\frac{\partial V}{\partial D(x)} = 0$ gives the result above.

## 2.5 The Global Optimum (Key Result)

When $G$ is also optimal — i.e., $p_g = p_{data}$ — we have $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ for all $x$, meaning the discriminator **cannot do better than random guessing**. At this point, the value function evaluates to:

$$
V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log(4)
$$

Substituting $D^*$ back into $V$ and simplifying, the generator's objective reduces to minimizing the **Jensen-Shannon divergence**:

$$
C(G) = V(D^{*}, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{g})
$$

Minimizing $C(G)$ is therefore equivalent to minimizing $JS(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$ — pushing $p_g$ toward $p_{data}$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Value Function (KTU High-Yield) | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | The full GAN objective |
| 2 | Min-Max Game | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$ | $D$ maximizes, $G$ minimizes |
| 3 | Optimal Discriminator | $D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Holds for fixed $G$ |
| 4 | Generator Objective (Saturating) | $J^{(G)} = \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Suffers from vanishing gradient early on |
| 5 | Generator Objective (Non-saturating) | $J^{(G)} = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | **Preferred in practice** |
| 6 | Discriminator Loss (Binary Cross Entropy) | $J^{(D)} = -\mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Equivalent to negative $V$ |
| 7 | Global Optimum Value | $V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log(4)$ | At $p_g = p_{data}$ |
| 8 | JS Divergence Connection | $C(G) = 2 \cdot JS(p_{data} \Vert p_g) - \log(4)$ | Minimizing $C$ $\equiv$ minimizing JS |
| 9 | KL Divergence (Asymmetric) | $KL(P \Vert Q) = \int P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} dx$ | Asymmetric: $KL(P\Vert Q) \neq KL(Q\Vert P)$ |
| 10 | JS Divergence (Symmetric) | $JS(P \Vert Q) = \frac{1}{2}KL(P \Vert M) + \frac{1}{2}KL(Q \Vert M)$ where $M = \frac{P+Q}{2}$ | Symmetric and bounded in $[0, \log 2]$ |

> [!NOTE] 
> **Why does JS divergence matter for GANs?** It is **symmetric**, unlike KL divergence. For a generator trying to mimic $p_{data}$, symmetry is desirable because we don't want to penalize "missing modes" and "extra modes" differently. JS divergence treats both directions of error equally.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

GANs are extensively used in:

- **Computer Vision:** Image super-resolution (SRGAN), image-to-image translation (Pix2Pix, CycleGAN), photorealistic image synthesis (StyleGAN).
- **Medical Imaging:** Synthesizing MRI/CT scans to augment scarce medical datasets.
- **Drug Discovery:** Generating novel molecular structures for pharmaceutical research.
- **Text-to-Image:** Models like BigGAN and DALL·E.
- **Audio Synthesis:** WaveGAN for generating raw audio waveforms.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Training only on normal data, identifying outliers as those the generator cannot reproduce.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Optimal Discriminator $D^{*}(x)$

**Goal:** Given a *fixed* generator $G$, find $D(x)$ that maximizes the value function $V(D,G)$.

**Step 1 — Rewrite the value function as an integral over $x$.** Using the fact that $G$ maps noise $z$ to a sample $x = G(z)$, the noise expectation becomes an integral over the data space weighted by the generator's induced distribution $p_g$:

$$
V(D,G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_{x} p_{g}(x) \log \big(1 - D(x)\big) \, dx
$$

**Step 2 — Combine into a single integral** (the integrands live on the same domain $x$):

$$
V(D,G) = \int_{x} \Big[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_{g}(x) \log \big(1 - D(x)\big) \Big] \, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Pointwise optimization.** Because the integral is taken over independent contributions from each $x$, the function $D(x)$ that maximizes the integral is found by *pointwise* maximization of the integrand.

Let $y = D(x)$, $a = p_{data}(x)$, $b = p_g(x)$. The integrand becomes $f(y) = a \log y + b \log(1 - y)$, with $y \in (0,1)$.

**Step 4 — Take the derivative w.r.t. $y$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d f(y)}{d y} = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1 - y} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $y$:**

$$
\frac{a}{y} = \frac{b}{1-y} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a(1 - y) = by \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a = y(a + b)
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{a}{a + b}
$$

**Step 6 — Restore the original symbols:**

$$
\boxed{\,D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_{g}(x)}\,}
$$

This is a **rigorous, closed-form expression** for the discriminator that maximizes $V$ for a given $G$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Value Function at the Global Optimum

**Goal:** Show that at the global optimum, $V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log(4)$ and the generator's cost reduces to JS divergence.

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^{*}(x)$ into $V$:**

$$
V(D^{*}, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_{g}(x)} \, dx + \int_{x} p_{g}(x) \log \frac{p_{g}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_{g}(x)} \, dx
$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $\log 2$ inside each log to expose KL terms:**

$$
V(D^{*}, G) = \log(2) \int p_{data} \, dx + \int p_{data} \log \frac{p_{data}}{(p_{data} + p_g)/2} \, dx + \log(2) \int p_g \, dx + \int p_g \log \frac{p_g}{(p_{data} + p_g)/2} \, dx
$$

Since both $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ are valid probability distributions, $\int p_{data} \, dx = \int p_g \, dx = 1$.

**Step 3 — Recognize the KL divergences.** Letting $M = \frac{p_{data} + p_g}{2}$:

$$
V(D^{*}, G) = 2 \log 2 + KL\!\left(p_{data} \,\Big\Vert\, M\right) + KL\!\left(p_{g} \,\Big\Vert\, M\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of JS divergence:**

$$
JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{g}) = \frac{1}{2}KL(p_{data} \Vert M) + \frac{1}{2}KL(p_{g} \Vert M)
$$

Multiplying both sides by 2:

$$
2 \cdot JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{g}) = KL(p_{data} \Vert M) + KL(p_{g} \Vert M)
$$

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,V(D^{*}, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{g})\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** The constant $-\log(4)$ does not depend on $G$. Therefore, training $G$ to *minimize* $V(D^{*}, G)$ is **equivalent** to minimizing the **Jensen-Shannon divergence** between $p_g$ and $p_{data}$.

**Step 7 — At global optimum $p_g = p_{data}$**, the two distributions are identical, so $JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{data}) = 0$, giving:

$$
V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log(4)
$$

And $D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}}{2 p_{data}} = \frac{1}{2}$, meaning the discriminator is **indifferent** — the global optimum is achieved.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Loss Functions Used in Practice

| Loss Function | Formula (Discriminator) | Formula (Generator) | Comment |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Vanilla GAN (Saturating)** | $-\log D(x) - \log(1 - D(G(z)))$ | $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ | Suffers from vanishing gradient early on |
| **Non-saturating GAN** | Same as above | $-\log D(G(z))$ | **Default in modern implementations** |
| **Least Squares GAN (LSGAN)** | $(D(x) - 1)^2 + (D(G(z)))^2$ | $(D(G(z)) - 1)^2$ | More stable; encourages $D$ to push fakes toward $0$ |
| **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** | $D(x) - D(G(z))$ | $-D(G(z))$ | Uses **1-Lipschitz** constraint via weight clipping; addresses vanishing gradient |
| **WGAN-GP** | $D(x) - D(G(z)) + \lambda (\Vert \nabla_{\hat{x}} D(\hat{x}) \Vert_2 - 1)^2$ | $-D(G(z))$ | Uses **gradient penalty** instead of weight clipping; smoother optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Vanilla GAN loss is *theoretically* elegant but *practically* unstable. The non-saturating heuristic and variants like LSGAN and WGAN were introduced to mitigate vanishing gradients and mode collapse.

## 3.4 Vanishing Gradient Problem — Mathematical Explanation

When the discriminator is *too good* (i.e., it can reject generated samples with high confidence), $D(G(z)) \to 0$. The generator's saturating loss becomes:

$$
J^{(G)} = \mathbb{E}_{z} \big[ \log(1 - D(G(z))) \big] \approx \log(1 - 0) = \log(1) = 0
$$

But its gradient w.r.t. $\theta_g$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(G)}}{\partial \theta_g} \approx \frac{-1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D(G(z))}{\partial \theta_g} \to 0
$$

This means the generator receives **negligible gradient information** and stops learning — a fundamental challenge first noted in the original 2014 paper.

**Solution:** Replace the saturating loss with the **non-saturating** one:

$$
J^{(G)}_{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z} \big[ \log D(G(z)) \big]
$$

Its gradient is:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(G)}_{\text{NS}}}{\partial \theta_g} \approx \frac{-1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D(G(z))}{\partial \theta_g}
$$

Now, when $D(G(z)) \to 0$, this gradient $\to \infty$, providing a **strong learning signal** precisely when the generator is performing poorly. This is why Goodfellow et al. recommend this variant.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GAN Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional flow of a Generative Adversarial Network, including the data pathways, loss computation, and parameter updates for both networks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Noise Vector z from pz]:::noise --> B[Generator Network G with parameters theta_g]:::generator
    B --> C[Fake Sample x_fake = G of z]:::sample
    C --> E[Discriminator Network D with parameters theta_d]:::discriminator
    D[Real Sample x_real from p_data]:::sample --> E
    E --> F[Probability Output D of x]:::output
    F --> G{Loss Computation Stage}:::decision
    G --> H[Discriminator Loss J_D]:::loss
    G --> I[Generator Loss J_G]:::loss
    H --> J[Update theta_d via gradient ASCENT on V]:::update
    I --> K[Update theta_g via gradient DESCENT on V]:::update
    J --> E
    K --> B
    L[KTU Recap: The Two Networks Play an Adversarial Min Max Game]:::note
    L -.-> A
    L -.-> D

    classDef noise fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000
    classDef generator fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#1E5BBA,color:#000000
    classDef discriminator fill:#FFB4D4,stroke:#BA1E5B,color:#000000
    classDef sample fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#555555,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#D4FFB4,stroke:#3A8A1E,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF4B4,stroke:#B89A1E,color:#000000
    classDef loss fill:#FFD4B4,stroke:#BA5A1E,color:#000000
    classDef update fill:#D4B4FF,stroke:#5A1EBA,color:#000000
    classDef note fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#888888,color:#333333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### Diagram Description (for examiner's reference)

- The **Generator ($G$)** takes a noise vector $z$ from a prior distribution $p_z$ (top-left path) and produces a *fake* sample $G(z)$.
- The **Discriminator ($D$)** receives **both** the real sample (from $p_{data}$, middle-left) and the fake sample (from $G$, top-center) as inputs.
- $D$ outputs a probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$, which is fed into the loss computation block.
- The losses $J_D$ (discriminator) and $J_G$ (generator) are computed simultaneously and used to update $\theta_d$ (gradient ascent on $V$) and $\theta_g$ (gradient descent on $V$), respectively.
- The dashed feedback arrow from the note represents the **adversarial training loop** — both networks improve iteratively against each other.

## 4.2 Detailed Training Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Sample m noise vectors from pz]:::step --> S2[Step 2: Sample m real examples from p_data]:::step
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Generate m fakes: x_fake = G of z]:::step
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Update D by ASCENDING gradient of V]:::step
    S4 --> S5{Stop Condition Met}:::check
    S5 -- No --> S6[Step 5: Sample m NEW noise vectors]:::step
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Update G by DESCENDING gradient of V or ASCENDING non saturating loss]:::step
    S7 --> S8{Stop Condition Met}:::check
    S8 -- No --> S5
    S5 -- Yes --> END1[Convergence: p_g approximates p_data and D outputs 0.5]:::finish
    S8 -- Yes --> END1

    classDef step fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#1E5BBA,color:#000000
    classDef check fill:#FFF4B4,stroke:#B89A1E,color:#000000
    classDef finish fill:#D4FFB4,stroke:#3A8A1E,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE] 
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** In practice, $D$ is updated **$k$ times per single update of $G$** (typically $k=1$ or $k=2$) to keep $D$ close to its optimal value before updating $G$. This is essential for the theory (which assumes $D$ is optimized first) to hold approximately.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

> **Q1. Define Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). Name its two main components and state their roles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a class of deep learning framework introduced by **Ian Goodfellow et al. in 2014**, used to learn complex real-world data distributions through an **adversarial** two-player game.

The two main components are:

1. **Generator ($G$):** Takes random noise $z$ as input and generates fake samples $G(z)$ that mimic the real data distribution. (1½ marks)

2. **Discriminator ($D$):** Acts as a binary classifier that distinguishes real samples (from $p_{data}$) from fake samples (produced by $G$). It outputs a probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$. (1½ marks)

The two networks are trained **adversarially** — $G$ tries to fool $D$, while $D$ tries to detect fakes correctly.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

> **Q2. State and explain the value function $V(D,G)$ used in the training of GANs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The value function for GAN training is:

$$
V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}\big[\log D(x)\big] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}\big[\log(1 - D(G(z)))\big]
$$

- The **Discriminator** is trained to **maximize** this function, i.e., it should assign $D(x) \to 1$ for real data and $D(G(z)) \to 0$ for fakes. (1 mark)
- The **Generator** is trained to **minimize** this function, i.e., it tries to make $D(G(z)) \to 1$ so that the second term becomes $\log(1 - 1) = 0$. (1 mark)
- This adversarial setup constitutes a **min-max game**: $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions feature **internal choice** — students must answer either **OR** block. Below, we provide two completely independent 14-mark questions. Each is split into (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

> **Q(a).** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Generative Adversarial Network. (7 marks)
>
> **Q(b).** Derive the expression for the optimal discriminator $D^{*}(x)$ in a GAN. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

The **GAN architecture** consists of two neural networks working in opposition:

**Generator Network ($G$):**
- Input: A random noise vector $z \sim p_z$ (typically $z \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$ drawn from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $\mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$). (1 mark)
- Output: A synthetic data sample $G(z)$ of the same dimensionality as the real data. (1 mark)
- Goal: Learn the mapping $G: z \to x_{\text{fake}}$ such that the distribution of $G(z)$ approximates $p_{data}$. (1 mark)
- Often implemented using transposed convolutions (for images), MLPs (for tabular data), or RNNs (for sequences).

**Discriminator Network ($D$):**
- Input: Either a real sample $x \sim p_{data}$ or a fake sample $G(z)$. (1 mark)
- Output: A scalar probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ — the probability that $x$ is real. (1 mark)
- Goal: Maximize its classification accuracy, i.e., $D(x) \to 1$ for real, $D(G(z)) \to 0$ for fake. (1 mark)
- Typically a binary classifier (CNN for images, MLP otherwise) ending with a sigmoid activation.

**Working (Adversarial Loop):** Both networks are trained alternately. $D$ is updated $k$ times to keep it near its optimum, then $G$ is updated once to fool $D$. The **value function** $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ is minimized by $G$ and maximized by $D$. (1 mark)

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 for the complete architectural view.)*

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Step 1:** Start with the value function:

$$
V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]
$$

**Step 2:** Convert the noise expectation into an integral over $x$ using the induced distribution $p_g$:

$$
V(D,G) = \int_x p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx
$$

**Step 3:** Combine into a single integrand:

$$
V(D,G) = \int_x \big[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \big] \, dx
$$

**Step 4:** Since the integral is over independent contributions, **pointwise maximization** of the integrand w.r.t. $D(x) = y$ (with $a = p_{data}(x)$, $b = p_g(x)$) gives:

$$
f(y) = a \log y + b \log(1 - y)
$$

**Step 5:** Take the derivative and set to zero: (2 marks)

$$
\frac{df}{dy} = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1-y} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a(1 - y) = by
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $y$:

$$
y = \frac{a}{a + b} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}
$$

**Step 7:** Conclude the optimal discriminator: (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{\,D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}\,}
$$

This result is **fundamental** to the theoretical analysis of GANs and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

> **Q(a).** Explain in detail the step-by-step training procedure (algorithm) of a vanilla GAN. (7 marks)
>
> **Q(b).** Discuss the vanishing gradient problem in GANs and the practical heuristic used to overcome it. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Algorithm: Vanilla GAN Training (per iteration)** (Assume a batch size $m$, number of discriminator updates per generator update = $k$.)

**Step 1 — Sample Noise:** (1 mark)
Draw $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, z^{(2)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$ from the prior $p_z(z)$.

**Step 2 — Sample Real Data:** (1 mark)
Draw $m$ real data samples $\{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$ from the training set (representing $p_{data}$).

**Step 3 — Update the Discriminator ($k$ times):** (3 marks)
For each of the $k$ inner iterations:
$$
\theta_d \leftarrow \theta_d + \eta \nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \Big]
$$
This is **gradient ascent** on $V$ w.r.t. $\theta_d$, making $D$ better at distinguishing real from fake.

**Step 4 — Re-sample Noise:** (1 mark)
Draw a fresh mini-batch of $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$.

**Step 5 — Update the Generator (once):** (1 mark)
$$
\theta_g \leftarrow \theta_g - \eta \nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))
$$
This is **gradient descent** on $V$ w.r.t. $\theta_g$, pushing $G$ to produce samples that fool $D$.

> **Note:** Steps 1–5 form one outer iteration. The process is repeated for many epochs (typically hundreds to thousands), and progress is monitored by visualizing generated samples and tracking $D$'s accuracy.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**The Vanishing Gradient Problem:**

The generator's original loss is $J^{(G)} = \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. (1 mark)

To analyze its behavior, examine the gradient w.r.t. $\theta_g$:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(G)}}{\partial \theta_g} = \mathbb{E}_z \left[ \frac{-1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D(G(z))}{\partial G(z)} \cdot \frac{\partial G(z)}{\partial \theta_g} \right]
$$

**Early in training**, the discriminator is typically *much stronger* than the generator because distinguishing real from crude fakes is an easier task than generating realistic samples. (1 mark)

Consequently, for a *poor* generator, $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, which means:

$$
1 - D(G(z)) \approx 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{-1}{1 - D(G(z))} \approx -1
$$

Wait — let me reconsider. The issue arises **differently**. When $D$ is strong, for a *decent* $G$, $D(G(z))$ may actually be *moderate* (not exactly 0), but the function $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ **saturates** to values near $0$ for small $D(G(z))$, meaning its gradient w.r.t. $D(G(z))$ approaches **zero**. (1 mark)

Mathematically, $\frac{d}{dy} \log(1 - y)\big|_{y \to 0} = -\frac{1}{1-y} \to -1$, but the issue is more subtle: the **log function flattens** near $y = 1$, and the *expected* loss becomes dominated by samples for which $D$ is highly confident, which provide almost no learning signal. (1 mark)

**The Practical Heuristic — Non-Saturating Loss:**

Goodfellow et al. proposed using, in place of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$, the following:

$$
J^{(G)}_{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_z \big[ \log D(G(z)) \big]
$$

This is mathematically *different* from the minimax objective's $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ — the original minimax was about the $D$-player, while this is a $G$-centric reformulation that **maximizes** $\log D(G(z))$. (1 mark)

Its gradient is:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(G)}_{\text{NS}}}{\partial \theta_g} = -\mathbb{E}_z \left[ \frac{1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D(G(z))}{\partial \theta_g} \right]
$$

When $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (i.e., $D$ easily rejects the fakes), the term $\frac{1}{D(G(z))} \to \infty$, providing a **strong gradient** to update the generator precisely when it is failing. (1 mark)

This non-saturating heuristic is now the **default** in nearly all GAN implementations and was crucial to the practical success of GANs.

**Other Solutions** (Bonus 1 mark): Wasserstein loss with 1-Lipschitz constraint, LSGAN, gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), spectral normalization.

---

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the discriminator's and generator's objectives:** Many students write "$D$ minimizes $V$" — this is **wrong**. $D$ **maximizes** $V$, and $G$ **minimizes** $V$. (–1 mark)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the non-saturating heuristic:** Writing only $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ for the generator without mentioning the saturation issue and the practical fix will lose marks. (–1 to 2 marks)
> 
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps:** Jumping directly to $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}}{p_{data} + p_g}$ without the integral manipulations, change of variable, and derivative steps is penalized heavily. (–2 to 3 marks)
> 
> 4. **Mixing up KL and JS divergence:** KL is asymmetric, JS is symmetric. Confusing them is a common error. (–1 mark)
> 
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram** in the architecture question — without a labeled figure showing $G$, $D$, real and fake paths, the answer is considered incomplete. (–2 marks)
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to state the global optimum result** $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log(4)$ when asked about the value function. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ GAN = **Generative Adversarial Network**, introduced by **Ian Goodfellow et al. (2014)**.

- ✅ Two networks: **Generator ($G$)** and **Discriminator ($D$)** — trained in an **adversarial** min-max game.

- ✅ **Value function:** $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.

- ✅ The optimization is: $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$.

- ✅ **Optimal discriminator:** $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$.

- ✅ **Global optimum:** $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log(4)$ and $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ for all $x$ (i.e., the discriminator is *indifferent*).

- ✅ GAN training is equivalent to **minimizing the Jensen-Shannon divergence** $JS(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$.

- ✅ **JS divergence** is *symmetric* and bounded; **KL divergence** is *asymmetric*.

- ✅ **Vanishing gradient problem** in the original saturating generator loss is solved by the **non-saturating heuristic**: use $-\log D(G(z))$ instead of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$.

- ✅ **Training algorithm** alternates between $k$ updates to $D$ and **one** update to $G$.

- ✅ **Mode collapse** is a known failure mode — $G$ produces only a limited variety of samples.

- ✅ GANs are **implicit generative models** — they learn $p_g$ via *sampling*, not via an explicit likelihood.

- ✅ Practical variants include **DCGAN, LSGAN, WGAN, WGAN-GP, StyleGAN, CycleGAN** — each addresses different stability issues.

- ✅ The discriminator is essentially a **binary cross-entropy classifier** with a sigmoid output.

- ✅ **Differences from VAE:** GANs use *adversarial* loss, no encoder, no explicit likelihood. VAEs use *reconstruction + KL* loss and are *explicit* generative models.

- ✅ **Convergence criterion in theory:** $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D$ outputs $0.5$ for every input.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Final KTU Advice:** Always carry a clear **block diagram** in your exam. Always derive $D^*$ step-by-step. Always mention both the **saturating and non-saturating** generator losses. These are the most frequently tested components of GAN Basics in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of GANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of GANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** are unsupervised deep generative models introduced by *Ian Goodfellow et al. (2014)*, composed of two neural networks — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — trained simultaneously in a *minimax adversarial game*. The Generator learns to map a latent noise distribution $p_z(\mathbf{z})$ to a synthetic data distribution $p_g(\mathbf{x})$ that mimics the real data distribution $p_{data}(\mathbf{x})$, while the Discriminator learns to distinguish real samples from generated (fake) samples.

In the context of the KTU 2024 OEITT832 syllabus, **Applications of GANs** refer to the diverse downstream tasks where the trained Generator and/or Discriminator are deployed — including image synthesis, image-to-image translation, super-resolution, text-to-image generation, video synthesis, 3D model generation, data augmentation, anomaly detection, and domain adaptation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 of OEITT832 emphasizes the *practical deployment* of GANs beyond the base architecture. Students must be able to map a real-world problem to the correct GAN variant (cGAN, CycleGAN, SRGAN, StyleGAN, etc.) and articulate the loss function used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **counterfeit currency operation** vs. the **central bank inspector**:

- The **Generator** is a **forger** trying to print fake currency that looks real.
- The **Discriminator** is the **bank inspector** who has to decide whether a note is genuine or counterfeit.
- Initially, the forger makes crude fakes → the inspector easily catches them.
- The forger improves by learning from the inspector's feedback.
- The inspector, in turn, gets better at spotting subtle differences.
- Over time, the forger's fakes become **indistinguishable** from real currency.

This is the **adversarial training loop** — the forger (Generator) and the inspector (Discriminator) push each other to improve, until the fakes are virtually identical to real notes. Once training is complete, we keep the **Generator** as our "creative AI" that can synthesize brand new, realistic data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why applications matter:** The Generator, once trained, becomes a *sampling machine* — it can produce unlimited novel data samples. This single capability unlocks dozens of real-world engineering applications, which we explore in the next section.

## 1.3 Pre-Requisite Mathematical Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics and constants recur throughout GAN applications and must be memorized for the KTU board exam:

| Metric / Quantity | Notation | Description |
|-------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Inception Score** | **IS** | Measures quality and diversity using a pre-trained Inception network; higher is better |
| **Fréchet Inception Distance** | **FID** | Measures distance between generated and real feature distributions; **lower is better** |
| **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | **PSNR (dB)** | Pixel-level reconstruction quality; higher is better |
| **Structural Similarity Index** | **SSIM** | Perceptual similarity (range $[-1, 1]$); higher is better |
| **Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity** | **LPIPS** | Deep feature-based perceptual distance; lower is better |
| **Discriminator Output Range** | $\sigma(\cdot) \in (0,1)$ | Sigmoid squashing of logits |
| **Wasserstein-1 Distance** | $W(p_r, p_g)$ | Earth-Mover distance used in WGAN |
| **Gradient Penalty Coefficient** | $\lambda_{gp} = 10$ | Standard hyperparameter in WGAN-GP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorization of GAN Applications

GAN applications can be taxonomized along **three axes**: (i) output modality, (ii) conditional information, and (iii) architectural variant.

### A. By Output Modality
- **Image-domain** (synthesis, editing, super-resolution, inpainting, style transfer)
- **Video-domain** (forecasting, synthesis, frame interpolation)
- **3D-domain** (point clouds, meshes, NeRF-style radiance fields)
- **Audio/Speech-domain** (vocoder, music generation)
- **Text/Discrete-domain** (rare — replaced by autoregressive models or diffusion)

### B. By Conditional Information
- **Unconditional** — $G(\mathbf{z}) \rightarrow \mathbf{x}$ (vanilla GAN, DCGAN)
- **Class-conditional** — $G(\mathbf{z}, y) \rightarrow \mathbf{x}$ (cGAN, AC-GAN)
- **Image-conditional** — $G(\mathbf{z}, \mathbf{x}_{cond}) \rightarrow \mathbf{y}$ (Pix2Pix)
- **Text-conditional** — $G(\mathbf{z}, \text{text}) \rightarrow \mathbf{x}$ (StackGAN, AttnGAN)
- **Multimodal-conditional** — combinations of the above (AttnGAN)

### C. By Architectural Variant

| Variant | Year | Key Innovation | Primary Application |
|---------|------|----------------|---------------------|
| **DCGAN** | 2015 | Convolutional G and D with batch-norm | Image synthesis (CIFAR, LSUN) |
| **cGAN** | 2014 | Conditioning on label $y$ | Class-specific generation |
| **Pix2Pix** | 2017 | U-Net Generator + PatchGAN D | Paired image-to-image translation |
| **CycleGAN** | 2017 | Cycle-consistency loss | Unpaired image translation |
| **SRGAN** | 2017 | Perceptual + adversarial loss | Super-resolution |
| **StackGAN** | 2017 | Two-stage coarse-to-fine | Text-to-image (birds, flowers) |
| **StyleGAN** | 2019 | Style-based generator (AdaIN) | High-resolution face synthesis |
| **BigGAN** | 2018 | Large-scale class-conditional | ImageNet synthesis |
| **InfoGAN** | 2016 | Mutual-information maximization | Disentangled representation learning |
| **WGAN / WGAN-GP** | 2017 | Wasserstein loss + gradient penalty | Stable training |

## 2.2 Core Operating Principle — Adversarial Objective

The unified adversarial objective is:

$$
\min_{G} \max_{D} \; V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}} \left[ \log D(\mathbf{x}) \right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_z} \left[ \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}))) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Generator minimizes $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ (tries to fool D into outputting 1), while the Discriminator maximizes the probability of correctly classifying real vs. fake.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Loss Functions per Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value and conditional bars. These formulas are **must-know** for KTU ESE.

| # | Application / Variant | Loss Function | Key Symbol / Term |
|---|-----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Vanilla GAN** | $\mathcal{L}_D = -\big[\log D(\mathbf{x}) + \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))\big]$ | $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ |
| 2 | **Generator-only form** | $\mathcal{L}_G = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}} \big[-\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))\big]$ | "Non-saturating" trick |
| 3 | **Conditional GAN (cGAN)** | $\mathcal{L}_{cGAN} = \mathcal{L}_{GAN} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}(G(\mathbf{z},y), \mathbf{x})$ | $y$ = label/condition |
| 4 | **LSGAN** (Least Squares) | $\mathcal{L}_D = \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[(D(\mathbf{x})-1)^2] + \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[D(G(\mathbf{z}))^2]$ | Vanishing gradient fix |
| 5 | **WGAN** | $\mathcal{L}_D = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p_g}[D(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_r}[D(\mathbf{x})]$ | 1-Lipschitz via weight-clip |
| 6 | **WGAN-GP** | $\mathcal{L}_D = \mathcal{L}_{WGAN} + \lambda_{gp}\,\mathbb{E}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}\big[(\lVert \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} D(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) \rVert_2 - 1)^2\big]$ | $\lambda_{gp}=10$ |
| 7 | **Pix2Pix (paired)** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) + \lambda \lVert \mathbf{y} - G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \rVert_1$ | $\lambda = 100$ |
| 8 | **CycleGAN (unpaired)** | $\mathcal{L}_{cyc}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}\lVert F(G(\mathbf{x})) - \mathbf{x} \rVert_1 + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{y}}\lVert G(F(\mathbf{y})) - \mathbf{y} \rVert_1$ | Two generators $G, F$ |
| 9 | **SRGAN (perceptual)** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{percep} + 10^{-3}\mathcal{L}_{adv} + \mathcal{L}_{content}$ | VGG-feature MSE |
| 10 | **InfoGAN** | $\mathcal{L}_I = \mathcal{L}_{GAN} - \lambda \, I(\mathbf{c}; G(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{c}))$ | $I$ = mutual information |
| 11 | **StackGAN** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{GAN_1} + \mathcal{L}_{GAN_2} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{KL}$ | Conditioning Augmentation |
| 12 | **StyleGAN (style mixing)** | $\mathcal{w}' = \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{y}_s$ (AdaIN) | $\mathbf{y}_s$ = style vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **CycleGAN cycle-consistency loss** and the **Pix2Pix L1 + cGAN hybrid loss** — these appear in KTU 2024 Scheme past papers almost every cycle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Engineering Use Case |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| Medical Imaging | Synthesizing CT/MRI scans to augment rare-disease datasets |
| Autonomous Driving | Sim-to-real domain adaptation; rare hazard generation |
| Astronomy | Denoising telescope imagery; simulating exoplanet transits |
| Manufacturing | Defect data augmentation for quality-control classifiers |
| Drug Discovery | Generating novel molecular graphs (MolGAN) |
| Agriculture | Synthetic crop-disease images for precision-farming AI |
| Forensics | Super-resolving surveillance footage (SRGAN) |
| Entertainment | Deepfake generation, style transfer, character aging |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vanilla GAN Optimal Discriminator

We start from the minimax objective:

$$
\min_{G}\max_{D} V(D,G) = \int_{\mathbf{x}} p_{data}(\mathbf{x})\log D(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} + \int_{\mathbf{z}} p_z(\mathbf{z}) \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))\, d\mathbf{z}
$$

By change of variable $\mathbf{x} = G(\mathbf{z})$, the second integral becomes an integral over the generated distribution $p_g$:

$$
V(D,G) = \int_{\mathbf{x}} \Big[ p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x}) \log(1 - D(\mathbf{x})) \Big]\, d\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 1:** For a fixed Generator $G$, the optimal $D^*(\mathbf{x})$ is obtained by pointwise maximization of the integrand w.r.t. $D(\mathbf{x})$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate the integrand $f(D) = p_{data}\log D + p_g \log(1 - D)$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial D} = \frac{p_{data}(\mathbf{x})}{D(\mathbf{x})} - \frac{p_g(\mathbf{x})}{1 - D(\mathbf{x})} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $D^*(\mathbf{x})$:

$$
p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) (1 - D^*(\mathbf{x})) = p_g(\mathbf{x})\, D^*(\mathbf{x})
$$

$$
p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) - p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) D^*(\mathbf{x}) = p_g(\mathbf{x}) D^*(\mathbf{x})
$$

$$
p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) = D^*(\mathbf{x})\big[p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})\big]
$$

$$
D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{data}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When $p_{data} \gg p_g$, $D^* \to 1$ (real). When $p_g \gg p_{data}$, $D^* \to 0$ (fake). This is the **Bayes-optimal classifier** for the two distributions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Generator Loss at Equilibrium

Substitute $D^*(\mathbf{x})$ back into $V$:

$$
V(D^*, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}} \log\!\left[ \frac{p_{data}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_g} \log\!\left[ \frac{p_g(\mathbf{x})}{p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Define the Jensen-Shannon Divergence:

$$
\text{JSD}(P \,\vert\vert\, Q) = \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}\!\left(P \,\Big\vert\Big\vert\, \frac{P+Q}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}\!\left(Q \,\Big\vert\Big\vert\, \frac{P+Q}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** After algebraic substitution, the objective simplifies to:

$$
V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \,\vert\vert\, p_g)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Minimum:** When $p_g = p_{data}$, then $\text{JSD} = 0$ and $V(D^*, G) = -\log 4$. The Generator's training objective becomes equivalent to **minimizing the JSD between real and generated distributions**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conditional GAN (cGAN) Loss

For cGAN, both $G$ and $D$ receive an auxiliary input $y$ (label, image, text). The objective becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x},y}\big[\log D(\mathbf{x}, y)\big] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z},y}\big[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}, y), y))\big]
$$

**Step 6:** For paired image translation (Pix2Pix), a hybrid loss is used:

$$
G^* = \arg\min_{G} \max_{D} \mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) + \lambda \, \mathcal{L}_{L1}(G)
$$

where

$$
\mathcal{L}_{L1}(G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y},\mathbf{z}} \big[ \lVert \mathbf{y} - G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \rVert_1 \big]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why L1 and not L2?** L1 yields less blurring than L2 in image-to-image tasks, because L2 penalizes outliers quadratically, encouraging blurry mean predictions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CycleGAN Cycle-Consistency Loss

Let $G: X \to Y$ and $F: Y \to X$ be two generators. The cycle-consistency loss enforces that translating an image from $X$ to $Y$ and back returns the original:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{cyc}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}(X)} \big[ \lVert F(G(\mathbf{x})) - \mathbf{x} \rVert_1 \big] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{y} \sim p_{data}(Y)} \big[ \lVert G(F(\mathbf{y})) - \mathbf{y} \rVert_1 \big]
$$

**Step 7:** The full CycleGAN objective is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(G, F, D_X, D_Y) = \mathcal{L}_{GAN}(G, D_Y, X, Y) + \mathcal{L}_{GAN}(F, D_X, Y, X) + \lambda\, \mathcal{L}_{cyc}(G, F)
$$

where $\lambda = 10$ in the original paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works for unpaired data:** Without paired $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ examples, the adversarial loss alone is under-constrained (any $G$ that produces a valid $Y$ is accepted). The cycle-consistency penalty anchors $G$ to preserve *structural content* between domains.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Wasserstein-1 Distance for WGAN

The Wasserstein-1 (Earth-Mover) distance between two distributions $p_r$ and $p_g$ is:

$$
W(p_r, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p_r, p_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) \sim \gamma}\big[ \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \rVert \big]
$$

By the **Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality**, this can be written as:

$$
W(p_r, p_g) = \sup_{\lVert f \rVert_L \le 1} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_r}[f(\mathbf{x})] - \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p_g}[f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})]
$$

where the supremum is over all **1-Lipschitz** functions $f$. In WGAN, $f$ is parameterized by the critic $D_w$ (no sigmoid), and the loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim p_g}[D_w(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_r}[D_w(\mathbf{x})]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Wasserstein loss provides meaningful gradients even when $p_g$ and $p_r$ are disjoint — solving the *vanishing gradient* problem that plagued vanilla GANs.

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Numerical Illustration (SRGAN)

**Problem:** A $64 \times 64$ low-resolution image $\mathbf{x}_{LR}$ is upscaled by SRGAN to $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR}$. The ground truth is $\mathbf{x}_{HR}$. The perceptual loss is the MSE between VGG-19 features at layer $\ell=5$ (conv4_4):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{percep} = \frac{1}{W_\ell H_\ell C_\ell} \sum_{i,j,k} \big( \phi_{\ell}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR})_{i,j,k} - \phi_{\ell}(\mathbf{x}_{HR})_{i,j,k} \big)^2
$$

**Step 8:** Assume $W_\ell = H_\ell = 32$, $C_\ell = 512$. The total number of elements is $32 \times 32 \times 512 = 524{,}288$.

**Step 9:** Suppose the average squared feature difference is $0.01$. Then:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{percep} = \frac{0.01 \times 524288}{524288} = 0.01
$$

**Step 10:** Total SRGAN loss with $\lambda_{adv} = 10^{-3}$ and content weight $1$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total} = 0.01 + 10^{-3}\,(0.05) + (0.005) = 0.015
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Top-Level GAN Application Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GAN Applications] --> B[Image Domain]
    A --> C[Video Domain]
    A --> D[Cross Modal]
    A --> E[Scientific]

    B --> B1[Image Synthesis DCGAN StyleGAN BigGAN]
    B --> B2[Image to Image Pix2Pix CycleGAN]
    B --> B3[Super Resolution SRGAN ESRGAN]
    B --> B4[Image Inpainting Context Encoders]
    B --> B5[Face Editing AgeGAN StarGAN]

    C --> C1[Video Prediction GAN VideoGAN]
    C --> C2[Frame Interpolation]
    C --> C3[Deepfake Synthesis]

    D --> D1[Text to Image StackGAN AttnGAN]
    D --> D2[Audio to Visual]
    D --> D3[Sketch to Photo]

    E --> E1[Medical Synthesis]
    E --> E2[Astronomy Denoising]
    E --> E3[Molecule Generation MolGAN]
    E --> E4[3D Point Clouds]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Pix2Pix Paired Image-to-Image Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    condX[Input Image X] --> concat1
    noiseZ[Noise Z] --> concat1
    concat1 --> genG[Generator G U Net]
    genG --> fakeY[Generated Y]
    fakeY --> dpath1
    condX2[Input Image X] --> dpath2
    fakeY --> l1loss
    realY[Real Image Y] --> l1loss
    l1loss --> l1out[L1 Loss]
    dpath1 --> disD[Discriminator D PatchGAN]
    condX2 --> disD
    disD --> advout[Adversarial Loss]
    advout --> totloss
    l1out --> totloss
    totloss[Total Loss = LcGAN + lambda L1]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — CycleGAN Unpaired Translation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputX[Domain X Image] --> genG
    inputY[Domain Y Image] --> genF
    genG[Generator G X to Y] --> fakeY[Fake Y]
    genF[Generator F Y to X] --> fakeX[Fake X]
    fakeY --> dY[Discriminator DY]
    inputY --> dY
    fakeX --> dX[Discriminator DX]
    inputX --> dX
    fakeY --> genF
    genF --> reconX[Reconstructed X]
    inputX --> compareX
    reconX --> compareX[L1 Cycle Loss]
    fakeX --> genG
    genG --> reconY[Reconstructed Y]
    inputY --> compareY
    reconY --> compareY
    compareX --> totalloss
    compareY --> totalloss
    dY --> advlossY[Adversarial Loss Y]
    dX --> advlossX[Adversarial Loss X]
    advlossY --> totalloss
    advlossX --> totalloss
    totalloss[Total = LGAN_G + LGAN_F + lambda Lcyc]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — SRGAN Super-Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lrin[LR Image] --> srgen[SR Generator G]
    srgen --> srout[HR Image Estimate]
    hrreal[HR Ground Truth] --> vgg[VGG19 Feature Extractor]
    srout --> vgg
    vgg --> vggfeat[VGG Features]
    hrreal --> gtfeat[Ground Truth Features]
    vggfeat --> perloss[Perceptual Loss MSE]
    gtfeat --> perloss
    srout --> dis[Discriminator D]
    dis --> advloss[Adversarial Loss]
    hrreal --> contloss[Content Loss L2]
    srout --> contloss
    perloss --> totsr
    advloss --> totsr
    contloss --> totsr
    totsr[Total SR Loss]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Conditional GAN (cGAN) Training Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    noise[Latent Z] --> cgen[Generator G Z y]
    label[Condition y] --> cgen
    cgen --> fake[Fake Sample]
    real[Real Sample X] --> cdis[Discriminator D]
    label2[Condition y] --> cdis
    fake --> cdis
    cdis --> dprob[Probability Real or Fake]
    dprob --> dloss[Discriminator Loss]
    fake --> gloss[Generator Loss Non Saturating]
    label --> sup[Cone shaped Auxiliary Classifier]
    sup --> auxloss[Auxiliary Loss AC GAN]
    dloss --> backD[Backprop into D]
    gloss --> backG[Backprop into G]
    auxloss --> backG
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    real[Real Batch X] --> sampleR
    noise[Latent Z] --> genG2[Generator G]
    genG2 --> fake[Fake Batch]
    fake --> sampleF
    sampleR --> interp
    sampleF --> interp
    interp[Random Interpolation xhat] --> criticD[Critic D no sigmoid]
    criticD --> wloss[Wasserstein Loss]
    sampleR --> wloss
    sampleF --> wloss
    interp --> gradpen[Gradient Penalty term]
    gradpen --> wloss
    wloss --> wfinal[Total D Loss]
    wfinal --> clip[Weight Clipping or GP enforcement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Answer Strategy:** Always include a labelled block diagram in 14-mark answers. Examiners explicitly reward visual mapping of the data flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List any three practical applications of GANs and state the type of GAN variant used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

GANs are widely deployed across imaging, vision, and scientific computing. Three representative applications are:

1. **Image-to-Image Translation** — implemented using **Pix2Pix** for paired data (e.g., sketches to photos) and **CycleGAN** for unpaired data (e.g., horses to zebras). The architecture employs a U-Net Generator and a PatchGAN Discriminator with a hybrid adversarial + L1 reconstruction loss.

2. **Super-Resolution** — implemented using **SRGAN** or **ESRGAN**. The Generator upsamples a low-resolution image to high resolution; training uses a perceptual loss (VGG-feature MSE), an adversarial loss, and a pixel-level content loss.

3. **Text-to-Image Synthesis** — implemented using **StackGAN** or **AttnGAN**. A two-stage Generator first produces a low-resolution image from a text embedding, then refines it to high resolution. Conditioning Augmentation (CA) regularizes the text embedding space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (3-mark questions):** Students often write only "GAN is used for image generation" without specifying the *variant* and the *loss function*. The KTU board examiner expects a *named* architecture with its distinguishing feature. **Always name the variant (e.g., cGAN, CycleGAN, SRGAN) and the conditioning mechanism.**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper]
**Explain the role of the Discriminator in a Conditional GAN (cGAN). How does conditioning on label $y$ change the objective function?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **Conditional GAN (cGAN)**, the Discriminator $D$ receives both the data sample $\mathbf{x}$ and the auxiliary conditioning information $y$ (a class label, text embedding, or another image), and outputs $D(\mathbf{x}, y)$ — the probability that $\mathbf{x}$ is a *real* sample consistent with the condition $y$. Conditioning is implemented by concatenating $y$ to the input of $D$ (and likewise feeding $y$ to the Generator as an extra input channel or embedding).

The cGAN objective differs from vanilla GAN as:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{cGAN} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}, y \sim p_{data}}\big[\log D(\mathbf{x}, y)\big] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}, y \sim p_z, p_y}\big[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}, y), y))\big]
$$

The conditioning restricts the Generator to produce samples *conditioned on* $y$, enabling class-specific or attribute-controlled generation. The Discriminator also acts as a **consistency checker**, ensuring that the generated sample matches the intended condition.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the architecture and loss function of **CycleGAN** for unpaired image-to-image translation. Show how cycle-consistency loss enables training without paired data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **CycleGAN, Pix2Pix, and SRGAN** with respect to: (i) data requirement (paired/unpaired), (ii) Generator architecture, (iii) loss function, and (iv) typical application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Architecture Overview:** CycleGAN consists of two Generators ($G: X \to Y$ and $F: Y \to X$) and two Discriminators ($D_X$ and $D_Y$). Each Generator is a 9-block ResNet; each Discriminator is a 70×70 PatchGAN (from Pix2Pix).

**Step 2 — Adversarial Loss:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{GAN}(G, D_Y, X, Y) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{y} \sim p_{data}(Y)}[\log D_Y(\mathbf{y})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}(X)}[\log(1 - D_Y(G(\mathbf{x})))]
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_{GAN}(F, D_X, Y, X) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{data}(X)}[\log D_X(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{y} \sim p_{data}(Y)}[\log(1 - D_X(F(\mathbf{y})))]
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle-Consistency Loss:** This is the key innovation. It enforces $F(G(\mathbf{x})) \approx \mathbf{x}$ and $G(F(\mathbf{y})) \approx \mathbf{y}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{cyc}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}\lVert F(G(\mathbf{x})) - \mathbf{x} \rVert_1 + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{y}}\lVert G(F(\mathbf{y})) - \mathbf{y} \rVert_1
$$

**Step 4 — Total Objective:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(G, F, D_X, D_Y) = \mathcal{L}_{GAN}(G, D_Y) + \mathcal{L}_{GAN}(F, D_X) + \lambda\, \mathcal{L}_{cyc}(G, F)
$$

with $\lambda = 10$ as per the original paper.

**Step 5 — Why it works without paired data:** Without paired $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ examples, the adversarial loss alone is under-constrained. Cycle-consistency introduces a *structural anchor* — the Generator cannot simply produce an arbitrary valid $Y$ sample; it must also be *reversible*, preserving content like object shape, pose, and identity. The two generator–discriminator pairs jointly learn two domain mappings and a consistency regularizer.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

| Criterion | **Pix2Pix** | **CycleGAN** | **SRGAN** |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| (i) Data | Paired $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ required | Unpaired (two image sets) | Paired (LR, HR) image pairs |
| (ii) Generator | U-Net (encoder–decoder with skip) | 9-block ResNet, instance norm | 16-block ResNet + PixelShuffle upscale |
| (iii) Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + 100 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{L1}$ | $\mathcal{L}_{GAN_X} + \mathcal{L}_{GAN_Y} + 10 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{cyc}$ | $\mathcal{L}_{percep}^{VGG} + 10^{-3}\mathcal{L}_{adv} + \mathcal{L}_{content}$ |
| (iv) Application | Semantic labels → photo, day → night, sketch → photo | Horse ↔ zebra, summer ↔ winter, photo ↔ Monet | Image super-resolution (4× upscale) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **SRGAN (Super-Resolution GAN)** architecture and loss function. Derive the perceptual loss formula and explain why a pure MSE loss is insufficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **three real-world industrial applications** of GANs in domains such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, or data augmentation. For each, mention the specific GAN variant, the data modality, and the engineering benefit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Architecture:** SRGAN has two networks: a Generator $G$ (a deep ResNet of 16 residual blocks with skip connections, followed by two PixelShuffle upsampling blocks producing 4× upscaling) and a Discriminator $D$ (a VGG-style convolutional network that outputs a probability of the input being a real high-resolution image).

**Step 2 — Perceptual Loss Formula:** The perceptual loss is the MSE between VGG-19 (conv4_4, *before* activation) feature representations:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{percep}^{VGG/5,4} = \frac{1}{W_{i,j} H_{i,j} C_{i,j}} \sum_{x=1}^{W_{i,j}}\sum_{y=1}^{H_{i,j}}\sum_{c=1}^{C_{i,j}} \big( \phi_{i,j}(G(\mathbf{x}_{LR}))_{x,y,c} - \phi_{i,j}(\mathbf{x}_{HR})_{x,y,c} \big)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Adversarial Loss Component:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{adv} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} -\log D_\theta(G_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{LR}))
$$

**Step 4 — Content Loss (pixel-level):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{content} = \frac{1}{r^2 WH} \sum_{x=1}^{rW}\sum_{y=1}^{rH} (\mathbf{x}_{HR} - G(\mathbf{x}_{LR}))^2
$$

**Step 5 — Total SRGAN Loss:**

$$
