\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{percep} + 10^{-3}\, \mathcal{L}_{adv} + \mathcal{L}_{content}
$$

**Step 6 — Why MSE alone is insufficient:** Pixel-wise MSE (or PSNR-optimizing losses) yields overly smooth, blurry outputs because MSE penalizes large errors quadratically, so the optimal prediction is the *mean* of plausible high-resolution solutions. PSNR is high but the output lacks high-frequency texture (hair, pores, fine edges). The perceptual + adversarial loss pushes the Generator toward a *plausible* sharp output, producing results with much higher perceptual quality (and higher LPIPS reduction, often improving MOS by 1–2 points in human studies).

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Application 1: Medical Imaging — Data Augmentation**
- **Variant:** Conditional GAN (cGAN) or 3D-GAN.
- **Data Modality:** 2D/3D CT or MRI scans.
- **Benefit:** Synthesizes realistic tumor or lesion images to balance rare-disease datasets, addressing the chronic shortage of labelled medical images. Improves downstream classifier accuracy (e.g., tumor segmentation) by 5–15% in published benchmarks.

**Application 2: Autonomous Driving — Sim-to-Real Domain Adaptation**
- **Variant:** CycleGAN.
- **Data Modality:** Synthetic simulation frames (e.g., from CARLA) ↔ real road camera frames.
- **Benefit:** Translates simulated driving scenes into realistic ones at low cost, reducing the need for expensive real-world data collection in hazardous conditions (rain, fog, night). Provides rare-event (near-miss, accident) data for training safety-critical perception models.

**Application 3: Industrial Defect Detection — Synthetic Defect Generation**
- **Variant:** DCGAN or WGAN-GP.
- **Data Modality:** Manufacturing surface images (PCBs, steel, fabric).
- **Benefit:** Generates synthetic defect samples (cracks, scratches, dents) to augment a quality-control classifier. Crucial when real defect data is severely imbalanced (e.g., 1 defect per 10,000 normal images). Improves defect recall by 10–20% with no additional real defect collection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Do not** write only the architecture description in 1–2 lines — KTU expects a step-by-step derivation of the loss function (at least 3–4 lines of mathematical expansion).
> 2. **Always** state the *value of hyperparameters* (e.g., $\lambda = 10$ for CycleGAN, $\lambda = 100$ for Pix2Pix, $\lambda_{gp} = 10$ for WGAN-GP). Examiners reward numerical specifics.
> 3. For "comparison" sub-questions, **use a table** with at least 3 rows. Unstructured prose loses marks.
> 4. **Draw a block diagram** of the data flow for every architecture question. Marks are explicitly allocated for figures.
> 5. **Application questions require the GAN variant name** — generic answers like "GAN is used" get zero credit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of GANs (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Vanilla GAN objective:** $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$ with $\log D$ and $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$.
- ✅ **Optimal Discriminator:** $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p_{data}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{data}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}$.
- ✅ **At equilibrium:** $V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \,\vert\vert\, p_g)$.
- ✅ **cGAN** adds conditioning $y$ to both G and D; loss includes $\lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$ for paired translation.
- ✅ **Pix2Pix:** Paired, U-Net Generator, PatchGAN Discriminator, $\lambda = 100$ on L1.
- ✅ **CycleGAN:** Unpaired, 2 G + 2 D, cycle-consistency loss with $\lambda = 10$.
- ✅ **SRGAN:** Perceptual (VGG) + adversarial + content loss; weights $1, 10^{-3}, 1$.
- ✅ **WGAN:** Wasserstein-1 distance, critic (no sigmoid), 1-Lipschitz via weight clip or gradient penalty $\lambda_{gp} = 10$.
- ✅ **StackGAN:** Two-stage text-to-image; Stage-I 64×64, Stage-II 256×256; uses Conditioning Augmentation.
- ✅ **StyleGAN:** Disentangled style control via AdaIN; progressive growing to 1024×1024.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** **IS** ↑ (higher better), **FID** ↓ (lower better), **PSNR** ↑, **SSIM** ↑, **LPIPS** ↓.
- ✅ **Information-theoretic:** InfoGAN maximizes $I(\mathbf{c}; G(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{c}))$ for disentanglement.
- ✅ **Mode collapse** is a key failure mode — addressed by mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs, WGAN, or spectral normalization.
- ✅ **Standard KTU hyperparameters to memorize:** $\lambda = 100$ (Pix2Pix L1), $\lambda = 10$ (CycleGAN cycle), $\lambda_{gp} = 10$ (WGAN-GP), $10^{-3}$ (SRGAN adv weight).
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Medical synthesis, sim-to-real, defect augmentation, super-resolution, face editing, text-to-image, deepfakes, drug discovery.
- ✅ **Always include:** a labelled block diagram, the exact loss function form, and a named variant for any 14-mark application question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoencoders - Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autoencoders - Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autoencoder (AE)** is a specialized class of artificial neural networks designed for **unsupervised representation learning**. It consists of two mirrored, symmetric components:

1. An **Encoder** function $\phi : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$, which maps the original high-dimensional input $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a lower-dimensional latent representation $z \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ (where typically $m < n$).
2. A **Decoder** function $\psi : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$, which reconstructs the input from the latent code to produce an output $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

The network is trained to minimize a **reconstruction loss** $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$, thereby forcing the bottleneck layer to retain only the most salient features of the input distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Autoencoders are feedforward, non-recurrent neural networks that learn an efficient data encoding by attempting to reconstruct its own input as the target output, primarily used for dimensionality reduction, feature extraction, denoising, and generative modeling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Art Student & Sketchpad" Analogy**
> Imagine an art student who is asked to recreate a famous photograph (the *input*). However, she is only allowed to look at the photograph for **3 seconds** before it is taken away. To recreate it later, she must compress the most important visual information into a **tiny mental sketch** (the *latent code / bottleneck*). The sketch captures the *essence* (shapes, colors, composition) but discards unnecessary details (background noise, fine textures). When asked to draw the recreation, she uses the sketch to produce a *close approximation* of the original photo.
> 
> - The art student = the **Autoencoder**
> - The 3-second glance = the **Encoder** (compression stage)
> - The mental sketch = the **Latent Code ($z$)** (bottleneck representation)
> - The recreation drawing = the **Decoder** (reconstruction stage)
> - The difference between the recreation and the original = **Reconstruction Loss**

### 1.3 Why Autoencoders? — Engineering Motivation

Autoencoders are the cornerstone of **representation learning** in modern deep learning. They solve a critical engineering problem: how to compress data automatically without requiring hand-crafted features (unlike PCA or LDA). Their applications include:

- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Non-linear counterpart to Principal Component Analysis (PCA).
- **Anomaly Detection:** A well-trained AE reconstructs *normal* data with low error; outliers yield high error.
- **Denoising:** Learning to reconstruct clean images from corrupted inputs.
- **Generative Modeling:** Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) form the basis of modern generative AI pipelines.
- **Pretraining:** Stacked autoencoders historically pioneered deep network initialization.

> [!TIP]
> **Core Design Principle:** The network is intentionally *constrained* (through bottleneck size, sparsity, or noise injection) so that it cannot trivially learn the identity function. This constraint is what forces meaningful learning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components — The "Three Blocks"

The autoencoder architecture is composed of **three canonical blocks**:

| Block | Symbol | Function | Input Dimension | Output Dimension |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Encoder** | $\phi$ | Compresses input to latent space | $n$ | $m$ (where $m \le n$) |
| **Bottleneck / Code** | $z$ | Compact latent representation | $m$ | $m$ |
| **Decoder** | $\psi$ | Reconstructs input from code | $m$ | $n$ |

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

The encoder performs a deterministic mapping:

$$z = \phi(x) = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$$

where:
- $W_e \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the encoder weight matrix.
- $b_e \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the encoder bias vector.
- $\sigma_e$ is the encoder activation function (commonly ReLU, sigmoid, or tanh).

The decoder reconstructs the input:

$$\hat{x} = \psi(z) = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$$

where:
- $W_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the decoder weight matrix.
- $b_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the decoder bias vector.
- $\sigma_d$ is the decoder activation function.

The full network is the composition $\psi \circ \phi$, parameterized by $\theta = \{W_e, b_e, W_d, b_d\}$.

### 2.3 Loss Function Engineering

The objective is to minimize reconstruction error. The two most common loss functions are:

1. **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — for continuous data (e.g., normalized images):

$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

2. **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** — for binary data (e.g., binarized images with sigmoid output):

$$\mathcal{L}_{BCE}(x, \hat{x}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ x_i \log(\hat{x}_i) + (1 - x_i) \log(1 - \hat{x}_i) \right]$$

The training objective is:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(x, \psi(\phi(x)))$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Encoder Mapping** | $z = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$ | Compresses input to latent space |
| 2 | **Decoder Mapping** | $\hat{x} = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$ | Reconstructs input from latent code |
| 3 | **MSE Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$ | Continuous data reconstruction |
| 4 | **BCE Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum [x \log \hat{x} + (1-x) \log(1-\hat{x})]$ | Binary data reconstruction |
| 5 | **Bottleneck Constraint** | $\dim(z) < \dim(x)$ | Forces undercomplete representation |
| 6 | **Tied Weights** | $W_d = W_e^\top$ | Weight sharing for regularization |
| 7 | **Sparse Penalty** | $\Omega(z) = \sum_j \mathrm{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ | L1 / KL-divergence sparsity term |
| 8 | **Total Loss (Sparse AE)** | $\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \lambda \cdot \Omega(z)$ | Reconstruction + sparsity penalty |

### 2.5 Taxonomy of Autoencoder Variants (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Constraint Mechanism | Primary Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Undercomplete AE** | $\dim(z) < \dim(x)$ | Dimensionality reduction |
| **Sparse AE** | Sparsity penalty on activations | Feature selection, interpretability |
| **Denoising AE** | Corruption of input, reconstruct clean version | Robust feature learning, denoising |
| **Contractive AE** | Jacobian penalty $\Vert \nabla_x \phi(x) \Vert^2$ | Locally invariant features |
| **Variational AE (VAE)** | Probabilistic latent space (KL regularization) | Generative modeling |
| **Convolutional AE** | Convolutional encoder/decoder | Image data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the **semiconductor industry**, autoencoders are used for defect detection in wafer fabrication. A well-trained AE reconstructs *normal* chip patterns; defective wafers produce high reconstruction error, enabling automated quality control. In **medical imaging**, denoising autoencoders reconstruct clean MRI scans from noisy low-radiation inputs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Forward Pass Derivation (Layer-wise)

Consider a **stacked (deep) autoencoder** with $L$ encoder layers and $L$ decoder layers. Let the input be $x = h^{(0)}$.

**Step 1 — Encoder Layer 1:**

$$h^{(1)} = \sigma\left(W^{(1)} h^{(0)} + b^{(1)}\right)$$

This compresses the original input to a slightly lower-dimensional space. The activation function $\sigma$ (e.g., ReLU) introduces non-linearity.

**Step 2 — Encoder Layer 2 (and onwards):**

$$h^{(l)} = \sigma\left(W^{(l)} h^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}\right), \quad l = 1, 2, \dots, L$$

Each successive layer progressively extracts higher-level, more abstract features.

**Step 3 — Bottleneck / Latent Code:**

$$z = h^{(L)} = \sigma\left(W^{(L)} h^{(L-1)} + b^{(L)}\right)$$

This is the **compressed representation** — the lowest-dimensional point in the network.

**Step 4 — Decoder Layer 1 (mirror begins):**

$$h^{(L+1)} = \sigma\left(W^{(L+1)} z + b^{(L+1)}\right)$$

Note: $W^{(L+1)}$ typically has transposed dimensions relative to $W^{(L)}$.

**Step 5 — Decoder Layer $l$ (general):**

$$h^{(L+l)} = \sigma\left(W^{(L+l)} h^{(L+l-1)} + b^{(L+l)}\right), \quad l = 1, 2, \dots, L$$

**Step 6 — Reconstruction Output:**

$$\hat{x} = \sigma_{out}\left(W^{(2L)} h^{(2L-1)} + b^{(2L)}\right)$$

The output activation $\sigma_{out}$ is typically:
- **Sigmoid** for binary cross-entropy loss.
- **Linear (identity)** for MSE loss.

### 3.2 Backpropagation Derivation Through the Architecture

The autoencoder is trained using **gradient descent**. We derive the gradients of the MSE loss with respect to the parameters.

**Step 1 — Define the loss for a single sample:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient of loss w.r.t. output $\hat{x}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} = - (x_i - \hat{x}_i) = \hat{x}_i - x_i$$

**Step 3 — Propagate through the output layer activation:**

$$\delta^{(2L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} \odot \sigma_{out}'(\hat{x})$$

where $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**. This is the standard error signal at the output layer.

**Step 4 — Backpropagate through decoder layers (for $l = 2L-1, \dots, L+1$):**

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'\left(z^{(l)}\right)$$

This propagates the error signal backwards through the decoder.

**Step 5 — Cross the bottleneck:** The error signal continues into the encoder:

$$\delta^{(L)} = \left( W^{(L+1)\top} \delta^{(L+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'\left(z^{(L)}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Backpropagate through encoder layers (for $l = L-1, \dots, 1$):**

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'\left(z^{(l)}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Compute parameter gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \cdot z^{(l-1)\top}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

**Step 8 — Apply gradient descent update:**

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

$$b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typical values: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Sparse Autoencoder Penalty

For a **Sparse Autoencoder**, we add a penalty term to the loss. Let $\hat{\rho}_j$ be the average activation of hidden unit $j$ across the training batch:

$$\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_j^{(1)}(x_i)$$

We enforce $\hat{\rho}_j = \rho$ (a small sparsity parameter, e.g., $\rho = 0.05$) using **KL-divergence**:

$$\Omega(z) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathrm{KL}\left(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j} \right]$$

The total loss becomes:

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \lambda \cdot \Omega(z)$$

where $\lambda$ is the **sparsity weight** controlling the trade-off.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Architectural Design Choices

| Design Decision | Options | Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bottleneck size** | Small (high compression) vs. Large (mild compression) | Smaller = more abstract features, higher reconstruction error |
| **Depth (# of layers)** | Shallow (2-3 layers) vs. Deep (5+ layers) | Deeper = more powerful but risk of vanishing gradients |
| **Activation (encoder)** | ReLU (sparse, fast) vs. Tanh (zero-centered) vs. Sigmoid | ReLU preferred for hidden layers |
| **Output activation** | Sigmoid (BCE) vs. Linear (MSE) | Matches data distribution |
| **Weight tying** | $W_d = W_e^\top$ vs. independent | Tying reduces parameters, acts as regularizer |
| **Regularization** | L2 weight decay vs. Sparsity vs. Denoising | Prevents overfitting, encourages robust features |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputX["Input X n-dim vector"]:::inputStyle
    EncDense1["Encoder Layer 1 Linear plus ReLU"]:::encStyle
    EncDense2["Encoder Layer 2 Linear plus ReLU"]:::encStyle
    EncDense3["Encoder Layer 3 Linear plus ReLU"]:::encStyle
    Bottleneck["Bottleneck Latent Code z m-dim m less than n"]:::bottleneckStyle
    DecDense1["Decoder Layer 1 Linear plus ReLU"]:::decStyle
    DecDense2["Decoder Layer 2 Linear plus ReLU"]:::decStyle
    DecDense3["Decoder Layer 3 Linear plus Sigmoid"]:::decStyle
    OutputXHat["Reconstruction X-hat n-dim"]:::outputStyle
    LossNode["Reconstruction Loss MSE or BCE"]:::lossStyle

    InputX --> EncDense1
    EncDense1 --> EncDense2
    EncDense2 --> EncDense3
    EncDense3 --> Bottleneck
    Bottleneck --> DecDense1
    DecDense1 --> DecDense2
    DecDense2 --> DecDense3
    DecDense3 --> OutputXHat
    OutputXHat --> LossNode
    InputX -.Compare.-> LossNode
    LossNode -.Gradients backprop.-> EncDense1
    LossNode -.Gradients backprop.-> DecDense1

    classDef inputStyle fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef encStyle fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#145214,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bottleneckStyle fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0d0d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef decStyle fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#7a3d05,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputStyle fill:#9467bd,stroke:#4a2e7a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lossStyle fill:#8c564b,stroke:#4a2c25,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENC["ENCODER phi"]
        direction TB
        E1["Input Layer n neurons"] --> E2["Hidden Layer 1 n1 neurons"] --> E3["Hidden Layer 2 n2 neurons"] --> E4["Code Layer m neurons"]
    end

    subgraph DEC["DECODER psi"]
        direction TB
        D1["Hidden Layer 2 n2 neurons"] --> D2["Hidden Layer 1 n1 neurons"] --> D3["Output Layer n neurons"]
    end

    E4 --> D1
    E4 -.Code z.-> D1

    classDef encBlock fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#145214,color:#ffffff
    classDef decBlock fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#7a3d05,color:#ffffff
    class E1,E2,E3,E4 encBlock
    class D1,D2,D3 decBlock
```

### 4.3 Architecture Schematic Description (Text-Based)

The autoencoder follows a **symmetric hourglass topology**:

```
INPUT  →  [ENC-1]  →  [ENC-2]  →  [ENC-3]  →  [CODE]  →  [DEC-3]  →  [DEC-2]  →  [DEC-1]  →  OUTPUT
n dim       n1          n2           n3            m          n3           n2           n1           n dim
   ────────────────────────────────────────────────────╲╱───────────────────────────────────────────
                                                    BOTTLENECK
                                                  (LATENT SPACE)
```

- **Dimensionality progression:** $n > n_1 > n_2 > n_3 > m < n_3 < n_2 < n_1 < n$
- The narrowest point is the **bottleneck** or **latent code $z$**.
- The encoder and decoder are typically **mirror images** in layer dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, May 2024]**

Define an **autoencoder**. With a neat diagram, explain the role of the **encoder**, **bottleneck**, and **decoder** in the autoencoder architecture.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **autoencoder** is an unsupervised neural network trained to reconstruct its input at the output by learning a compressed latent representation. It consists of three parts:

1. **Encoder $\phi$:** Compresses the input $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ into a lower-dimensional latent code $z \in \mathbb{R}^m$ via $z = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$.
2. **Bottleneck / Code $z$:** The compact, information-rich representation at the network's narrowest point, where $m < n$.
3. **Decoder $\psi$:** Reconstructs the input from the code as $\hat{x} = \sigma(W_d z + b_d)$.

The network minimizes reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between **undercomplete** and **sparse** autoencoders. How does each enforce learning of useful features?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Undercomplete AE | Sparse AE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Constraint** | Bottleneck dimension $m < n$ | Sparsity penalty on activations |
| **Mechanism** | Compresses input by limiting code size | Forces most neurons to be inactive |
| **Effect** | Learns most salient compressed features | Learns distributed, selective features even when $m \ge n$ |

Both prevent the network from trivially learning the identity function, forcing it to extract meaningful patterns.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, April 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **forward pass equations** of a deep autoencoder with 3 encoder layers and 3 decoder layers. Clearly define the bottleneck and the output layer activation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **backpropagation training algorithm** for the autoencoder, including the gradient of the MSE loss w.r.t. the output and the parameter update rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Pass Derivation (7 Marks):**

Let the input be $x = h^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Encoder layers** (progressively compress):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(1)} &= \sigma\left(W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}\right) \quad \text{[Layer 1: } n \to n_1 \text{]} \\
h^{(2)} &= \sigma\left(W^{(2)} h^{(1)} + b^{(2)}\right) \quad \text{[Layer 2: } n_1 \to n_2 \text{]} \\
h^{(3)} &= \sigma\left(W^{(3)} h^{(2)} + b^{(3)}\right) \quad \text{[Layer 3: } n_2 \to m \text{]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **bottleneck** is $z = h^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^m$, where $m \ll n$.

**Decoder layers** (progressively reconstruct):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(4)} &= \sigma\left(W^{(4)} z + b^{(4)}\right) \quad \text{[Layer 4: } m \to n_2 \text{]} \\
h^{(5)} &= \sigma\left(W^{(5)} h^{(4)} + b^{(5)}\right) \quad \text{[Layer 5: } n_2 \to n_1 \text{]} \\
\hat{x} &= \sigma_{out}\left(W^{(6)} h^{(5)} + b^{(6)}\right) \quad \text{[Layer 6: } n_1 \to n \text{]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output activation choices:**
- Sigmoid $\sigma_{out}(y) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-y}}$ for BCE loss with binary inputs.
- Linear (identity) for MSE loss with continuous inputs.

**(b) Backpropagation Derivation (7 Marks):**

The MSE loss for a single sample is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

**Step 1 — Output layer error signal:**

$$\delta^{(6)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} \odot \sigma_{out}'(\hat{x}) = (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma_{out}'(\hat{x})$$

**Step 2 — Backpropagate through decoder (layers 5, 4):**

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left(W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)}\right) \odot \sigma'(h^{(l)}), \quad l = 5, 4$$

**Step 3 — Cross the bottleneck:**

$$\delta^{(3)} = \left(W^{(4)\top} \delta^{(4)}\right) \odot \sigma'(z)$$

**Step 4 — Backpropagate through encoder (layers 2, 1):**

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left(W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)}\right) \odot \sigma'(h^{(l)}), \quad l = 2, 1$$

**Step 5 — Parameter gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (h^{(l-1)})^\top, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

**Step 6 — Gradient descent update with learning rate $\eta$:**

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \delta^{(l)} (h^{(l-1)})^\top$$

$$b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \delta^{(l)}$$

This is iterated across all training samples (minibatch gradient descent) until convergence.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, April 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a denoising autoencoder**. How does it differ from a basic autoencoder, and what is the training objective? Derive the modified loss function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **sparse autoencoder** architecture. Derive the sparsity penalty using **KL-divergence** and explain how the total loss combines reconstruction and sparsity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Denoising Autoencoder (7 Marks):**

A **Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)** is trained to reconstruct a *clean* version of the input from a *corrupted* version. This forces the network to learn robust, invariant features rather than a trivial identity mapping.

**Architecture:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x} &\sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x) \quad \text{[Corruption process, e.g., Gaussian noise or masking]} \\
z &= \sigma_e(W_e \tilde{x} + b_e) \quad \text{[Encoder operates on corrupted input]} \\
\hat{x} &= \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d) \quad \text{[Decoder reconstructs clean input]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Differences from basic AE:**
- Input is corrupted via $q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$ (e.g., random masking of 20% of pixels, additive Gaussian noise).
- Reconstruction target is the **original clean $x$**, not the corrupted $\tilde{x}$.

**Modified loss function:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{DAE}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}} \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x)} \left[ \Vert x - \psi(\phi(\tilde{x})) \Vert^2 \right]$$

The network learns a **manifold** of clean data by denoising, useful in medical imaging, speech enhancement, and robust feature learning.

**(b) Sparse Autoencoder (7 Marks):**

A **Sparse Autoencoder** adds a regularization term that penalizes the average activation of hidden units, forcing most units to be inactive for any given input — even when the bottleneck is large.

**Step 1 — Average activation of unit $j$:**

$$\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_j(x_i)$$

where $N$ is the batch size. The target sparsity is $\rho$ (a small value, e.g., $0.05$).

**Step 2 — KL-divergence penalty:**

$$\Omega(z) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathrm{KL}\left(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Combined loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^2}_{\text{Reconstruction}} + \lambda \cdot \underbrace{\Omega(z)}_{\text{Sparsity}}$$

where $\lambda$ is the sparsity weight.

**Intuition:** KL-divergence is zero when $\hat{\rho}_j = \rho$ and increases as the actual activation diverges from the target. This forces each hidden unit to fire on average only $\rho$ fraction of the time, yielding a **distributed sparse code** — a hallmark of efficient biological neural systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing encoder/decoder dimensions:** Students often write encoder as $n \to n$ and decoder as $m \to m$. Always state that encoder *reduces* dimensionality and decoder *restores* it.
> 2. **Skipping the bottleneck constraint:** The relationship $m < n$ (for undercomplete AE) **must** be explicitly stated for full marks.
> 3. **Wrong loss-output activation pairing:** MSE $\leftrightarrow$ Linear output; BCE $\leftrightarrow$ Sigmoid output. Mismatching these causes incorrect gradient derivations.
> 4. **Forgetting the activation derivative in backprop:** The chain rule term $\sigma'(h^{(l)})$ is frequently omitted in $\delta^{(l)}$ computation — this is a guaranteed 1-2 mark loss.
> 5. **Treating DAEs and basic AEs identically:** The corruption step $\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \mid x)$ is the *defining* feature of denoising AEs and must be explicitly shown.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Autoencoder Architecture**

- [ ] **Definition:** Autoencoder = Encoder + Bottleneck + Decoder; trained to reconstruct input.
- [ ] **Three blocks:** Encoder $\phi$, Code $z$, Decoder $\psi$. Network is $\psi(\phi(x))$.
- [ ] **Dimensionality flow:** Input $n \to$ Code $m \to$ Output $n$, with $m < n$ for undercomplete AE.
- [ ] **Encoder equation:** $z = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$.
- [ ] **Decoder equation:** $\hat{x} = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$.
- [ ] **MSE loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$ — used for continuous data.
- [ ] **BCE loss:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum [x \log \hat{x} + (1-x) \log(1-\hat{x})]$ — used for binary data.
- [ ] **Backprop error signal:** $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot \sigma'(h^{(l)})$.
- [ ] **Parameter gradient:** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (h^{(l-1)})^\top$.
- [ ] **Update rule:** $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \cdot \delta^{(l)} (h^{(l-1)})^\top$.
- [ ] **Undercomplete AE:** Constraint via bottleneck size $m < n$.
- [ ] **Sparse AE:** Constraint via KL-divergence penalty $\mathrm{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$.
- [ ] **Denoising AE:** Constraint via input corruption; reconstructs clean $x$ from $\tilde{x}$.
- [ ] **Tied weights:** $W_d = W_e^\top$ — reduces parameters, acts as regularizer.
- [ ] **Output activation:** Sigmoid for BCE; Linear for MSE.
- [ ] **Hidden activation:** ReLU preferred (avoids vanishing gradients).
- [ ] **Symmetric hourglass topology** is the standard architecture.
- [ ] **Applications:** Dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection, denoising, generative modeling, pretraining.
- [ ] **Difference from PCA:** Autoencoders learn **non-linear** mappings; PCA is restricted to linear projections.
- [ ] **Why it doesn't learn identity:** Bottleneck / sparsity / noise constraints prevent trivial copying.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Learning and Fine-Tuning - Pre-trained Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transfer Learning and Fine-Tuning - Pre-trained Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4: Transfer Learning and Fine-Tuning – Pre-trained Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> **Transfer Learning (TL)** is a machine learning paradigm in which a model developed for a *source task* (with a source domain $D_S$) is reused as the starting point for a model on a *related target task* (with target domain $D_T$), thereby transferring knowledge to improve learning efficiency and generalization on the target task.

In the context of Deep Learning, this almost always means: take a **Pre-trained Model** (a network already trained on a massive dataset like ImageNet, COCO, or a large text corpus) and adapt it to a new, often smaller, dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-trained Model:** A neural network whose weights have already been learned from a *very large benchmark dataset* and can serve as a powerful initialization or feature extractor for downstream tasks.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **mechanical engineer** who has spent 10 years mastering the principles of gears, levers, and torque.

Now you are asked to design a **robot arm**.

- **Without Transfer Learning:** You would have to re-learn physics from scratch, starting from "what is force?", which is incredibly slow and wasteful.
- **With Transfer Learning:** You *transfer* your knowledge of mechanics, adapt a few new ideas (motor control, sensors), and quickly master robotics. Your prior experience (the "pre-trained" part) gives you a massive head start.

In Deep Learning:
- The "engineer" = the neural network.
- The "years of mechanics" = millions of training images (e.g., ImageNet with 14 million images).
- The "robot arm" = your small custom dataset (e.g., 500 X-ray images).

### Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> - **Source Domain $D_S$:** The original large dataset (e.g., ImageNet).
> - **Target Domain $D_T$:** The new, smaller dataset we want to solve.
> - **Source Task $T_S$:** The original task (e.g., classifying 1000 ImageNet categories).
> - **Target Task $T_T$:** The new task (e.g., classifying cats vs. dogs).
> - **Fine-Tuning:** The process of lightly adjusting the pre-trained weights on the new dataset.
> - **Feature Extraction:** Freezing the convolutional base and only training a new classifier head.

### Standard Pre-trained Model Benchmarks (Keras Applications)

| Model | Year | Top-5 Acc (ImageNet) | Params (M) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **VGG-16** | 2014 | 90.1% | 138 |
| **ResNet-50** | 2015 | 92.9% | 25.6 |
| **InceptionV3** | 2015 | 93.7% | 23.8 |
| **MobileNetV2** | 2018 | 91.5% | 3.5 |
| **EfficientNet-B0** | 2019 | 93.5% | 5.3 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 Why Do We Need Transfer Learning?

Deep Neural Networks require **massive amounts of labeled data** and **enormous compute** (GPUs/TPUs) to train from random initialization. For most real-world KTU-level problems, we do not have:
1. 1.2 million labeled images.
2. Multiple GPUs running for weeks.
3. A team of research scientists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (Why it works):** Early and middle layers of CNNs learn *general, universal features* (edges, textures, shapes, object parts). Only the final layers learn *task-specific* features (e.g., "cat face" vs "dog face"). Therefore, the early layers are essentially **transferable** across tasks.

### ⚙️ The Two Main Transfer Learning Strategies

**Strategy 1: Feature Extraction (Frozen Base)**
- **Concept:** Treat the pre-trained CNN as a fixed, off-the-shelf feature extractor.
- **Mechanics:** Freeze all convolutional base layers. Remove the original classifier head. Add a new, randomly initialized classifier head matching the number of target classes. Train *only* the new head.
- **Pros:** Fast, low compute, prevents overfitting on small datasets.
- **Cons:** Cannot adapt low-level features to new domain.

**Strategy 2: Fine-Tuning (Full or Partial)**
- **Concept:** Unfreeze some (or all) layers of the pre-trained model and jointly train them with the new classifier head.
- **Mechanics:** Replace the classifier head, set a *low* learning rate ($\eta \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$), and continue training (backpropagation) to gently adjust weights without "catastrophically forgetting" prior knowledge.
- **Pros:** Higher accuracy, adapts features to new domain.
- **Cons:** More compute, risk of overfitting on small data.

### 🎚️ Fine-Tuning Variations

> [!NOTE]
> **Discriminative Learning Rates (Differential LR):** Use a *smaller* learning rate for early layers and a *larger* learning rate for later layers. Rationale: early features are already well-learned and need little change; later, task-specific features need more adaptation.

$$\eta_{layer_i} = \eta_{base} \times \text{decay}^{(N - i)}$$

Where $N$ is the total number of layers, and $i$ is the index of the current layer being updated.

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept / Symbol | Formula / Definition | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transfer Learning Objective** | Minimize $L_{target}(\theta) = L(f_\theta(x_T), y_T)$ starting from $\theta \leftarrow \theta_{pretrained}$ instead of random init. | Saves training time and compute. |
| **Catastrophic Forgetting** | $\Delta \theta = -\eta \nabla L_T$ — large LR destroys pre-trained knowledge. | Use $\eta \leq 10^{-4}$ in fine-tuning. |
| **Discriminative LR** | $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{L-l}$ | Stabilizes fine-tuning. |
| **Top-k Accuracy** | $\text{Top-k} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(y_i \in \text{top-k predictions})$ | Standard pre-trained model metric. |
| **ReLU Activation (feature maps)** | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Universal in CNN backbones. |
| **Softmax Classifier Head** | $P(y=k \vert x) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Output layer for new head. |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Training loss for the new head. |
| **Weight Freezing** | $\theta_l^{(t+1)} = \theta_l^{(t)}$ (no update for $l \in \text{frozen}$) | Used in feature extraction. |

### 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Fine-tuning ImageNet-pretrained ResNet on a small dataset of MRI scans to detect tumors.
- **NLP:** BERT / GPT pre-trained on billions of words, fine-tuned for sentiment analysis in Malayalam.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Pre-trained object detection models adapted for Indian road conditions.
- **Agriculture:** Identifying crop diseases in Kerala using a MobileNet (small) pre-trained backbone for edge deployment on Raspberry Pi.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📐 Mathematical Formulation of Transfer Learning

**Step 1: Define the Two Domains**
Let the source domain be $D_S$ with distribution $P_S(x, y)$ and the target domain be $D_T$ with distribution $P_T(x, y)$. The fundamental assumption of TL is:

$$P_S(x, y) \neq P_T(x, y) \quad \text{but} \quad \text{features and concepts overlap}.$$

**Step 2: Decompose a Pre-trained CNN**

A pre-trained CNN $f_\theta$ can be viewed as a composition:

$$f_\theta(x) = C_{\theta_C} \circ B_{\theta_B}(x)$$

Where:
- $B_{\theta_B}$ is the **convolutional base** (feature extractor), parameterized by $\theta_B$.
- $C_{\theta_C}$ is the **classifier head**, parameterized by $\theta_C$.

**Step 3: Pre-trained Initialization**

After pre-training on $D_S$, we have optimal weights:

$$\theta_B^*, \theta_C^* = \arg\min_{\theta_B, \theta_C} \mathcal{L}_S(B_{\theta_B}, C_{\theta_C}, D_S)$$

**Step 4: Transfer & Fine-Tune on $D_T$**

We discard $\theta_C^*$ and replace with a randomly initialized $\theta_C^{new}$ matching the number of target classes $K_T$. We then minimize:

$$\theta_B', \theta_C^{new} = \arg\min_{\theta_B, \theta_C^{new}} \mathcal{L}_T(C_{\theta_C^{new}} \circ B_{\theta_B}, D_T)$$

with initial conditions $\theta_B \leftarrow \theta_B^*$ and a small learning rate $\eta \ll \eta_{pretrain}$.

### 🧮 Derivation: Discriminative Learning Rate Schedule

**Step 1:** Define the base learning rate $\eta_0$ (e.g., $10^{-3}$) and a decay factor $\alpha$ (e.g., $0.95$).

**Step 2:** For a network with $L$ total trainable layers, the learning rate at layer $l$ is:

$$\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{L - l}$$

**Step 3:** Expand for $L = 3$ layers with $\alpha = 0.5, \eta_0 = 10^{-3}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_1 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{3-1} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.25 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_2 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{3-2} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5  = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_3 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{3-3} = 10^{-3} \cdot 1    = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Early layer (1) gets the smallest updates to preserve pre-trained knowledge; the last layer (3) gets the largest updates to adapt to the new task.

### 📈 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Dataset Size | Domain Similarity | Recommended Strategy |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| A | Small (<1k) | High | Feature Extraction (freeze base) |
| B | Small (<1k) | Low | Feature Extraction + aggressive regularization |
| C | Large (>10k) | High | Fine-tune entire network with small LR |
| D | Large (>10k) | Low | Fine-tune from scratch is competitive, but TL still helps |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A common mistake is using a *high* learning rate during fine-tuning. This causes **Catastrophic Forgetting**, where the network suddenly loses the carefully pre-trained features and accuracy crashes. Always use $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$.

### 🔄 Cross-Entropy Loss Behavior During Fine-Tuning

The training loss $L_{CE}$ should ideally show:

$$L_{CE}^{(t=0)} \gg L_{CE}^{(t=T_{train})}$$

That is, a steep descent curve during the first few epochs as the new classifier head learns the target classes, followed by a gentle refinement as the backbone adjusts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1: Transfer Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Large Source Dataset e.g. ImageNet] --> B[Pre-trained Model e.g. ResNet50]
    B --> C[Trained Weights: theta_star]
    C --> D[Transfer to Target Task]
    D --> E[Freeze Convolutional Base]
    E --> F[Replace Classifier Head]
    F --> G[Add New Dense Layer for K_T classes]
    G --> H[Train on Small Target Dataset]
    H --> I[Optional: Unfreeze last few layers]
    I --> J[Fine-tune with small learning rate]
    J --> K[Final Deployed Model]
```

### 🏗️ Diagram 2: Feature Extraction vs Fine-Tuning Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Method1[Strategy 1: Feature Extraction]
        F1[Input Image] --> F2[Convolutional Base FROZEN]
        F2 --> F3[New Classifier Head TRAINABLE]
        F3 --> F4[Predictions]
        style F2 fill:#ffcccb
        style F3 fill:#90ee90
    end
    
    subgraph Method2[Strategy 2: Fine-Tuning]
        S1[Input Image] --> S2[Convolutional Base TRAINABLE low LR]
        S2 --> S3[New Classifier Head TRAINABLE high LR]
        S3 --> S4[Predictions]
        style S2 fill:#90ee90
        style S3 fill:#90ee90
    end
```

### 🔁 Diagram 3: Discriminative Learning Rate Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Learning Rate eta_0] --> B[Layer N: Highest LR]
    A --> C[Layer N-1: eta_0 times alpha]
    A --> D[Layer 2: eta_0 times alpha sup N-2]
    A --> E[Layer 1: Lowest LR alpha sup N-1]
    B -.-> F1[Gentle updates]
    E -.-> F2[Minimal updates]
    style B fill:#ff9999
    style E fill:#99ccff
```

### 📊 Diagram 4: Pre-trained Model Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[ImageNet Pre-trained Models]
    Root --> Classic[Classic: VGG-16, VGG-19]
    Root --> Residual[Residual: ResNet-50, ResNet-101]
    Root --> Inception[InceptionV3]
    Root --> Mobile[Mobile: MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3]
    Root --> Efficient[EfficientNet B0 to B7]
    Root --> Vision[Vision Transformers: ViT, Swin]
    style Root fill:#ffe4b5
    style Classic fill:#e6e6fa
    style Residual fill:#e6e6fa
    style Inception fill:#e6e6fa
    style Mobile fill:#e6e6fa
    style Efficient fill:#e6e6fa
    style Vision fill:#e6e6fa
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing architecture diagrams in your answer scripts, clearly label which layers are frozen vs trainable, and annotate the learning rate used. Examiners reward clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Transfer Learning. List any two popular pre-trained models used in computer vision.*

**Model Answer:**
Transfer Learning is a deep learning technique where a model trained on a large dataset (source task) is repurposed as the starting point for a related but different task (target task), leveraging previously learned features to reduce training time and data requirements.

Two popular pre-trained models:
1. **VGG-16 / VGG-19** – Oxford's Visual Geometry Group, 16/19 weight layers, trained on ImageNet.
2. **ResNet-50** – Microsoft's Residual Network with 50 layers, uses skip connections to handle vanishing gradients.

*(Acceptable alternatives: InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, Xception.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Feature Extraction and Fine-Tuning in transfer learning.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Convolutional base** | Frozen (not updated) | Trainable (updated) |
| **Classifier head** | New, trainable | New, trainable |
| **Learning rate** | Standard (e.g., $10^{-3}$) | Small (e.g., $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$) |
| **Compute cost** | Low | High |
| **Best for** | Very small datasets | Larger datasets, domain shift |

---

### 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Rule:** In Part B, the student must answer **one** of the two alternative questions. Each alternative typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*Explain the complete transfer learning pipeline for an image classification task. Include the steps: data preparation, base model selection, freezing, custom head addition, compilation, and training.*

**Model Solution:**

The complete pipeline consists of the following steps:

**Step 1 – Data Preparation:** Resize all images to the input size expected by the pre-trained model (e.g., $224 \times 224$ for VGG/ResNet). Apply data augmentation (rotation, flip, zoom) to prevent overfitting on the small target dataset.

**Step 2 – Base Model Selection:** Load a pre-trained model from `keras.applications`, e.g., `ResNet50` with `weights='imagenet'` and `include_top=False` (excludes the original 1000-class head).

**Step 3 – Freezing the Base:** Set `base_model.trainable = False` to lock the convolutional weights and prevent them from being updated in the first training phase.

**Step 4 – Custom Classifier Head:** Add a `GlobalAveragePooling2D` layer, a `Dropout(0.5)` layer for regularization, and a final `Dense(K_T, activation='softmax')` layer where $K_T$ is the number of target classes.

**Step 5 – Compilation:** Compile with `optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=1e-3)`, `loss='categorical_crossentropy'`, and metrics `=['accuracy']`.

**Step 6 – Initial Training:** Train only the new head for 10–20 epochs. The loss should drop sharply.

**Step 7 – Optional Fine-Tuning:** Unfreeze the top ~20% of the base model layers, recompile with a much lower LR (e.g., `1e-5`), and train for a few more epochs.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]**
*Discuss the concept of Discriminative Learning Rates with a suitable mathematical expression. Why is it preferred over a uniform learning rate in fine-tuning?*

**Model Solution:**

In fine-tuning, all layers should not be updated with the same learning rate. Early convolutional layers learn *general* features (edges, textures) that are already optimal and should be preserved. Later layers learn *task-specific* features that require greater adaptation.

The mathematical expression for Discriminative Learning Rate is:

$$\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{(L - l)}$$

Where:
- $\eta_l$ = learning rate at layer $l$
- $\eta_0$ = base learning rate (e.g., $10^{-3}$)
- $\alpha$ = decay factor (e.g., $0.9$ to $0.99$, less than 1)
- $L$ = total number of trainable layers

**Example Calculation:** For $L = 5$ layers, $\eta_0 = 10^{-3}$, $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_1 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{-5} \\
\eta_2 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_3 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{2} = 2.50 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_4 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{1} = 5.00 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_5 &= 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5)^{0} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why Preferred:** It prevents *Catastrophic Forgetting* of the well-learned low-level features while still allowing the high-level, task-specific layers to adapt aggressively. This gives higher final accuracy and faster convergence compared to a single uniform learning rate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*What is a pre-trained model? Explain with examples how pre-trained models like ResNet and VGG are used for medical image classification where labeled data is scarce.*

**Model Solution:**

A **pre-trained model** is a neural network whose weights have been previously optimized on a large benchmark dataset (most commonly ImageNet, which contains over 14 million images across 1000 categories). These models capture rich hierarchical feature representations that can be reused.

**Application in Medical Image Classification:**

Suppose we want to classify chest X-rays into "Normal" and "Pneumonia" with only 500 labeled X-ray images — far too few to train a deep CNN from scratch.

**Step 1:** Load `ResNet50` pre-trained on ImageNet. The early layers have learned to detect edges, curves, and textures — features equally useful for X-ray images (which contain rib outlines, opacities, and density gradients).

**Step 2:** Freeze the convolutional base to retain the general visual knowledge.

**Step 3:** Replace the 1000-class ImageNet head with a 2-class softmax head (Normal / Pneumonia).

**Step 4:** Train the new head on the 500 X-rays. Within a few epochs, the model achieves high accuracy because it is leveraging powerful, transferable low-level features.

**Step 5:** Fine-tune the last few ResNet blocks with a low LR (e.g., $10^{-5}$) to specialize features for medical imaging.

This approach reduces the data requirement by an order of magnitude and training time by a factor of 10–50x.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]**
*Explain the problem of Catastrophic Forgetting in fine-tuning. How can it be prevented?*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Catastrophic Forgetting is a phenomenon in which a neural network, during fine-tuning on a new task, abruptly and severely loses the knowledge it had previously acquired during pre-training. This happens because the standard gradient descent update:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla L_T(\theta^{(t)})$$

is applied with a learning rate $\eta$ that is *too large*, causing the weights $\theta$ to drift far from the pre-trained optimum $\theta^*$.

**Symptoms in Practice:**
- Pre-training accuracy was 90%, but after 2 fine-tuning epochs, accuracy on the source task crashes to 20%.
- The fine-tuned model becomes unstable and validation loss diverges.

**Prevention Strategies:**

1. **Low Learning Rate:** Use $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$ during fine-tuning, at least 10x smaller than the pre-training rate.

2. **Freeze Early Layers:** Do not update low-level feature extractors that capture universal patterns.

3. **Discriminative Learning Rates:** Use higher LR only for the last few layers.

4. **Gradual Unfreezing:** Unfreeze layers one block at a time, not all at once.

5. **Regularization (EWC):** Elastic Weight Consolidation adds a penalty term to preserve important weights:

$$L_{total} = L_T + \lambda \sum_i F_i (\theta_i - \theta_i^*)^2$$

where $F_i$ is the Fisher information of weight $i$ in the source task.

6. **Early Stopping:** Monitor validation loss and stop training before overfitting destabilizes the model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing "Transfer Learning means using transfer learning libraries" — This is a circular definition. Examiners award 0 marks. You must define **source task, target task, and weight reuse**.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to mention `include_top=False` when loading a pre-trained model. This is a 1-mark deduction trap.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Using a high learning rate (e.g., $10^{-3}$) for fine-tuning. Examiners specifically look for the use of a *low* learning rate ($10^{-4}$ or $10^{-5}$). Wrong LR = 2 marks cut.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Confusing *Feature Extraction* with *Fine-Tuning* in the comparison table. Memorize the difference: in feature extraction, the base is frozen; in fine-tuning, it is trainable.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Not drawing a labeled architecture diagram in long answers. Always include a block diagram — it usually earns 1–2 easy marks.
> - **Pitfall 6:** Writing only the *name* of pre-trained models without their *year/depth/significance*. Mention at least 2–3 with details.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullet Points**
> - **Transfer Learning (TL)** = reusing a pre-trained model on a new task to save data, time, and compute.
> - **Pre-trained Models** = trained on huge datasets like ImageNet (14M images, 1000 classes).
> - **Source Task $T_S$ & Target Task $T_T$** = the original and the new task respectively.
> - **Feature Extraction** = freeze base, train only new head. Best for small datasets.
> - **Fine-Tuning** = unfreeze and update some/all layers with a **low learning rate** ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$).
> - **Discriminative Learning Rate** = $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{(L-l)}$ — early layers get smaller updates.
> - **Catastrophic Forgetting** = loss of pre-trained knowledge due to large LR updates.
> - **Standard pre-trained backbones:** VGG-16, ResNet-50, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0.
> - **Input image size for most ImageNet models:** $224 \times 224 \times 3$.
> - **Preprocessing requirement:** Images must be normalized (e.g., `preprocess_input`) to match pre-training statistics.
> - **Augmentation is critical** when the target dataset is small to prevent overfitting.
> - **Top-5 accuracy** is the standard reporting metric for ImageNet benchmarks.
> - **Dropout (typically 0.5)** is used in the new classifier head for regularization.
> - **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** is preferred over Flatten for the transition from conv base to dense layer.
> - **TL is a form of inductive bias** — it injects prior knowledge into the model.
> - **NLP analog of TL** = BERT, GPT, Word2Vec pre-trained and fine-tuned.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line KTU Exam Mantra:**
> *"Pre-trained + Freeze for small data. Pre-trained + Fine-tune with LOW LR for better accuracy. Always remember Catastrophic Forgetting."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fine-Tuning Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT832 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fine-Tuning Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Fine-Tuning Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard Terminology):**
> **Fine-Tuning** is a transfer learning paradigm in which a pre-trained neural network (typically trained on a large, generic dataset such as ImageNet, BERT-corpus, or GPT-token corpora) is further trained (adapted) on a smaller, task-specific dataset by selectively updating its parameters using back-propagation. The objective is to leverage the low-level feature representations already learned and re-purpose them for a downstream task with limited data.

---

## 🔍 Intuitive Overview — The "Music Student" Analogy

Imagine a highly trained classical violinist who has spent **15 years** mastering scales, bowing techniques, and ear training. Now, suppose this violinist is asked to play a **Bollywood fusion track** for an upcoming concert. Does the violinist need to relearn music from scratch? **Absolutely not.** They already understand rhythm, melody, and instrument handling — they only need to **adapt** their existing skill set to the new genre.

| Concept in Analogy | Deep Learning Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 15 years of classical training | **Pre-training** on massive data (e.g., ImageNet, C4) |
| Knowledge of scales, bowing | Low/mid-level **feature extractors** (edges, textures, syntax) |
| Learning Bollywood fusion | **Fine-tuning** on a small target dataset |
| Retaining classical foundation | **Frozen base layers** (partial fine-tuning) |
| Adjusting bowing style for fusion | **Updated top layers** (task-specific head) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** Pre-training gives the model a *head start*; fine-tuning gives it *specialization*. The art of fine-tuning lies in deciding **how much** to change, **which layers** to change, and **how fast** to change them.

---

## 🎯 Why Fine-Tuning? (The Motivation)

1. **Data Scarcity:** Most real-world KTU projects and industry deployments do not have millions of labeled samples. Fine-tuning allows us to achieve state-of-the-art performance with as few as **100–10,000 labeled examples**.
2. **Compute Economy:** Pre-training a large model (e.g., a 175B parameter LLM) can cost **millions of dollars** in GPU hours. Fine-tuning is orders of magnitude cheaper.
3. **Generalization:** Pre-trained models capture **universal priors** (translation invariance in vision, syntactic structure in NLP) that boost downstream accuracy.
4. **Faster Convergence:** Starting from a pre-trained initialization converges **5–10× faster** than training from random initialization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Why fine-tuning instead of training from scratch?"*, always anchor your answer around three pillars: **Data, Compute, and Convergence.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Fine-Tuning Strategies

## 1. The Mathematical Foundation of Fine-Tuning

Let us formalize the problem. We are given:

- A pre-trained model with parameters $\theta_{\text{pre}} = \{\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_L\}$ spread across $L$ layers.
- A target dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\text{target}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $N \ll N_{\text{pre}}$.

The fine-tuning objective is to find:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\theta) = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\big(f_\theta(x_i), \, y_i\big)$$

where $f_\theta$ is the neural network and $\ell$ is the task loss (e.g., cross-entropy).

The optimization is initialized at $\theta^{(0)} = \theta_{\text{pre}}$ rather than at random initialization.

---

## 2. The Spectrum of Fine-Tuning Strategies

Fine-tuning is **not a single technique** — it is a *spectrum* of design choices. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following strategies:

### Strategy A — Feature Extraction (Linear Probing)
> **Freeze the entire backbone; train only the classification head.**

$$\theta_{\text{updated}} = \theta_{\text{pre}}, \quad W_{\text{head}} \leftarrow W_{\text{head}} - \eta \, \nabla_{W_{\text{head}}} \mathcal{L}$$

- **When to use:** Very small datasets, very dissimilar domains.
- **Pros:** Fast, low memory, prevents overfitting.
- **Cons:** Limited expressivity; cannot adapt low-level features.

### Strategy B — Full Fine-Tuning
> **Unfreeze all layers and update every parameter.**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \, \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\theta^{(t)})$$

- **When to use:** Large target dataset, similar domain to pre-training.
- **Pros:** Maximum flexibility, highest accuracy ceiling.
- **Cons:** Expensive in memory and compute; risk of **catastrophic forgetting**.

### Strategy C — Discriminative Learning Rates (DLR)
> **Use different learning rates for different layers — small for early layers, large for later layers.**

$$\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{\ell}, \quad \gamma \in (0, 1)$$

where $\ell$ indexes layers from output ($\ell = 0$) backward.

- **Intuition:** Early layers learn universal features (edges, n-grams) and should change slowly. Later layers learn task-specific features and should adapt quickly.
- **Typical values:** $\gamma = 0.95$ to $0.99$, so that $\eta_L$ (deepest) is **100–1000× smaller** than $\eta_0$.

### Strategy D — Gradual Unfreezing
> **Progressively unfreeze layers from top to bottom during training.**

- Epoch 1: Train only the head.
- Epoch 2: Unfreeze top transformer block, train head + block $L$.
- Epoch 3: Unfreeze block $L-1$, continue.
- … continue until all layers are unfrozen.

This is the **ULMFiT strategy** (Howard & Ruder, 2018) and is highly effective for NLP.

### Strategy E — Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
> **Update only a tiny fraction of parameters using auxiliary modules.**

#### E.1 — LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
For a pre-trained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, instead of updating $W_0$, we learn a low-rank decomposition:

$$W = W_0 + \Delta W = W_0 + B \cdot A, \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, \; A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}, \; r \ll \min(d, k)$$

Only $A$ and $B$ are trained. The number of trainable parameters drops from $d \times k$ to $r \times (d + k)$ — often a **1000× reduction**.

#### E.2 — Adapter Layers
Insert small bottleneck MLP blocks **inside** each transformer layer:

$$\text{Adapter}(h) = h + W_{\text{up}} \cdot \sigma\big(W_{\text{down}} \cdot h\big)$$

with $W_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$, $W_{\text{up}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$, $m \ll d$.

#### E.3 — Prompt Tuning
Prepend **learnable continuous vectors** (soft prompts) $P \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times d}$ to the input embeddings. The backbone $W_0$ remains fully frozen; only $P$ is updated.

### Strategy F — Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation
When full fine-tuning overwrites useful pre-trained knowledge, we add a **regularization term**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i} \big(\theta_i - \theta_i^{\text{pre}}\big)^2$$

This is the **L2-SP (Inverted Dropout)** regularizer that anchors parameters near their pre-trained values.

---

## 3. 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Strategy** | **Trainable Params** | **Memory** | **Best For (KTU Context)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feature Extraction | $W_{\text{head}}$ only | Lowest | Tiny datasets, baseline |
| Full Fine-Tuning | All $W, b$ | Highest | Large target data |
| Discriminative LR | All $W, b$ | High | Transfer across similar domains |
| Gradual Unfreezing | Progressively all | High | NLP, sequential domain shift |
| LoRA | $A, B$ matrices (rank $r$) | Very Low | LLM adaptation, edge deployment |
| Adapters | Bottleneck weights $W_{\text{up}}, W_{\text{down}}$ | Low | Multi-task learning |
| Prompt Tuning | Soft prompt $P$ | Lowest | Very large frozen LLMs |

| **Key Formula** | **Expression** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Fine-tuning objective | $\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\theta)$ | Adaptation goal |
| DLR schedule | $\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{\ell}$ | Layer-wise learning rate decay |
| LoRA decomposition | $W = W_0 + BA, \quad r \ll \min(d,k)$ | Low-rank weight update |
| Adapter block | $h' = h + W_{\text{up}}\sigma(W_{\text{down}}h)$ | Bottleneck residual adapter |
| L2-SP regularizer | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \lambda \sum_i (\theta_i - \theta_i^{\text{pre}})^2$ | Anti-forgetting penalty |
| Effective params (LoRA) | $\#_{\text{train}} = r(d+k)$ | Replaces $dk$ trainable params |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In the table above, **never write** absolute value using the pipe symbol `\|x\|` inside a markdown table — use `\vert x \vert` or `\Vert x \Vert` to prevent table-breaking.

---

## 4. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (KTU-style project):** Pre-train on ImageNet → fine-tune on 500 chest X-rays with frozen lower layers.
- **LLM Chatbots (Industry):** Use **LoRA** to adapt a 7B-parameter LLaMA model to a company's customer support data on a single A100 GPU.
- **Edge Deployment:** A 1GB full model can be adapted with a **4MB LoRA adapter** that is hot-swappable across tasks.
- **Robotics:** Sim-to-real transfer — pre-train in simulation, fine-tune with a few real-world demonstrations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Discriminative Learning Rate Justification

We motivate DLR by analyzing the **magnitude of feature change** across layers.

**Step 1 — Define the feature drift at layer $\ell$:**

Let $h_\ell(x)$ be the activation of layer $\ell$ for input $x$. The drift induced by a parameter update $\Delta\theta$ is bounded (via Lipschitz continuity) as:

$$\vert \Delta h_\ell \vert \leq L_\ell \cdot \vert \Delta \theta_\ell \vert$$

where $L_\ell$ is the Lipschitz constant of the layer's forward map.

**Step 2 — Empirically established pattern (Yosinski et al., 2014):**

Lower layers converge to general-purpose filters within the first few epochs of pre-training. Their filters transfer across tasks with high cosine similarity $\cos(\theta_i^{\text{pre}}, \theta_i^{\text{target}}) \approx 0.9$. Upper layers are highly task-specific with low transfer cosine similarity.

**Step 3 — Implication for fine-tuning step size:**

We want $\vert \Delta h_\ell \vert$ to be approximately **constant across layers** to avoid distorting transferable features:

$$\vert \eta_\ell \cdot \nabla_{\theta_\ell} \mathcal{L} \vert \cdot L_\ell \approx \text{const}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\eta_\ell$:**

Since lower layers have larger $L_\ell$ (deeper accumulated sensitivity), we need smaller $\eta_\ell$:

$$\eta_\ell = \frac{C}{L_\ell \cdot \vert \nabla_{\theta_\ell} \mathcal{L} \vert}$$

**Step 5 — Discretize into a simple exponential schedule (ULMFiT-style):**

Define $\ell$ counting from the output layer (so $\ell = 0$ is the head, $\ell = L$ is the earliest layer):

$$\boxed{\eta_\ell = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{\ell}, \quad \gamma \in [0.9, 0.99]}$$

**Verification:** At $\ell = L$ (the input-side layer), $\eta_L = \eta_0 \gamma^L$. For $L = 12$ and $\gamma = 0.95$, we get $\eta_L \approx 0.54 \, \eta_0$, meaning the deepest layer is updated at roughly half the rate of the head — a moderate, controlled change. ✅

---

## Derivation 2: LoRA Parameter Count and Memory Savings

**Step 1 — Original trainable parameters in $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$:**

$$N_{\text{original}} = d \times k$$

**Step 2 — LoRA trainable parameters (matrices $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$):**

$$N_{\text{LoRA}} = r \times k + d \times r = r(d + k)$$

**Step 3 — Compression ratio:**

$$\rho = \frac{N_{\text{LoRA}}}{N_{\text{original}}} = \frac{r(d+k)}{dk} = r\left(\frac{1}{d} + \frac{1}{k}\right)$$

For $d = k = 4096$ (typical LLM hidden size) and $r = 8$:

$$\rho = 8 \left(\frac{1}{4096} + \frac{1}{4096}\right) = 8 \times \frac{2}{4096} = \frac{16}{4096} = \frac{1}{256}$$

This is a **256× reduction** in trainable parameters.

**Step 4 — Initialization trick (asymmetry):**

- $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma = 1/\sqrt{r}$ (Gaussian)
- $B = 0$ (zero matrix)

This ensures $\Delta W = B \cdot A = 0$ at initialization, so the model **behaves identically to the pre-trained model** at step 0.

**Step 5 — Merging for inference:**

After training, the LoRA matrices can be **merged** into $W_0$ for zero-overhead inference:

$$W_{\text{merged}} = W_0 + B \cdot A$$

No additional forward-pass latency. ✅

---

## Derivation 3: Gradual Unfreezing Schedule

Let $U(t) \subseteq \{1, 2, \dots, L\}$ denote the set of unfrozen layers at training step $t$.

**Step 1 — Define unfreezing schedule:**

$$U(t) = \{L - \lfloor t / T_u \rfloor, \, L - \lfloor t / T_u \rfloor + 1, \, \dots, \, L\}$$

where $T_u$ is the unfreeze interval (e.g., $T_u = \text{epoch}/2$).

**Step 2 — Effective parameter count grows over time:**

$$N_{\text{eff}}(t) = \sum_{\ell \in U(t)} \vert \theta_\ell \vert$$

At $t = 0$: only the head is trained. At $t = (L-1) T_u$: all layers are trained.

**Step 3 — Total training cost (FLOPs):**

$$C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} N_{\text{eff}}(t) \cdot \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{target}} \vert}{\text{batch size}}$$

This is approximately **half the cost of full fine-tuning** because early steps train fewer parameters.

---

## Derivation 4: Catastrophic Forgetting Bound (L2-SP)

**Step 1 — Total objective:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{P} \left(\theta_i - \theta_i^{\text{pre}}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Gradient:**

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + 2\lambda \left(\theta - \theta^{\text{pre}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Update rule:**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \left[\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta^{(t)}) + 2\lambda \left(\theta^{(t)} - \theta^{\text{pre}}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium analysis (when $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} = 0$):**

At equilibrium, $\theta^* - \theta^{\text{pre}} = 0$, meaning the model reverts to pre-trained weights — the regularization **anchors** the parameters.

**Step 5 — Choosing $\lambda$ (KTU standard):**

$$\lambda = \frac{\alpha}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{P} \left(\theta_i^{\text{pre}}\right)^2, \quad \alpha \in [0.01, 0.1]$$

This makes $\lambda$ **scale-invariant** with respect to weight magnitude. ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Fine-Tuning Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pre-trained Model Available] --> B{Dataset Size N}
    B -->|N < 1000| C{Similar Domain?}
    B -->|N > 10000| D[Full Fine-Tuning]
    B -->|1000 < N < 10000| E{Compute Budget?}

    C -->|Yes| F[Feature Extraction + Linear Head]
    C -->|No| G[LoRA / Adapter Layers]

    E -->|Limited GPU| H[LoRA rank 4 to 16]
    E -->|Ample GPU| I[Discriminative LR + Gradual Unfreezing]

    F --> J[Deploy]
    G --> J
    D --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style B fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style C fill:#F5A623,color:#fff

    style G fill:#7ED321,color:#fff

```

---

## Diagram 2: LoRA Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
